title: Microsoft Fabric Modules
description: A complete 20-module Microsoft Fabric crash course covering platform foundations, OneLake, Lakehouse, orchestration, Spark, SQL, realtime intelligence, BI, security, administration, and capstone delivery.

chapters.title: Platform Foundations & Architecture
chapters.description: The Unified Analytics Platform Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Understand what Microsoft Fabric is, how its workloads share a single SaaS foundation, and why the unified architecture matters for every role in the data team.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 1 This is where everything begins. Microsoft Fabric is not a collection of tools stitched together. It is a single platform with a single storage layer, a single security model, and a single billing system that serves every role on the data team — from the engineer writing PySpark to the analyst building dashboards to the data scientist training models. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. Before Fabric, building an enterprise data platform meant assembling a dozen Azure services: Azure Data Factory for orchestration, Azure Data Lake Storage for raw files, Azure Synapse for SQL analytics, Azure Databricks for Spark, Power BI for reporting, and a web of connections, credentials, and configuration holding it all together. Each service had its own storage, its own security model, its own billing meter, and its own learning curve. Fabric replaces that assembly with a unified SaaS experience. You open one portal. Your data lives in one lake. Your compute is drawn from one capacity pool. Your security is enforced at one layer. And every workload — pipelines, Spark, SQL, streaming, BI, data science — operates on that shared foundation. This module gives you the architectural mental model that makes everything else in this course make sense. When you understand how Fabric is constructed, every subsequent module becomes a natural extension of what you already know.

sections.title: What Is Microsoft Fabric? Microsoft Fabric is an end-to-end analytics platform delivered as Software-as-a-Service (SaaS). It was announced at Microsoft Build in May 2023 and reached general availability in November 2023. Since then it has become one of the fastest-growing data platforms in the enterprise market. The simplest way to understand Fabric: it is one platform that does everything your data team needs — data movement, data engineering, data warehousing, real-time analytics, data science, and business intelligence — without requiring you to provision, connect, or manage separate services. The SaaS Model and What It Changes Traditional analytics platforms are Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS). You provision a cluster, configure networking, manage scaling, apply patches, and monitor infrastructure. You are responsible for keeping the lights on. Fabric is SaaS. Microsoft manages the infrastructure entirely. There are no clusters to provision, no virtual networks to configure (unless you choose to), no patches to apply. You open a browser, sign in, and start working. This is not a cosmetic difference. It changes the economics and the operational model fundamentally: • No idle costs for paused clusters — Fabric’s Spark pools start on demand from warm pools and cost nothing when idle. • No infrastructure management — Microsoft handles scaling, patching, availability, and disaster recovery for the platform. • Unified billing — A single capacity unit (CU) pool serves all workloads. You do not get separate bills for storage, compute, and networking from seven different services. • Instant provisioning — Creating a Lakehouse, a Warehouse, or a Notebook takes seconds, not minutes or hours. • Automatic updates — New features appear in the platform without migration projects. You are always on the latest version. Positioning: Fabric vs. Databricks vs. Synapse vs. Snowflake The enterprise analytics market has several major platforms. Understanding where Fabric sits helps you evaluate it honestly: Platform Model Strengths Key Difference from Fabric Microsoft Fabric SaaS (unified) All-in-one: ETL, Spark, SQL, BI, streaming, ML in one portal with one lake Fabric IS Fabric Databricks PaaS (multi-cloud) Best-in-class Spark, MLflow, Unity Catalog, Delta Lake originator Separate platform; no native BI layer; you bring your own storage and orchestration Azure Synapse Analytics PaaS (Azure) SQL pools, Spark pools, pipelines in one Azure resource Fabric’s predecessor; being deprecated in favor of Fabric; no SaaS model Snowflake SaaS (multi-cloud) Elastic SQL warehouse, data sharing, strong governance SQL-centric; limited Spark/ML; no native BI; separate from orchestration

blocks.content: Key insight: Fabric’s competitive advantage is not that any single workload is best-in-class. It is that all workloads share one storage layer, one security model, and one governance framework. The integration is the product.

sections.title: The Seven Workloads at a Glance Fabric organizes its capabilities into workloads. Each workload is a set of tools optimized for a specific role or task, but they all operate on the same underlying platform. Think of workloads as different lenses on the same data.

blocks.content: 1. Data Factory Data Factory is Fabric’s orchestration and data movement engine. It contains two sub-tools: Pipelines (code-free orchestration with activities like Copy, Notebook, ForEach, and If Condition) and Dataflows Gen2 (Power Query-based low-code transformations with 300+ connectors). If data needs to move from point A to point B, or a sequence of jobs needs to run on a schedule, Data Factory is where you build it. 2. Data Engineering Data Engineering is the Spark-powered workload for building ETL/ELT pipelines at scale. Its primary items are the Lakehouse (the storage artifact that holds files and Delta tables) and Notebooks (interactive, multi-language development environments for PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, and R). If you are transforming, cleaning, enriching, or reshaping data, this is your workload. 3. Data Science Data Science shares the same Notebooks and Spark engine as Data Engineering, but adds Experiments (MLflow-tracked training runs), Models (a registry for versioned ML artifacts), and PREDICT() integration for batch scoring. If you are training models, tracking experiments, or deploying predictions, this workload provides the scaffolding. 4. Data Warehouse The Data Warehouse is a fully managed, T-SQL-native analytical store. It supports full DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), stored procedures, views, and rich SQL analytics. Under the hood, it stores data in Delta format in OneLake, but exposes a familiar SQL Server interface. If your team thinks in T-SQL and needs a governed, high-performance analytical database, this is the workload. 5. Real-Time Intelligence Real-Time Intelligence handles streaming and time-series workloads. Its key items are the Eventhouse (a KQL database optimized for high-velocity, append-heavy data), Eventstreams (no-code streaming ingestion and routing), Real-Time Dashboards (auto-refreshing visualizations), and Activator (event-driven alerting that fires actions when data conditions are met). If data arrives continuously and you need sub-second query responses, this is the workload. 6. Power BI Power BI is Fabric’s business intelligence layer, and it is the most widely adopted component — used by over 550,000 organizations worldwide. In Fabric, Power BI gains DirectLake mode, which lets semantic models query Delta tables in OneLake directly without importing data or using DirectQuery. This eliminates the traditional trade-off between performance and freshness. Power BI covers semantic models, DAX, reports, dashboards, paginated reports, and governed distribution through apps. 7. SQL Database SQL Database is Fabric’s transactional (OLTP) engine — a fully managed SQL Server-based database for operational workloads. Unlike the Data Warehouse (which is analytical), SQL Database supports normalized schemas, indexes, triggers, and full transactional isolation. Its data automatically replicates to OneLake as Delta tables, enabling analytics without ETL. This is the newest Fabric workload, reaching general availability in late 2025.

The critical takeaway: these seven workloads are not seven products. They are seven experiences built on one platform. They share OneLake for storage, Microsoft Entra ID for identity, and Fabric capacities for compute. Data created in one workload is instantly accessible in every other workload without copying, moving, or configuring connections.

sections.title: OneLake — The Single Data Lake OneLake is Fabric’s storage layer, and it is the foundation on which everything else is built. Every Fabric workload reads from and writes to OneLake. There is no separate storage account to provision, no connection string to configure, no access key to manage. When you create a Lakehouse, the data goes to OneLake. When you create a Warehouse, the data goes to OneLake. When Power BI uses DirectLake mode, it reads from OneLake. Think of OneLake as OneDrive for data. Just as every Microsoft 365 user gets a OneDrive that stores their files in a single namespace, every Fabric tenant gets a OneLake that stores all analytical data in a single namespace. Why One Copy of Data Matters In legacy architectures, data is copied between services constantly. Raw files land in ADLS Gen2. A pipeline copies them into a Synapse SQL pool. Another process exports aggregates to a separate storage account for Power BI. A data science team extracts a sample to their own storage for training. Each copy introduces lag, inconsistency, cost, and a security boundary to manage. OneLake eliminates this. Because all workloads share the same storage layer, a Delta table written by a Spark notebook is instantly queryable by the Warehouse’s SQL engine, instantly visible to Power BI’s DirectLake mode, and instantly accessible from a KQL database — without copying a single byte. The Hierarchical Namespace OneLake organizes data in a clean hierarchy:

blocks.content:
Tenant (your organization)
    └── Workspace A
    │     └── Lakehouse: Sales
    │     │     └── Files/     (raw CSVs, JSONs, images)
    │     │     └── Tables/    (managed Delta tables)
    │     └── Warehouse: Analytics
    │     └── Notebook: ETL_Pipeline
    │
    └── Workspace B
          └── Lakehouse: Marketing
          └── Eventhouse: Clickstream

Under the hood, OneLake maps to Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2). The files are stored as Delta Parquet, the industry-standard open format. This means you can access OneLake data through standard ADLS Gen2 APIs, mount it locally with OneLake File Explorer, and read it with any tool that understands Parquet or Delta Lake. Delta Lake as the Default Format Every table in Fabric — whether created in a Lakehouse, a Warehouse, or a SQL Database — is stored in Delta Lake format. Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel to Parquet files. It consists of two things: Parquet data files and a JSON transaction log that tracks every change. This is not a configuration choice. It is a platform decision. Fabric is a Delta-native platform. When you INSERT a row into a Warehouse table, a Delta commit is written to OneLake. When you run df.write.saveAsTable() in a Notebook, a Delta commit is written to OneLake. The format is always Delta. OneLake Shortcuts Shortcuts are virtual pointers to data stored outside OneLake. You can create a shortcut to an ADLS Gen2 container, an Amazon S3 bucket, a Google Cloud Storage location, or a Dataverse environment. The data stays where it is — Fabric reads it in place without copying. This is how Fabric bridges the gap between a unified platform and the reality that enterprise data lives in many places. Shortcuts give you zero-copy access to external data while maintaining the OneLake namespace as the single point of discovery.

sections.title: Capacities, SKUs, and the Compute Model Fabric’s compute is organized around capacities. A capacity is a pool of compute resources, measured in Capacity Units (CUs), that is shared across all Fabric workloads within the workspaces assigned to that capacity. This is fundamentally different from the legacy model, where each service had its own compute. In the old world, your Synapse SQL pool had dedicated DTUs, your Databricks cluster had its own VMs, and your ADF integration runtime had separate compute. In Fabric, one capacity pool serves pipelines, Spark jobs, SQL queries, KQL queries, and Power BI refreshes. F-SKUs: The Capacity Tiers Fabric capacities come in tiers called F-SKUs. Each SKU specifies a CU count: SKU Capacity Units (CUs) Typical Use Case F2 2 Individual developers, proof-of-concept, learning F4 4 Small team development and testing F8 8 Small team production workloads F16 16 Medium team production workloads F32 32 Department-level production workloads F64 64 Large-scale production workloads F128 128 Enterprise-scale production workloads F256+ 256+ Large enterprise, heavy concurrent workloads

blocks.content:
Every Fabric workload consumes CUs from this shared pool. When a Spark job runs, it draws CUs. When a SQL query executes, it draws CUs. When a pipeline copies data, it draws CUs. The consumption is metered and tracked in the Fabric Capacity Metrics app.
Throttling and Burst
Fabric uses a smoothing and throttling model to manage CU consumption:
•  Smoothing: Short bursts of heavy usage are smoothed over time. A Spark job that uses 4x your CU capacity for 30 seconds is averaged across a longer window, so brief spikes do not trigger throttling.
•  Throttling: If sustained usage exceeds your capacity for an extended period, Fabric begins delaying background operations (like Power BI refreshes) and eventually rejects interactive operations. Understanding this is critical for capacity planning.
•  Autoscale Billing for Spark: Introduced in 2025, this is a serverless, pay-as-you-go option where Spark jobs run independently of your Fabric capacity and are billed only for execution time. This prevents Spark jobs from consuming CUs that other workloads need.
Storage Billing
Storage in OneLake is billed separately from compute. You pay for the amount of data stored, similar to ADLS Gen2 pricing. Shortcut data (data that lives outside OneLake) does not incur Fabric storage charges — you pay the storage provider directly.

sections.title: Workspaces, Items, and the Fabric Object Model Everything in Fabric lives inside a workspace. A workspace is a container — think of it as a project folder that groups related items together and applies shared security and capacity. Workspaces A workspace is: • A collaboration boundary — team members are granted roles (Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer) at the workspace level. • A capacity binding — each workspace is assigned to a Fabric capacity, which determines the compute resources available to everything in that workspace. • A governance scope — sensitivity labels, Git integration, and deployment pipelines operate at the workspace level. • An organizational unit — workspaces can be organized into domains (e.g., Finance, Marketing, Operations) for data mesh-style governance. A common pattern is to create separate workspaces for development, testing, and production, and use Fabric’s Deployment Pipelines to promote items between them. Items Inside a workspace, you create items. An item is a first-class Fabric object — it has a type, a name, metadata, and a lifecycle. The item types you will encounter most frequently: Item Type Workload Purpose Lakehouse Data Engineering Dual-interface store: Files/ for raw data, Tables/ for managed Delta tables, plus auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint Warehouse Data Warehouse T-SQL-native analytical database with full DML support Notebook Data Engineering / Data Science Interactive multi-language code environment (PySpark, SQL, Scala, R) Pipeline Data Factory Orchestration workflow with activities, triggers, and parameters Dataflow Gen2 Data Factory Low-code Power Query-based transformation with data destinations Semantic Model Power BI In-memory analytical model with DAX measures and relationships Report Power BI Interactive data visualization and storytelling Eventhouse Real-Time Intelligence KQL database cluster for streaming and time-series data Eventstream Real-Time Intelligence No-code streaming ingestion and routing SQL Database SQL Database Transactional OLTP database with automatic OneLake replication Environment Data Engineering Library and Spark configuration management for Notebooks ML Model Data Science Registered, versioned machine learning model artifact ML Experiment Data Science MLflow experiment tracking for training runs

blocks.content: Naming Conventions A disciplined naming convention prevents chaos as your Fabric estate grows. A recommended pattern:
The exact convention matters less than consistency. Pick a pattern, document it, and enforce it.

sections.title: Personas and Roles in Fabric Fabric is designed for the entire data team, not just engineers. Different personas use different workloads, but they all work on the same platform and the same data.

blocks.content: Persona Primary Workloads Key Activities Data Engineer Data Engineering, Data Factory Build ETL/ELT pipelines with Notebooks and PySpark. Create and maintain Lakehouses. Orchestrate workflows with Pipelines. Manage Delta table health (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM). Data Analyst / SQL Analyst Data Warehouse, Power BI Write T-SQL queries against the Warehouse or Lakehouse SQL endpoint. Build semantic models and DAX measures. Create reports and dashboards. Data Scientist Data Science, Data Engineering Explore data in Notebooks. Engineer features with PySpark. Train and track models with MLflow. Deploy batch predictions with PREDICT(). BI Developer Power BI Design semantic models with proper star schemas. Write DAX measures. Build interactive reports. Manage governed distribution through apps. Platform / Fabric Admin Administration, all workloads Manage tenant settings and feature flags. Monitor capacity utilization and throttling. Configure security policies, domains, and deployment pipelines. Audit activity logs. Analytics Engineer Data Engineering, Data Warehouse Bridge engineering and analytics. Build the Gold layer of the medallion architecture. Create governed views and tables for downstream consumption.

Key insight: the power of Fabric is that these personas do not need separate environments. The data engineer who builds a Delta table in a Notebook and the analyst who queries that same table through the SQL analytics endpoint are working on the same data, in the same workspace, with zero data movement.

sections.title: Fabric vs. the Legacy Stack If you are coming from the Azure data ecosystem, you already know many of the concepts in Fabric — they have new names, new interfaces, and new integration points, but the underlying ideas are familiar. This mapping helps you translate your existing knowledge.

blocks.content: Legacy Azure Service Fabric Equivalent What Changed Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2) OneLake No separate storage account to provision. OneLake is automatic, tenant-wide, and Delta-native. ADLS Gen2 is still the underlying protocol. Azure Data Factory (ADF) Data Factory (Pipelines + Dataflows Gen2) Same concepts (activities, triggers, linked services), but Fabric-native. Runs inside the portal, writes to OneLake directly, and consumes CUs from Fabric capacity. Azure Synapse Analytics — SQL Pools Data Warehouse Fully managed, no dedicated pool provisioning. Decoupled compute and storage. Stores data in Delta format in OneLake. Azure Synapse Analytics — Spark Pools Spark in Data Engineering Serverless Spark with auto-provisioning. Starter pools and warm pools for fast session start. Environments for library management. Azure Databricks Notebooks + Spark + Lakehouse Fabric’s Spark is functionally similar but tightly integrated with OneLake, the SQL engine, and Power BI. Databricks remains a separate platform with its own Unity Catalog. Azure HDInsight Spark in Data Engineering HDInsight was the original managed Hadoop/Spark service on Azure. Fabric’s Spark is its modern replacement. Power BI Service (standalone) Power BI (within Fabric) Same reports and semantic models, but now with DirectLake mode, OneLake integration, and unified security. Azure Stream Analytics Real-Time Intelligence (Eventstreams) Eventstreams replaces Stream Analytics for most Fabric scenarios, with no-code stream processing and direct routing to OneLake. Azure Data Explorer (ADX) Eventhouse (KQL Database) Same KQL query language, same columnar engine, now integrated into Fabric with OneLake storage. Azure SQL Database SQL Database in Fabric Transactional SQL database with automatic replication to OneLake for analytics.

Important context: Azure Synapse Analytics is being gradually deprecated in favor of Fabric. Microsoft has announced that Synapse workloads should migrate to Fabric for long-term support. If you are building new projects today, build them on Fabric.

sections.title: Licensing, Trials, and Cost Planning Understanding Fabric’s cost model before you start building prevents surprises. The model is simpler than the legacy Azure stack, but it requires capacity planning. Licensing Options License Type How It Works Best For Free Trial 60-day trial with an F64 capacity equivalent. No credit card required. Full functionality. Learning, evaluation, proof-of-concept. This is how most individuals start with Fabric. Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) Hourly billing for the capacity SKU you choose. Turn it on, turn it off. No commitment. Variable workloads, development environments, teams that want flexibility without commitment. Reserved Capacity 1-year or 3-year commitment for a specific SKU. Significant discount over PAYG (typically 20–40% savings). Production workloads with predictable, steady-state usage. Fabric Free (Power BI Free) Limited individual access. Can view shared content but cannot create workspaces or full Fabric items. Individual users who only consume reports shared by others. Power BI Pro / PPU Per-user licenses for Power BI features (sharing, distribution). Required for some collaboration scenarios even within Fabric. Report sharing and app distribution in organizations without Premium/Fabric capacity.

blocks.content:
Estimating Monthly Costs
A rough framework for estimating Fabric costs:
•  Compute: Determined by the F-SKU you choose. An F64 capacity is the entry point for most production workloads. Cost varies by region and commitment type.
•  Storage: Billed per GB stored in OneLake. Delta Lake format with OPTIMIZE and VACUUM keeps storage efficient. Shortcut data is billed at the source, not in OneLake.
•  Autoscale Spark: If enabled, Spark jobs are billed separately at a per-second rate based on vCores consumed. This is additive to your capacity cost but prevents Spark from starving other workloads.
•  Networking: Standard Azure egress charges apply for data leaving OneLake. Intra-region data movement within Fabric is free.
Cost Planning Best Practices
•  Start with the free trial for evaluation and learning.
•  Use PAYG for development environments that can be paused outside business hours.
•  Right-size your production capacity using the Fabric Capacity Metrics app.
•  Consider Autoscale Billing for Spark if Spark workloads are bursty and compete with SQL/BI workloads for CUs.
•  Use reserved capacity for stable production workloads to save 20–40%.
•  Monitor the Capacity Metrics app weekly during the first month to understand your actual consumption pattern.

sections.title: Module 1 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: What Microsoft Fabric is — a unified SaaS analytics platform with one storage layer (OneLake), one compute pool (Capacity Units), and one security model (Microsoft Entra ID)
The seven workloads — Data Factory, Data Engineering, Data Science, Data Warehouse, Real-Time Intelligence, Power BI, and SQL Database — and what each is designed for
OneLake — the single storage foundation, Delta Lake as the default format, shortcuts for zero-copy external access
Capacities and compute — F-SKUs, CU consumption, throttling and burst behavior, autoscale billing for Spark
Workspaces and items — the organizational model, item types, and naming conventions
The Fabric portal — navigation, workspace views, workload switcher, and settings
Personas — which roles use which workloads, and how they collaborate on shared data
Legacy mapping — how Fabric replaces Azure Data Factory, Synapse, HDInsight, ADLS Gen2, and other Azure data services
Licensing and cost planning — trial, PAYG, reserved capacity, and how to estimate monthly costs

blocks.content: Architecture Quiz Before moving to Module 2, test yourself:

items: Draw the Fabric architecture from memory: workloads at the top, OneLake in the middle, capacity and security at the bottom. Can you name all seven workloads?
A colleague asks: “What is the difference between a Lakehouse and a Warehouse in Fabric?” How do you answer?
Your manager asks: “Why would we move from Azure Synapse to Fabric?” What are the three strongest reasons?
An analyst wants to query a Delta table that a data engineer created in a Notebook. Does the analyst need Spark? What interface do they use?
You are planning capacity for a team of 10 data engineers and 20 analysts. What F-SKU would you start with, and how would you monitor whether it is sufficient?

blocks.content: You are not just learning a tool.
You are building the mental model of a platform that unifies data movement, engineering, analytics, real-time streaming, machine learning, and business intelligence into one shared foundation.

In Module 2, we go deep into OneLake — the storage layer that makes all of this possible. You will understand shortcuts, Delta Lake internals, mirroring, security at the storage layer, and how to navigate OneLake programmatically and visually.

chapters.title: OneLake — The Storage Foundation
chapters.description: One Lake. One Copy. One Truth. Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Master OneLake as the unified storage layer — understand shortcuts, Delta Lake format, ADLS Gen2 compatibility, and how every Fabric workload reads and writes through a single namespace.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 2 In Module 1, you learned that Microsoft Fabric is a unified platform with seven workloads sharing one storage layer, one compute pool, and one security model. This module is about that storage layer. OneLake is the single most important architectural decision in Fabric, and understanding it deeply will change how you think about every other topic in this course. Every Delta table you create in a Lakehouse, every row you INSERT into a Warehouse, every streaming event you land in an Eventhouse, every column a Power BI report reads — all of it lives in OneLake. There is no second storage system. There is no sidecar. There is no cache that secretly holds the real data somewhere else. OneLake is it. The consequence is profound: when one workload writes data, every other workload can read it instantly, without copying, without ETL, without configuring a connection. A Spark notebook writes a Delta table. The Warehouse’s SQL engine queries it. Power BI visualizes it. A KQL queryset scans it. All reading from the same bytes on the same storage layer. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. We will now take OneLake apart, layer by layer: the hierarchical namespace, the Delta Lake format that underpins every table, shortcuts that bring external data into the namespace without copying, mirroring that replicates external databases, the catalog that makes everything discoverable, the security model that governs access, and the APIs that let you interact with OneLake programmatically.

sections.title: OneLake Architecture Deep Dive OneLake is Fabric’s unified storage layer. It is provisioned automatically with every Fabric tenant — there is no setup, no storage account to create, no access key to generate. When your organization activates Fabric, OneLake exists. The best analogy is OneDrive for data. Every Microsoft 365 user gets a OneDrive that stores their documents in a single, managed namespace. Every Fabric tenant gets a OneLake that stores all analytical data in a single, managed namespace. The parallel is intentional — Microsoft designed OneLake to be as invisible as OneDrive. You do not think about where OneDrive stores your files. You should not have to think about where OneLake stores your tables. The Hierarchical Namespace OneLake organizes data in a four-level hierarchy:

blocks.content: Tenant └── Workspace └── Item (Lakehouse, Warehouse, Eventhouse, SQL Database, ...) └── Files and Tables

Tenant is the top level — your organization’s Fabric instance. Every Fabric tenant has exactly one OneLake. There is no multi-region OneLake and no per-department OneLake. One tenant, one lake. Workspace is the primary organizational container. Each workspace maps to a folder in OneLake. Workspaces carry security (who can access), capacity binding (which compute pool pays for operations), and governance scope (sensitivity labels, Git integration). Item is a Fabric object that owns storage. A Lakehouse is an item. A Warehouse is an item. An Eventhouse is an item. Each item gets its own folder within the workspace’s OneLake path. Not all items own storage — a Notebook, for example, does not have its own OneLake folder because it writes to a Lakehouse. Files and Tables are the data itself. Inside a Lakehouse, you see two sections: Files/ (for unstructured and semi-structured data like CSVs, JSONs, images) and Tables/ (for managed Delta tables). Inside a Warehouse, all data is in managed tables.
A concrete example:

Contoso (tenant)
    └── sales_prod (workspace)
    │     └── lh_bronze_sales (Lakehouse)
    │     │     └── Files/
    │     │     │     └── raw_orders/     (CSV landing zone)
    │     │     │     └── raw_customers/  (JSON exports)
    │     │     └── Tables/
    │     │           └── orders          (Delta table)
    │     │           └── customers       (Delta table)
    │     └── lh_gold_sales (Lakehouse)
    │     │     └── Tables/
    │     │           └── fact_orders     (Delta table)
    │     │           └── dim_customers   (Delta table)
    │     └── wh_sales (Warehouse)
    │           └── dbo.fact_orders      (Delta, via T-SQL)
    │           └── dbo.dim_customers    (Delta, via T-SQL)
    │
    └── marketing_prod (workspace)
          └── lh_campaigns (Lakehouse)
                └── Tables/
                      └── campaigns       (Delta table)

Under the Hood: ADLS Gen2
OneLake is built on Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2). The files in OneLake are stored in ADLS Gen2 containers with hierarchical namespace enabled. This is not an implementation detail you can ignore — it has practical consequences:
•  ADLS Gen2 APIs work. Any tool that can read ADLS Gen2 (Azure Storage Explorer, azcopy, the Azure Storage SDKs) can read OneLake. The endpoint is onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com.
•  Standard Parquet tooling works. Because the underlying files are Parquet (wrapped in Delta Lake’s transaction log), external tools like DuckDB, Polars, or pandas can read OneLake files directly if they authenticate correctly.
•  File paths are predictable. The OneLake path follows the pattern: https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/{workspace_name}/{item_name}/Tables/{table_name}/.
You do not normally need to think about ADLS Gen2. Fabric abstracts it away. But when you need to integrate with external tools, connect from outside Fabric, or debug storage-level issues, knowing this foundation is essential.

sections.title: Delta Lake as the Default Format Every table in Fabric is a Delta Lake table. This is not optional. It is not a configuration choice. It is a platform-level decision that applies to every Lakehouse table, every Warehouse table, every SQL Database table that replicates to OneLake, and every mirrored table. Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer, originally created by Databricks, that adds three critical capabilities to plain Parquet files: ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel. Understanding what Delta Lake is and how it works is essential because every table you touch in this course is a Delta table. What Makes a Delta Table A Delta table is not a single file. It is a directory containing two things: • Parquet data files — The actual data, stored in columnar Parquet format. Each file contains a subset of the rows. The number of files depends on how the data was written and how many partitions exist. • The _delta_log/ directory — A folder containing JSON files that form the transaction log. Each JSON file represents a commit — a record of what changed. The log is the source of truth for what data is currently in the table.

blocks.content:
Tables/orders/
    └── _delta_log/
    │     └── 00000000000000000000.json   (initial commit)
    │     └── 00000000000000000001.json   (second commit)
    │     └── 00000000000000000002.json   (third commit)
    │     └── 00000000000000000010.checkpoint.parquet
    └── part-00000-xxxxx.snappy.parquet
    └── part-00001-xxxxx.snappy.parquet
    └── part-00002-xxxxx.snappy.parquet

When you read a Delta table, the engine reads the transaction log first to determine which Parquet files are currently valid. When you write to a Delta table, the engine writes new Parquet files and then appends a new JSON entry to the log. This two-step process is what enables ACID transactions — the log commit is atomic.
ACID Transactions
ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. In practical terms:
•  Atomicity: A write either fully succeeds or fully fails. You will never see a table in a half-written state. If a Spark job crashes mid-write, the transaction log is not updated, so readers never see partial data.
•  Consistency: Schema enforcement ensures that every row conforms to the table’s schema. You cannot accidentally write a string into an integer column.
•  Isolation: Concurrent readers and writers do not interfere. A long-running read sees a consistent snapshot of the table, even if writes are happening simultaneously.
•  Durability: Once a commit is written to the transaction log, the data is durable in OneLake’s ADLS Gen2 storage.
Before Delta Lake, data lakes were notorious for the “data swamp” problem — files overwritten mid-read, schema drift, no rollback, no audit trail. Delta Lake solves all of these problems. Fabric adopts Delta as the universal format precisely because it turns a raw data lake into a reliable, governed data platform.
Time Travel
Because the transaction log records every commit, Delta Lake can reconstruct the table as it existed at any previous point. This is called time travel.

# Read the table as it was 3 versions ago
df = spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', 3).load(path)

# Read the table as it was at a specific timestamp
df = spark.read.format('delta').option('timestampAsOf', '2026-01-15').load(path)

# In SQL
SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 3
SELECT * FROM orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-01-15T00:00:00'

Time travel is invaluable for debugging (“what did this table look like before yesterday’s pipeline ran?”), auditing (“who changed what and when?”), and reproducibility (“train the model on last Tuesday’s snapshot”). Important: Time travel depends on the transaction log and the underlying Parquet files. If you run VACUUM to delete old files, you lose the ability to travel to versions that referenced those files. We will cover VACUUM in detail later in this module. Schema Enforcement and Evolution Delta Lake enforces schema on write. If you try to append a DataFrame with a column that does not exist in the table, or with a mismatched type, the write fails with a clear error. When you intentionally want to change the schema, you use schema evolution:

# Allow new columns to be added automatically
df.write.format('delta') \
    .option('mergeSchema', 'true') \
    .mode('append') \
    .saveAsTable('orders')
Schema enforcement prevents silent data corruption. Schema evolution provides controlled flexibility. Together, they give you the safety of a database with the flexibility of a data lake.

sections.title: OneLake Shortcuts Shortcuts are virtual pointers to data that lives outside your Lakehouse — or even outside OneLake entirely. They make external data appear as if it were a native OneLake folder or table, without copying a single byte. This is one of Fabric’s most powerful architectural features. Enterprise data does not live in one place. It is scattered across Azure storage accounts, AWS S3 buckets, Google Cloud Storage, Dataverse environments, and other Fabric workspaces. Shortcuts let you bring all of that data into a single, unified namespace without data movement. How Shortcuts Work A shortcut is a metadata pointer stored in OneLake. When a query reads from a shortcut, the Fabric engine resolves the pointer and reads directly from the source. The data never transits through an intermediate copy.

blocks.content:
lh_bronze_sales (Lakehouse)
    └── Tables/
    │     └── orders              (local Delta table)
    │     └── external_products   ───→ Shortcut → ADLS Gen2 account
    │     └── partner_inventory   ───→ Shortcut → Amazon S3 bucket
    └── Files/
          └── reference_data/     ───→ Shortcut → Another Lakehouse

From the perspective of a Notebook, a SQL query, or a Power BI report, a shortcut looks exactly like a local table or folder. The query engine handles the redirection transparently. You write the same SELECT statement regardless of whether the data is local or shortcutted. Supported Shortcut Sources Source Connection Type Notes Another Fabric Lakehouse Internal (OneLake) Cross-workspace, cross-tenant. No data movement. Ideal for sharing Gold tables across teams. Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 External (ADLS Gen2) Most common external shortcut. Requires authentication (service principal, managed identity, or shared key). Amazon S3 External (S3) Cross-cloud access. S3-compatible APIs. Requires IAM credentials configured in Fabric. Google Cloud Storage External (GCS) Cross-cloud access. Requires GCS credentials. Dataverse External (Dataverse) Direct access to Dynamics 365 / Power Platform data. No export pipeline needed. OneLake within same workspace Internal (OneLake) Useful for creating logical views across Lakehouses within the same project.

Creating Shortcuts
Shortcuts can be created through the Fabric portal UI or programmatically via the OneLake REST API.
In the portal:
•  Open your Lakehouse.
•  Right-click on the Tables/ or Files/ section.
•  Select “New shortcut.”
•  Choose the source type (OneLake, ADLS Gen2, S3, etc.).
•  Provide the connection details and target path.
•  Name the shortcut and confirm.
The shortcut appears instantly. No data is copied. Queries against the shortcut read from the source in real time.
Shortcut Best Practices
•  Use shortcuts for reference data. If your Gold Lakehouse has dimension tables that multiple teams need, other teams should create shortcuts to those tables rather than copying them.
•  Use shortcuts for cross-cloud access. If your organization stores some data in AWS S3, create S3 shortcuts rather than building copy pipelines. This eliminates data movement costs and latency.
•  Be aware of latency. A shortcut to an S3 bucket in us-east-1 from a Fabric capacity in West Europe will add network latency to every query. For high-frequency queries, consider whether the latency is acceptable.
•  Shortcuts respect source permissions. Access to shortcutted data requires both Fabric permissions (can the user see the Lakehouse?) and source permissions (does the connection credential have access to the external path?).
Warning: Shortcuts to external sources are read-only. You cannot write to an S3 bucket or ADLS Gen2 container through a shortcut. Writes must go through native Fabric mechanisms.

sections.title: OneLake File Explorer and APIs OneLake is not locked inside the Fabric portal. You can access it from your desktop, from scripts, and from external tools. OneLake File Explorer OneLake File Explorer is a Windows application that mounts OneLake as a drive on your local machine. Once installed and authenticated, you see your Fabric workspaces, Lakehouses, and files in Windows File Explorer — just like a mapped network drive. This enables workflows like: • Dragging CSV files from your desktop directly into a Lakehouse’s Files/ folder. • Opening Parquet files in local tools (Excel, DuckDB, Python scripts) for quick inspection. • Copying files between your local machine and OneLake without using the portal. Important: OneLake File Explorer is currently Windows-only. macOS and Linux users can access OneLake through the REST API or the Azure Storage SDKs instead. Programmatic Access via REST API OneLake exposes a standard ADLS Gen2-compatible REST API. Any HTTP client can interact with OneLake files.

blocks.content:
# Python: List files in a Lakehouse using the Azure Storage SDK
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.filedatalake import DataLakeServiceClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
service = DataLakeServiceClient(
    account_url='https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com',
    credential=credential
)

# Navigate the hierarchy
fs_client = service.get_file_system_client('sales_prod')      # workspace
paths = fs_client.get_paths('lh_bronze_sales.Lakehouse/Files') # item + folder
for path in paths:
    print(path.name)

The endpoint is always onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com. Authentication uses Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) tokens. The Azure Identity library’s DefaultAzureCredential handles this automatically in most environments. Access from Notebooks Inside a Fabric Notebook, you access OneLake through the integrated Spark session. The Lakehouse attached to your Notebook is automatically mounted, so you can reference files and tables without full paths:

# Read a Delta table from the attached Lakehouse
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/orders')

# Read a CSV from the Files section
df = spark.read.csv('Files/raw_orders/2026-01/', header=True)

# Write a new Delta table
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('Tables/clean_orders')

# Use abfss:// paths for cross-Lakehouse access
df = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'abfss://sales_prod@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/
     lh_gold_sales.Lakehouse/Tables/fact_orders'
)

The abfss:// protocol is the Azure Blob File System Secure protocol. It is the standard way to reference ADLS Gen2 paths in Spark. In Fabric, the pattern is: abfss://{workspace}@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/{item_name}.{item_type}/{path}.

sections.title: Data Formats — Delta, Parquet, CSV, JSON OneLake can store any file format. But not all formats are equal in Fabric, and understanding the hierarchy of formats will shape your data engineering decisions. Format Comparison Format Type Schema Transactions When Used in Fabric Delta Lake Columnar + log Enforced Full ACID Every managed table. Default and preferred format for all structured data. Parquet Columnar Embedded None Raw data files, external data, interop with non-Delta tools. Foundation of Delta. CSV Row-based text None None Landing zone for external data, exports from legacy systems. Always convert to Delta for analytics. JSON / NDJSON Semi-structured text None None API responses, event logs, semi-structured data. Read with Spark, convert to Delta.

blocks.content: The Conversion Pattern A common pattern in Fabric data engineering: land raw data in any format, then convert to Delta as quickly as possible.

External Source
       │
       v
  Files/raw/  (CSV, JSON, Parquet — as-is from source)
       │
       v  [PySpark Notebook: read, validate, write]
       │
  Tables/clean_data  (Delta table — schema-enforced, queryable)

Once data is in Delta format, every Fabric workload can query it: Spark, the Warehouse SQL engine, the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint, Power BI DirectLake, and KQL. Raw CSV and JSON files are only accessible through Spark or direct file APIs — they cannot be queried by the SQL engine or Power BI.
Important: The Files/ section of a Lakehouse is for landing and staging. The Tables/ section is for analytics-ready data. Get data from Files/ to Tables/ as early as possible in your pipeline. This is a core principle of Fabric data engineering.
Why Not Just Use Parquet?
Parquet is an excellent columnar format, and Delta Lake is built on top of it. So why not skip Delta and use plain Parquet?
The answer is the transaction log. Without it, you lose:
•  ACID transactions — concurrent reads and writes can corrupt data.
•  Schema enforcement — nothing prevents writing mismatched columns.
•  Time travel — no history of changes.
•  MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE — plain Parquet only supports full-file overwrites.
•  The Warehouse SQL engine — which requires Delta format to query tables.
In Fabric, Delta is always the right choice for structured analytical data. Plain Parquet is appropriate only for interoperability with external systems that do not understand Delta.

sections.title: Mirroring — Bringing External Data In Mirroring is Fabric’s mechanism for near-real-time replication of external databases into OneLake. Unlike shortcuts (which point to data in place without copying), mirroring creates a continuously updated copy of the source data as Delta tables in OneLake. This is the bridge between operational systems and the analytical platform. Your transactional database keeps running its OLTP workload. Fabric mirrors the data into OneLake, where every analytical workload can query it without impacting the source. Supported Mirroring Sources Source Replication Method Status Azure SQL Database Change Data Capture (CDC) Generally Available Azure SQL Managed Instance CDC via gateway Generally Available SQL Server (on-prem / VM) CDC or Change Feed (SQL Server 2025) Preview Azure Cosmos DB (NoSQL) Change Feed Generally Available Snowflake Managed tables + Iceberg tables Generally Available PostgreSQL (Azure / generic) Logical replication Generally Available Azure Databricks (Delta) Change Data Feed Preview

blocks.content:
How Mirroring Works
The mirroring process follows a consistent pattern regardless of source:
•  Initial snapshot: Fabric reads the entire source table and writes it as a Delta table in OneLake. This is a one-time bulk copy.
•  Continuous replication: After the snapshot, Fabric subscribes to the source’s change feed (CDC, logical replication, etc.) and applies incremental changes to the Delta table. Inserts, updates, and deletes flow automatically.
•  Delta table output: The result is a standard Delta table in OneLake, queryable by all Fabric workloads. The table includes system columns for tracking replication metadata.
Latency depends on the source and change volume, but is typically seconds to minutes. This is not a daily batch — it is near-real-time replication.
Mirroring vs. Shortcuts vs. Pipelines
Approach
Data Movement
Latency
Best For
Shortcut
None (read in place)
Real-time (network latency only)
External analytics files (ADLS, S3). Reference data. Cross-Lakehouse sharing.
Mirroring
Continuous replication
Seconds to minutes
Operational databases (SQL, Cosmos, Snowflake). Near-real-time analytics on transactional data.
Pipeline (Copy Activity)
Batch copy
Minutes to hours (schedule-dependent)
One-time loads, complex transformations, sources without CDC support.

Choose shortcuts when you want to query external data without copying it. Choose mirroring when you need a continuously fresh copy of a transactional database. Choose pipelines when you need batch control, transformation logic, or the source does not support change feeds.

sections.title: OneLake Security Fundamentals OneLake Security is Fabric’s vision for unified data access control enforced at the storage layer. The idea is simple and powerful: security rules live with the data, not with the engine. No matter which Fabric engine reads the data — Spark, the SQL engine, KQL, Power BI — the same security rules apply. This is a fundamental shift from the legacy approach, where each engine had its own security model. In the old world, you configured row-level security separately in Synapse SQL, in Power BI, and in Databricks. If they were inconsistent, users could see different data depending on which tool they used. OneLake Security eliminates this by pushing security to the storage layer itself. Security Layers Fabric enforces security at multiple levels, from coarse to fine:

blocks.content: Layer Granularity Mechanism Workspace Entire workspace Workspace roles: Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer. Controls who can see and modify any item in the workspace. Item Single item (e.g., one Lakehouse) Per-item sharing and permissions. Controls who can access a specific Lakehouse, Warehouse, or Report. Table Single table within an item OneLake Security table-level permissions. Controls which tables a user can read. Column Specific columns within a table Column-level security. Hides sensitive columns (e.g., SSN, salary) from unauthorized users. Row Specific rows within a table Row-level security (RLS). Users see only the rows they are authorized to access (e.g., only their region’s data).
How OneLake Security Applies Across Engines When OneLake Security is configured, the access rules are evaluated at the storage layer before data reaches any engine:

User Query (Spark / SQL / KQL / Power BI)
       │
       v
  OneLake Security Layer
       │  ── Check table access: Can this user read this table?
       │  ── Check column access: Which columns can this user see?
       │  ── Check row-level rules: Which rows match this user’s filters?
       │
       v
  Filtered data returned to the engine
       │
       v
  Query result (user only sees authorized data)

This means a data engineer can configure security once, and it applies everywhere — in Notebooks, in the Warehouse, in Power BI reports, and in KQL queries. No duplication. No inconsistency. Important: OneLake Security is a newer capability that reached public preview in 2025 and is expected to reach general availability in 2026. During preview, some limitations apply — not all engine integrations may be fully enforced. Check Microsoft’s documentation for the latest status. For now, many organizations still configure engine-specific security (T-SQL RLS in the Warehouse, DAX RLS in Power BI) alongside OneLake Security.

sections.title: Storage Metrics, Monitoring, and Costs OneLake storage is billed separately from compute. Understanding how storage is consumed, how to monitor it, and how to optimize costs is essential for production operations. How Storage Is Billed You pay for the total volume of data stored in OneLake, measured in GB per month. The pricing model is straightforward: • Data stored in OneLake — All Parquet data files, Delta transaction logs, and Files/ section contents count toward your storage consumption. • Shortcut data is NOT billed in OneLake — Because shortcuts point to external data, the storage cost stays with the source provider (ADLS Gen2, S3, GCS). • Mirrored data IS billed in OneLake — Because mirroring creates a physical copy in Delta format, the replicated data counts toward your OneLake storage. • Soft-deleted and versioned data — Old Delta file versions that have not been vacuumed still consume storage. This is a common source of unexpected costs. Table Maintenance: OPTIMIZE and VACUUM Delta tables accumulate small files over time (from frequent appends) and retain old versions (from the transaction log). Two maintenance operations keep tables healthy and storage costs low:

blocks.content: OPTIMIZE compacts small files into larger ones. This improves read performance (fewer file opens) and can be combined with Z-ORDER for column-level data clustering: -- Compact small files OPTIMIZE orders
-- Compact and cluster by a frequently filtered column OPTIMIZE orders ZORDER BY (customer_id)

VACUUM deletes Parquet files that are no longer referenced by the transaction log. By default, it retains files for 7 days (to support time travel). After vacuuming, you cannot travel to versions older than the retention period: -- Delete unreferenced files older than 7 days (default) VACUUM orders
-- Delete unreferenced files older than 24 hours VACUUM orders RETAIN 24 HOURS

Warning: Setting VACUUM retention below 7 days is dangerous. Any readers or writers that started before the vacuum may fail because files they expected are gone. In production, stick to the 7-day default unless you have a specific reason and understand the risk. V-Order Optimization V-Order is a Fabric-proprietary write-time optimization that reorders data within Parquet files for faster reads across all engines. When V-Order is enabled (which it is by default in Fabric), writes take slightly longer, but reads are significantly faster — especially for Power BI DirectLake and SQL analytics workloads. V-Order applies columnar sorting, dictionary encoding, and other optimizations that are compatible with standard Parquet readers but optimized for Fabric’s engines. You do not need to configure it — it is on by default. But understanding that it exists explains why Fabric-written Parquet files often perform better than Parquet files written by external tools. Monitoring Storage Consumption You can monitor OneLake storage through several mechanisms:

items: Fabric Capacity Metrics app — Shows storage consumption trends at the capacity and workspace level.
Workspace settings — Each workspace shows its current storage consumption.
DESCRIBE DETAIL in SQL/Spark — Returns the size of a specific Delta table including file count and total bytes.
OneLake Storage Diagnostics — A Fabric-native tool for inspecting storage usage patterns and identifying optimization opportunities.

blocks.content: Cost Optimization Best Practices

items: Run OPTIMIZE regularly on tables that receive frequent small appends (e.g., daily or weekly).
Run VACUUM to reclaim storage from old file versions. Schedule it after OPTIMIZE.
Use shortcuts instead of copies for reference data and cross-team sharing.
Use partitioning judiciously — too many partitions create too many small files, increasing both storage overhead and query latency.
Monitor storage weekly during early adoption to understand growth patterns and set retention policies.
Convert raw files (CSV, JSON) to Delta as early as possible — Delta’s columnar compression is dramatically more storage-efficient.

sections.title: Module 2 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: OneLake architecture — the four-level hierarchy (tenant → workspace → item → files and tables), ADLS Gen2 under the hood, and the onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com endpoint
Delta Lake as the universal format — Parquet data files + JSON transaction log, ACID transactions, schema enforcement, schema evolution, and time travel
Shortcuts — virtual pointers to external data (ADLS Gen2, S3, GCS, Dataverse, other Lakehouses) that enable zero-copy access within the OneLake namespace
Programmatic access — OneLake File Explorer for Windows desktops, ADLS Gen2 REST APIs, Azure Storage SDKs, and abfss:// paths in Spark
The OneLake Catalog — built-in discovery and metadata: search, browse, domains, tags, endorsement, and lineage
Data formats — why Delta is the preferred format, when Parquet/CSV/JSON appear, and the Files/ → Tables/ conversion pattern
Mirroring — near-real-time replication from Azure SQL, Cosmos DB, Snowflake, PostgreSQL, and other sources into OneLake Delta tables
OneLake Security — table-level, column-level, and row-level security enforced at the storage layer, applied consistently across all engines
Storage management — OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, V-Order, storage billing, monitoring, and cost optimization

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Before moving to Module 3, complete these exercises:

items: Create a Lakehouse in your workspace. Upload a CSV file to the Files/ section. Write a Spark notebook that reads the CSV and writes it as a Delta table in the Tables/ section. Verify the table appears in the SQL analytics endpoint.

Create a shortcut in your Lakehouse that points to another Lakehouse (or an ADLS Gen2 container if available). Query the shortcutted data from a Notebook and confirm it works identically to local data.
Open the OneLake Catalog. Search for the items you created. Examine the metadata: schema, owner, workspace, lineage.
Run DESCRIBE DETAIL on your Delta table (in SQL or Spark) and note the file count and size. Run OPTIMIZE. Run DESCRIBE DETAIL again and compare.
Use time travel to read a previous version of your table after making a modification. Confirm the old data is still accessible.

blocks.content: You are not just storing files.

You are operating a unified storage layer that serves as the single source of truth for every engine, every workload, and every user in your organization. Every table is transactional. Every file is discoverable. Every access is governed.

In Module 3, we build on this foundation with the Lakehouse — the primary data engineering item in Fabric. You will learn how to create Lakehouses, load data through every available path, manage the dual Files/ and Tables/ interface, use the SQL analytics endpoint, and understand when to choose a Lakehouse over a Warehouse.

chapters.title: Lakehouse Fundamentals
chapters.description: Where Files Meet Tables Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Build and operate a Lakehouse — the core data engineering item in Fabric. Understand the dual interface (files + tables), SQL analytics endpoint, and how the Lakehouse bridges raw storage with structured analytics.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 3 In Module 1, you learned the architecture. In Module 2, you learned the storage layer. Now you build on that foundation with the item you will use more than any other in this course: the Lakehouse. The Lakehouse is Fabric’s primary data engineering artifact. It is where raw files land, where Delta tables are created, where Spark notebooks read and write, where the SQL analytics endpoint auto-generates a T-SQL surface, and where Power BI’s DirectLake mode reads data for dashboards. If OneLake is the storage layer, the Lakehouse is the door through which most data enters and exits that layer. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. The Lakehouse implements the lakehouse paradigm — a modern architecture that combines the flexibility of a data lake (store anything, any format, any scale) with the structure of a data warehouse (schema enforcement, SQL querying, ACID transactions). Before this paradigm existed, organizations had to choose between a lake (flexible but ungoverned) and a warehouse (governed but rigid). The Lakehouse gives you both in one item. This module takes you from creation to production: what a Lakehouse is, how to create one, every way to load data into it, the dual Files/ and Tables/ interface, the auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint, the default semantic model, when to choose a Lakehouse over a Warehouse, best practices for organization and maintenance, and a hands-on checkpoint that ties it all together.

sections.title: What Is a Lakehouse? The Lakehouse is a Fabric item that provides a dual-interface storage experience: an unstructured file system (Files/) and a managed table system (Tables/), both backed by OneLake. It is the convergence point where raw data arrives and structured, analytics-ready data emerges. The Lakehouse Paradigm The lakehouse paradigm was popularized by Databricks and has become the dominant architectural pattern for modern data platforms. The core idea: use an open file format (Delta Lake on Parquet) stored in a data lake, but layer on the governance features traditionally found only in warehouses — ACID transactions, schema enforcement, SQL access, and fine-grained security. Fabric’s Lakehouse is a concrete implementation of this paradigm. When you create a Lakehouse in Fabric, you get: • A Files/ section — A folder hierarchy in OneLake for landing raw, unstructured, or semi-structured data. CSVs, JSONs, images, Parquet files, XML — anything can go here. No schema is enforced. This is your landing zone. • A Tables/ section — A managed area where Delta tables live. Every table here has a schema, ACID transactions, and a transaction log. These tables are queryable by Spark, by the SQL analytics endpoint, and by Power BI. • A SQL analytics endpoint — An auto-generated, read-only T-SQL interface that exposes every Delta table in the Tables/ section. SQL analysts can query Lakehouse tables without Spark, without Python, without notebooks. They just write T-SQL. • A default semantic model — An auto-generated Power BI semantic model that makes Lakehouse tables immediately available for reporting. You can build a Power BI report directly on top of a Lakehouse without any additional modeling.

blocks.content: This four-in-one design is what makes the Lakehouse the most versatile item in Fabric. It serves data engineers (Files/ + Spark), SQL analysts (SQL analytics endpoint), and BI developers (default semantic model) from a single item.

sections.title: Loading Data into the Lakehouse Data enters a Lakehouse through multiple paths. Understanding all of them lets you choose the right one for each scenario. Ingestion Paths Compared Ingestion Method Target Code Required Best For Portal upload (drag-and-drop) Files/ None Quick uploads of small files during development. Not for production. OneLake File Explorer Files/ None Dragging files from your Windows desktop into OneLake. Good for ad-hoc loading. Notebook (PySpark) Files/ or Tables/ Python / Spark SQL Production ETL pipelines. Reading any format, transforming, writing Delta tables. Pipeline (Copy Activity) Files/ or Tables/ None (visual) Scheduled batch ingestion from 300+ sources. Enterprise orchestration. Dataflow Gen2 Tables/ None (visual) Low-code transformations with Power Query. Best for analyst-friendly ingestion. Shortcut Files/ or Tables/ None Zero-copy access to external data (ADLS Gen2, S3, other Lakehouses). No data movement. Mirroring Tables/ None Near-real-time replication from operational databases (SQL, Cosmos, Snowflake). ADLS Gen2 API / SDK Files/ Python / REST Programmatic upload from external systems. CI/CD pipelines. Automated data feeds.

blocks.content: The Typical Ingestion Flow In most production environments, the ingestion flow follows a two-step pattern: Step 1: Land raw data in Files/. Use a Pipeline Copy Activity, an API upload, or a shortcut to place raw files (CSV, JSON, Parquet) into the Files/ section. No transformation happens here — the goal is to capture the source data as-is, as quickly as possible. Step 2: Transform and write to Tables/. A Spark notebook (or Dataflow Gen2) reads from Files/, applies cleaning and transformation logic, and writes the result as a Delta table in Tables/. Once data is in Tables/, it is queryable by every engine in Fabric.

External Source
       │
       v  [Pipeline Copy Activity / API / Shortcut]
  Files/raw_data/   (CSV, JSON, Parquet — as-is)
       │
       v  [Notebook: spark.read → transform → df.write]
  Tables/clean_data  (Delta table — queryable by all engines)
       │
       v  [SQL Analytics Endpoint / Power BI / Warehouse]
  Downstream consumers

This two-step pattern is the foundation of the medallion architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold) that we will formalize in Module 17. For now, the key principle is: land fast in Files/, transform into Tables/. Important: You can also write directly to Tables/ (skipping Files/) if your source produces clean, well-structured data. A Pipeline Copy Activity can write Parquet directly to the Tables/ section, where Fabric auto-registers it as a Delta table. This shortcut is useful for simple ingestion scenarios where no transformation is needed.

sections.title: The Files Section — Unstructured and Semi-Structured The Files/ section of a Lakehouse is an open folder hierarchy in OneLake. There are no rules about what you put here, no schema enforcement, and no table registration. It is your landing zone, your staging area, and your archive for anything that is not (yet) a Delta table. What Goes in Files/ • Raw CSV exports from source systems • JSON payloads from APIs • Parquet files from external pipelines • Images, PDFs, and binary files for ML workloads • XML, NDJSON, and other semi-structured formats • Archived data that needs to be retained but not queried frequently Organizing the Files/ Hierarchy A disciplined folder structure prevents the Files/ section from becoming a data swamp. A recommended pattern:

blocks.content:
Files/
    └── raw/
    │     └── orders/
    │     │     └── 2026-01-15/     (date-partitioned landing)
    │     │     └── 2026-01-16/
    │     └── customers/
    │           └── full_export_2026-01.json
    └── staging/
    │     └── temp_merge_output/
    └── archive/
          └── 2025/

Key principles: use date-based subfolders for incremental loads, separate raw from staging from archive, and name folders descriptively. This structure makes it easy for notebooks to read specific partitions (e.g., spark.read.csv('Files/raw/orders/2026-01-15/')) and for humans to navigate in the portal. Files/ Limitations Files in the Files/ section are not queryable by the SQL analytics endpoint or by Power BI DirectLake. Only Delta tables in the Tables/ section are visible to those engines. This is by design — raw files lack the schema and transaction guarantees that SQL and BI tools require. If you need to query raw files with SQL, you have two options: convert them to Delta tables (the recommended path), or use Spark SQL’s ability to query files directly from a Notebook (e.g., SELECT * FROM csv.`Files/raw/orders/`). The second option is useful for exploration but not for production analytics.

sections.title: The Tables Section — Managed Delta Tables The Tables/ section is where analytics-ready data lives. Every table here is a Delta Lake table — schema-enforced, ACID-transactional, time-travel-capable, and queryable by every Fabric engine. Creating Tables Tables can be created through multiple paths:

blocks.content:
From a Notebook (most common): Read data from Files/ or any other source, transform it, and write it as a Delta table.
# Read raw CSV from Files/
raw = spark.read.csv('Files/raw/orders/', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Transform
clean = raw.filter(col('amount') > 0).withColumn('load_date', current_date())

# Write as a managed Delta table
clean.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('orders')
From SQL in a Notebook: Use Spark SQL to create tables with familiar DDL. %%sql CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders ( order_id STRING, customer_id STRING, amount DOUBLE, order_date DATE ) USING DELTA

From a Pipeline Copy Activity: Configure the Copy Activity to write directly to the Tables/ section. Fabric auto-registers the output as a Delta table.
From a Dataflow Gen2: Configure the data destination as a Lakehouse table. Dataflows Gen2 write directly to managed Delta tables.
Table Properties and Metadata
Once a table exists in the Tables/ section, Fabric automatically:
•  Registers the table in the Lakehouse’s metastore so it is visible to Spark SQL (spark.sql('SELECT * FROM orders')).
•  Exposes the table through the SQL analytics endpoint so T-SQL clients can query it.
•  Adds the table to the default semantic model so Power BI can report on it.
•  Indexes the table in the OneLake Catalog for discovery.
You can inspect table metadata with:
# Spark
spark.sql('DESCRIBE TABLE EXTENDED orders').show(truncate=False)

# Spark: file-level details
spark.sql('DESCRIBE DETAIL orders').show()

Partitioning Large tables benefit from partitioning — organizing data files into subdirectories by one or more columns (typically date or region). Partitioning enables partition pruning, where the engine skips entire directories that do not match the query filter. # Write a partitioned table df.write.format('delta') \ .mode('overwrite') \ .partitionBy('order_date') \ .saveAsTable('orders_partitioned')

Warning: Over-partitioning is a common mistake. If your partition column has high cardinality (e.g., customer_id with millions of values), you will create millions of tiny files. This destroys performance. Partition by low-cardinality columns (date, region, status) and aim for file sizes between 128 MB and 1 GB after OPTIMIZE.

sections.title: The SQL Analytics Endpoint Every Lakehouse automatically generates a SQL analytics endpoint — a read-only T-SQL interface that exposes all Delta tables in the Tables/ section. This is one of Fabric’s most powerful integration features: it means SQL analysts, BI tools, and any JDBC/ODBC client can query Lakehouse data without Spark, without Python, without notebooks. How It Works The SQL analytics endpoint is a separate Fabric item that is created and managed automatically alongside the Lakehouse. It has its own connection string and appears as a SQL database in tools like SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) and Azure Data Studio. When a query hits the SQL analytics endpoint, Fabric’s distributed SQL engine reads the Delta tables directly from OneLake. There is no data copy, no import step, no sync delay. The endpoint reads the same Delta files that Spark writes.

blocks.content:
Spark Notebook writes Delta table
       │
       v
  OneLake: Tables/orders/ (Delta files)
       │                    │
       v                    v
  Spark reads it      SQL Endpoint reads it
  (same files)        (same files, T-SQL syntax)

What You Can Do
The SQL analytics endpoint supports:
•  SELECT queries with full T-SQL syntax: JOINs, CTEs, window functions, aggregations, subqueries.
•  Views: CREATE VIEW to define reusable SQL abstractions over Lakehouse tables.
•  Security: Table-level and column-level permissions. Row-level security through security policies.
•  Cross-database queries: Join Lakehouse tables with Warehouse tables using three-part naming.
What You Cannot Do
The SQL analytics endpoint is read-only. It does not support:
•  INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or MERGE — all write operations must go through Spark or Pipelines.
•  CREATE TABLE — tables are created through Spark, Pipelines, or Dataflows, not through the SQL endpoint.
•  Stored procedures — the SQL analytics endpoint does not support procedural T-SQL.
Important: If you need full DML (write) capabilities through T-SQL, use a Fabric Data Warehouse instead. The Warehouse provides a full T-SQL surface with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, and stored procedures. The decision between Lakehouse and Warehouse often comes down to whether your team needs T-SQL writes.
Connecting External Tools
The SQL analytics endpoint has a standard TDS (Tabular Data Stream) connection string. You can connect from:
•  SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS)
•  Azure Data Studio
•  DBeaver, DataGrip, or any JDBC/ODBC client
•  Power BI Desktop (via the SQL endpoint connection)
•  Excel (via the SQL endpoint connection)
•  Custom applications using the Microsoft JDBC or ODBC drivers
The connection string is found in the Lakehouse settings or by clicking the SQL analytics endpoint item in the workspace.

sections.title: Default Semantic Model Every Lakehouse automatically generates a Power BI semantic model. This model is a DirectLake dataset that reads Delta tables directly from OneLake — no import, no DirectQuery, no scheduled refresh. When the Delta table changes, the model sees the new data on the next query. What Is DirectLake? DirectLake is Fabric’s native connection mode for Power BI. It is the third option beyond the traditional Import and DirectQuery modes:

blocks.content: Mode Data Location Performance Freshness Import Copied into Power BI model Fastest (in-memory) Stale until next refresh DirectQuery Queried from source on demand Slower (network round-trip) Always current DirectLake Read directly from OneLake Delta files Near-import speed Always current (no copy)

DirectLake combines the performance of Import (data is read into the Vertipaq engine’s columnar format) with the freshness of DirectQuery (no copy to maintain). It works because the data is already in columnar Delta/Parquet format in OneLake — the Vertipaq engine can read it directly.
The default semantic model uses DirectLake automatically. You do not configure it. This means every Lakehouse table is immediately available for Power BI reporting with near-zero setup.
Customizing the Default Semantic Model
The auto-generated model is a starting point, not a finished product. You can customize it by:
•  Selecting which tables to include (not all Lakehouse tables need to be in the model).
•  Defining relationships between tables (fact-to-dimension joins).
•  Adding DAX measures for calculated business metrics.
•  Setting data types and formatting for better report readability.
For simple reporting needs, the default model may be sufficient. For production BI, you will typically create a dedicated semantic model with proper star schema design, as covered in Module 14.
When the Default Semantic Model Falls Short
The default semantic model has limitations:
•  It includes all columns from included tables. There is no column pruning or calculated column support in the default model.
•  It does not support complex DAX measures until you manually add them.
•  It auto-detects relationships, which may not match your intended star schema design.
•  It does not support composite models (mixing DirectLake with DirectQuery from other sources).
For these scenarios, create a custom semantic model connected to the Lakehouse via DirectLake, or connect the semantic model to the SQL analytics endpoint using DirectQuery. Module 14 covers this in depth.

sections.title: Lakehouse vs. Warehouse — When to Use Which This is one of the most common questions in Fabric: should I use a Lakehouse or a Warehouse? The answer depends on your team, your workload, and your data lifecycle. Architectural Differences Both the Lakehouse and the Warehouse store data as Delta tables in OneLake. The underlying storage is identical. The difference is in the interface and capabilities each provides on top of that storage.

blocks.content: Criterion Lakehouse Data Warehouse Primary Interface Spark (PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, R) T-SQL SQL Access Read-only SQL analytics endpoint (auto-generated) Full DML: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, stored procedures Write Path Spark notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows T-SQL DML, Pipelines, Dataflows, COPY INTO Schema Flexibility Files/ section accepts any format; Tables/ are Delta All tables are structured Delta from creation Language Support Python, SQL, Scala, R in notebooks T-SQL only Spark Access Native — Lakehouse is a Spark-first item Can read from Spark, but not a Spark-native item Power BI Connection DirectLake via default semantic model DirectLake via semantic model Ideal Persona Data engineers, data scientists SQL analysts, analytics engineers, BI developers Partitioning Control Full Spark-level partition control Automatic distribution (round-robin) Stored Procedures Not supported Fully supported

Decision Framework Choose a Lakehouse when: your team writes PySpark, you need the Files/ landing zone for raw data, you process semi-structured formats (JSON, XML, images), you need Spark-level control over partitioning and optimization, or you are building the medallion architecture’s Bronze and Silver layers. Choose a Warehouse when: your team thinks in T-SQL, you need full DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE), you want stored procedures for encapsulated logic, your workload is pure SQL analytics without Spark, or you are building the Gold layer for governed BI consumption by SQL analysts. Use both when: your platform serves both data engineers and SQL analysts. A common pattern: data engineers build Bronze and Silver layers in Lakehouses with PySpark, then the Gold layer is either a Lakehouse (if downstream is Power BI DirectLake) or a Warehouse (if downstream needs T-SQL DML and stored procedures). Important: There is no wrong answer. Both items store Delta tables in OneLake. Both are queryable. Both feed Power BI. The choice is about which interface your team is most productive with. In many organizations, the answer is both — Lakehouses for engineering, Warehouses for SQL analytics.

sections.title: Lakehouse Best Practices A well-organized Lakehouse is the difference between a reliable data platform and a data swamp. These best practices apply to every Lakehouse you build. File Organization • Keep the Files/ section organized with a clear folder hierarchy: raw/, staging/, archive/. • Use date-based subfolders for incremental data (e.g., raw/orders/2026-01-15/). • Never leave raw files in Files/ indefinitely — process them into Tables/ and archive or delete the originals. • Avoid deeply nested folder hierarchies. Two to three levels of depth is sufficient. Table Organization • Name tables descriptively: orders, dim_customers, fact_daily_revenue — not table1, temp, or test_final_v2. • Use the medallion pattern (Bronze, Silver, Gold) to organize tables by data quality level. Different Lakehouses for each layer, or clear naming prefixes (bronze_orders, silver_orders, gold_fact_orders). • Document table purposes in the OneLake Catalog using descriptions and tags. • Endorse (Promote or Certify) tables that are ready for downstream consumption. Table Maintenance Delta tables require regular maintenance to stay performant: • OPTIMIZE weekly (or daily for high-volume tables) to compact small files. Target file sizes of 128 MB to 1 GB. • VACUUM weekly after OPTIMIZE to reclaim storage from old file versions. Use the default 7-day retention. • Z-ORDER on columns that are frequently used in WHERE clauses. This clusters data for faster filtered reads. • Monitor table sizes with DESCRIBE DETAIL. Watch for tables with thousands of small files — this is the most common performance problem.

blocks.content: -- Weekly maintenance script (run in a Spark SQL cell) OPTIMIZE orders ZORDER BY (customer_id); VACUUM orders;
OPTIMIZE customers; VACUUM customers;
-- Check table health after maintenance DESCRIBE DETAIL orders;
Naming Conventions Consistent naming is a governance foundation. Recommended patterns:

Item Pattern Example Lakehouse lh_{layer}_{domain} lh_bronze_sales, lh_gold_finance Tables (Bronze) bronze_{source}_{entity} bronze_crm_orders Tables (Silver) silver_{entity} silver_orders Tables (Gold) fact_{metric} or dim_{entity} fact_daily_revenue, dim_customers Files folders raw/{source}/{date}/ raw/crm/2026-01-15/
Security

items: Apply workspace roles as your first line of defense. Only team members who need access should be in the workspace.
Use OneLake Security (table-level, column-level) for finer-grained control once it reaches GA.
For the SQL analytics endpoint, configure T-SQL permissions (GRANT SELECT on specific tables) to restrict analyst access.
For the default semantic model, configure Row-Level Security (RLS) in DAX if reports are shared broadly.

sections.title: Module 3 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: What a Lakehouse is — the dual-interface item that combines Files/ (raw storage) with Tables/ (managed Delta tables), plus an auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint and default semantic model
