How to create a Lakehouse — one-click creation with automatic OneLake storage, SQL endpoint, semantic model, and catalog registration
Every ingestion path — portal upload, File Explorer, Notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows Gen2, shortcuts, mirroring, and APIs
The Files/ section — the unstructured landing zone for raw data, organized with date-based folder hierarchies
The Tables/ section — managed Delta tables with schema enforcement, created from Notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows, or SQL
The SQL analytics endpoint — auto-generated read-only T-SQL interface for SQL analysts and BI tools, with views and security but no DML
The default semantic model — auto-generated DirectLake Power BI dataset for instant reporting on Lakehouse tables
Lakehouse vs. Warehouse — choose by team skill (Spark vs. T-SQL) and write requirements (DML needs), not by performance
Best practices — file organization, table naming, maintenance (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, Z-ORDER), partitioning, and security

blocks.content: Build a Lakehouse from Scratch Before moving to Module 4, complete this end-to-end exercise:

items: Create a Lakehouse named lh_workshop in your workspace.
Download a sample CSV dataset (e.g., from Kaggle or a public dataset) and upload it to Files/raw/.
Create a Spark notebook attached to the Lakehouse. Read the CSV, inspect the schema, handle any data quality issues (nulls, type mismatches), and write the result as a Delta table in Tables/.
Switch to the SQL analytics endpoint view. Write a T-SQL query that aggregates the data (e.g., COUNT, SUM, GROUP BY). Confirm the table is queryable.
Open the default semantic model. Create a simple Power BI report with one visual on the Lakehouse data.
Run DESCRIBE DETAIL on your table. Note the file count. Run OPTIMIZE. Run DESCRIBE DETAIL again and observe the compaction.
Use time travel: append new rows to the table, then read the previous version to confirm the original data is still accessible.

blocks.content: You are not just creating a storage container.

You are building the central artifact of Fabric data engineering — the item where raw data transforms into governed, queryable, reportable analytics. Every module from here forward will read from or write to a Lakehouse.

In Module 4, we turn to Data Factory — Pipelines — the orchestration engine that automates everything you just did manually. You will learn to build production-grade pipelines that ingest data, trigger notebooks, handle errors, and run on schedule.

chapters.title: Data Factory — Pipelines
chapters.description: The Orchestration Engine Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Design and orchestrate data movement and transformation pipelines using Fabric Data Factory. Master activities, triggers, parameters, and monitoring for production ETL/ELT.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 4 In the previous modules you learned the platform (Module 1), the storage layer (Module 2), and the Lakehouse (Module 3). You can now create a Lakehouse, land data in Files/, transform it in a Notebook, and write Delta tables to Tables/. But so far, everything you have done is manual. You opened a notebook, clicked Run All, and watched it execute. That is fine for development. It is not how production data platforms work. Production data platforms are automated. Data arrives on a schedule. Notebooks execute without human intervention. Failures trigger retries and alerts. Parameters change based on the date, the source, or the environment. One pipeline orchestrates dozens of steps in the correct order, with dependencies, branching, and error handling. That is what Data Factory Pipelines do. A pipeline is a workflow graph — a directed sequence of activities that execute in order, with control flow logic, parameterization, and monitoring. If the Lakehouse is where data lives, the pipeline is how data gets there and how it stays fresh. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. This module takes you from understanding pipelines conceptually to building production-grade orchestration: the anatomy of a pipeline, the Copy Activity in depth, parameterization with dynamic content, orchestrating Notebooks and Spark jobs, scheduling with triggers, error handling and retry logic, monitoring and debugging, and the Apache Airflow alternative.

sections.title: Pipeline Anatomy — Activities, Links, and Control Flow A pipeline is a directed graph of activities. Each activity performs a unit of work: copying data, running a notebook, executing a stored procedure, calling a web API, or controlling flow (branching, looping, waiting). Activities are connected by dependency links that define execution order and conditional behavior. Activity Categories Category Key Activities Purpose Data Movement Copy Activity Move data between sources and destinations. The workhorse of ingestion pipelines. Transformation Notebook, Dataflow, Stored Procedure, Spark Job Definition Execute transformation logic: PySpark notebooks, Power Query dataflows, T-SQL procedures. Control Flow ForEach, If Condition, Until, Switch, Wait, Execute Pipeline Branching, looping, sub-pipeline invocation. Build dynamic, conditional workflows. Utility Set Variable, Append Variable, Lookup, Get Metadata, Web Activity, Fail Read metadata, call APIs, set variables, or force a pipeline failure with a message.

blocks.content: Dependency Links and Conditions When you connect two activities with a link, you specify a dependency condition that determines when the downstream activity runs:

Condition Symbol When It Fires Succeeded Green check The upstream activity completed successfully. Failed Red X The upstream activity failed (threw an error). Completed Blue circle The upstream activity finished, regardless of success or failure. Skipped Gray arrow The upstream activity was skipped (e.g., due to an If Condition branch not taken).

By combining these conditions, you build sophisticated control flow. For example: if the Copy Activity succeeds, run the Notebook; if it fails, send an email notification via a Web Activity and then execute a Fail activity to mark the pipeline as failed. The Activity Graph Here is a typical pipeline structure for a daily ingestion workflow:

This pattern — ingest, transform, log success on the happy path; alert and fail on the error path — is the foundation of production pipelines. Every pipeline you build will be a variation of this graph.

sections.title: Copy Activity Deep Dive The Copy Activity is the most-used activity in Data Factory. It moves data from a source to a destination (called a “sink” in ADF terminology). In Fabric, the most common pattern is copying data from an external source into a Lakehouse. Source and Sink Configuration Every Copy Activity has a source connection and a sink connection. In Fabric, configuring these is simpler than in ADF because you reference Fabric items directly: • Source: Select from 300+ built-in connectors — Azure SQL Database, Blob Storage, SharePoint, REST APIs, Salesforce, Snowflake, Oracle, SAP, and hundreds more. Each connector has specific configuration options (e.g., query for SQL sources, file path for storage sources). • Sink (Destination): Select a Fabric item — typically a Lakehouse (Files/ or Tables/ section), a Warehouse table, or a KQL Database. For Lakehouse targets, you choose whether to land in Files/ (as-is file copy) or Tables/ (auto-converted to Delta). Column Mapping The Copy Activity can map source columns to destination columns. This is useful when: • Source and destination have different column names. • You want to drop columns during copy (projection). • You need to change data types (e.g., source sends string dates, destination expects DATE type). For simple copies where source and destination schemas match, auto-mapping handles the mapping automatically. For schema drift scenarios (where source columns may appear or disappear), enable “Allow schema drift” in the sink settings. Performance Tuning Copy Activity performance depends on three factors: • Parallelism (Data Integration Units / DIUs): The number of parallel threads used for the copy. Higher DIU counts = faster copies for large datasets. Start with Auto and increase manually if throughput is insufficient. • Source partitioning: For SQL sources, enable parallel reads by partitioning the source query (e.g., by a date or ID range). This reads multiple chunks simultaneously instead of a single sequential scan. • File format and compression: Parquet with Snappy compression is the optimal format for OneLake ingestion. It provides the best combination of read performance and storage efficiency. Avoid landing as CSV if possible. Copy to Files/ vs. Copy to Tables/ When targeting a Lakehouse, you have two destination options:

blocks.content: Destination What Happens When to Use Lakehouse Files/ Raw file is copied as-is into the specified folder. No schema enforcement, no Delta conversion. When you want to land raw data for later processing by a Notebook. When the source format is non-tabular (images, XML, nested JSON). Lakehouse Tables/ Data is converted to Delta format and registered as a managed table. Schema is inferred or specified. When the source is clean, structured data that needs no transformation. Skips the Notebook step for simple ingestion.

Important: Copying directly to Tables/ is convenient but limited. You cannot apply complex transformations, handle deduplication, or implement merge logic in a Copy Activity. For production ETL with business rules, land in Files/ and transform with a Notebook.

sections.title: Parameterization and Dynamic Content Hard-coded pipelines are brittle. A pipeline that copies from one specific table on one specific date breaks the moment your requirements change. Parameterization makes pipelines flexible, reusable, and maintainable. Pipeline Parameters Parameters are values that you define at the pipeline level and pass in at runtime. They make a single pipeline serve multiple purposes.

blocks.content: // Reference in a SQL source query: SELECT * FROM @{pipeline().parameters.source_table} WHERE order_date = '@{pipeline().parameters.load_date}'

You can trigger the pipeline with different parameter values each time — today for orders, tomorrow for customers, next week for products — using the same pipeline definition. System Variables Fabric provides built-in system variables that you can reference in any expression:

Variable Returns Common Use @pipeline().RunId Unique ID for this pipeline run Audit logging, correlating logs across activities @pipeline().PipelineName Name of the current pipeline Dynamic logging messages @pipeline().TriggerTime Timestamp when the trigger fired Date-based file paths, partition keys @pipeline().GroupId Unique group ID for the run Linking related pipeline runs @utcnow() Current UTC timestamp Timestamping output files and log entries @formatDateTime(utcnow(),'yyyy-MM-dd') Formatted date string Building date-partitioned file paths

Dynamic Content and Expressions Dynamic content is the expression language that powers parameterization. It uses the @{...} syntax inside activity settings and supports string interpolation, functions, and conditional logic.
// Dynamic file path based on date Files/raw/orders/@{formatDateTime(pipeline().parameters.load_date, 'yyyy/MM/dd')}/
// Conditional expression @if(equals(pipeline().parameters.environment, 'prod'), 'lh_gold_sales', 'lh_dev_sales' )
// Concatenation @concat('Processing table: ', pipeline().parameters.source_table, ' for date: ', pipeline().parameters.load_date)

Dynamic content turns a static pipeline into a template. One pipeline definition can handle dozens of tables, dates, and environments through parameterization alone. This is the foundation of scalable orchestration.

sections.title: Orchestrating Notebooks and Spark Jobs The Notebook Activity is how pipelines trigger Spark-based transformations. It is the bridge between orchestration (Data Factory) and computation (Spark in Notebooks). Calling a Notebook from a Pipeline To add a Notebook Activity: • Drag a Notebook Activity onto the pipeline canvas. • In the settings, select the Notebook you want to run. • Optionally, select a Lakehouse to attach (the notebook will use this as its default Lakehouse). • Optionally, pass parameters from the pipeline to the notebook. • Connect the activity to the rest of your pipeline graph with dependency links. Passing Parameters to Notebooks Pipeline parameters can flow into notebook parameters, making the same notebook reusable for different dates, tables, or configurations.

blocks.content:
# In the pipeline: define a parameter 'load_date' = '2026-01-15'
# Pass it to the Notebook Activity's Base Parameters section

# In the notebook: receive the parameter
load_date = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('load_date', '2026-01-01')
print(f'Processing data for: {load_date}')

# Use it in your Spark logic
raw = spark.read.csv(f'Files/raw/orders/{load_date}/', header=True)
# ... transform and write

The second argument to getParam() is the default value, used when the notebook runs interactively (outside a pipeline). This lets you develop and test the notebook independently, then orchestrate it from a pipeline with dynamic values. Capturing Return Values Notebooks can return values to the pipeline using notebookutils.notebook.exit():

# At the end of the notebook
row_count = df.count()
notebookutils.notebook.exit(f'{row_count}')

# In the next pipeline activity, reference the output:
# @activity('TransformNotebook').output.result.exitValue
# This returns the string '12345' (the row count)

Return values are useful for logging, for conditional branching (if the notebook processed zero rows, skip downstream activities), and for chaining pipelines that depend on intermediate results. Spark Job Definitions For production Spark workloads that do not need the notebook UI, Fabric supports Spark Job Definitions — pre-packaged PySpark or Scala JAR files that run as batch jobs. These are referenced from pipelines just like notebooks but are better suited for stable, tested code that does not need interactive editing. Important: Choose Notebook Activities for pipelines where the transformation logic is still evolving or where you want interactive debugging. Choose Spark Job Definitions for mature, stable code that runs the same way every time.

sections.title: Scheduling and Triggers A pipeline without a trigger is a workflow that only runs when you click “Run.” Triggers automate execution based on time, events, or tumbling windows. Trigger Types Trigger Type How It Works Best For Scheduled Runs on a CRON-like schedule: daily, hourly, every 15 minutes, specific days of the week. Regular batch ingestion (daily load at 2:00 AM, hourly refresh). Tumbling Window Runs on fixed, non-overlapping time windows. Each window is independent and can be rerun. Supports backfill. Time-series processing where each run handles exactly one window (e.g., process yesterday’s data, then the day before if missed). Event-Based Fires when a file arrives in a storage location (OneLake, ADLS Gen2, Blob Storage). Event-driven ingestion: process each file as it lands, rather than on a schedule.

blocks.content: Scheduled Triggers in Detail Scheduled triggers are the most common type. You configure:

items: Start date and time — when the trigger becomes active.
Recurrence — every N minutes, hours, days, or weeks. For fine-grained control, use a CRON expression.
Time zone — the trigger fires relative to the specified time zone. Always set this explicitly to avoid confusion.
End date (optional) — when the trigger stops firing. Leave blank for indefinite schedules.

blocks.content: // Example schedule: Every day at 02:00 AM UTC // Recurrence: 1 day // Start time: 2026-01-01T02:00:00Z // Time zone: UTC
// Example CRON: Every weekday at 06:30 AM // 30 6 * * 1-5

Tumbling Window Triggers
Tumbling windows are ideal for time-partitioned processing. Each window represents a fixed time interval (e.g., one day), and the trigger creates one pipeline run per window. Key advantages:
•  Backfill support — If a window was missed (the pipeline was down), the trigger automatically creates runs for the missed windows when it resumes.
•  Dependency support — A tumbling window trigger can depend on another tumbling window trigger, ensuring that upstream processing completes before downstream starts.
•  Retry on window level — Each window can be individually retried without affecting other windows.
Event-Based Triggers
Event-based triggers fire when a file appears in a monitored storage location. This is powerful for real-time ingestion: as soon as a partner drops a file in an ADLS Gen2 container, the pipeline runs and processes it.
Important: Event-based triggers require that the storage location supports event notifications. OneLake and ADLS Gen2 support this natively. For S3 or other external sources, you may need an intermediate Event Hub or Logic App to bridge the event.

sections.title: Error Handling and Retry Logic Every production pipeline must answer the question: what happens when something goes wrong? Fabric pipelines provide multiple mechanisms for handling errors gracefully. Dependency Conditions for Error Paths The most important error-handling pattern uses the failure dependency condition. Connect a downstream activity to an upstream activity with a “Failed” condition, creating an explicit error path:

blocks.content:
Copy Activity
       │
       ├── (Succeeded) ──→ Notebook Activity
       │
       └── (Failed) ────→ Web Activity (send alert) ──→ Fail Activity

This pattern ensures that failures are detected, notifications are sent, and the pipeline run is explicitly marked as failed (so monitoring dashboards show the correct status).
Retry Policies
Every activity has a configurable retry policy:
•  Retry count: How many times to retry on failure (default: 0, max: 3). Each retry re-executes the activity from scratch.
•  Retry interval (seconds): How long to wait between retries. Use at least 30 seconds for transient failures (network timeouts, throttling).
Retries are appropriate for transient failures — network blips, temporary throttling, momentary source unavailability. They are not appropriate for persistent failures — schema mismatches, missing permissions, or logic errors. A retry policy of 2 retries with 30-second intervals covers most transient scenarios without wasting compute on persistent errors.
Timeout Settings
Every activity has a timeout (default: 12 hours for most activities, 7 days for the pipeline overall). Set realistic timeouts based on expected execution time:
•  A Copy Activity that normally completes in 5 minutes should have a timeout of 30 minutes, not 12 hours. A 12-hour timeout means a hung copy burns CUs for 12 hours before the pipeline notices.
•  A Notebook that normally runs for 2 hours should have a timeout of 4 hours. Generous enough for slow days, tight enough to catch runaway jobs.
The Fail Activity
The Fail Activity explicitly marks a pipeline run as “Failed” with a custom error message and error code. Without it, a pipeline that catches errors and handles them gracefully may report as “Succeeded” even though the underlying data process failed. Always end error paths with a Fail Activity.

// Fail Activity configuration
// Error message: @concat('Copy failed for table: ',
//     pipeline().parameters.source_table,
//     ' Error: ', activity('CopyData').error.message)
// Error code: COPY_FAILED

Warning: Without a Fail Activity at the end of your error path, the pipeline may report “Succeeded” because the error-handling activities themselves succeeded. This creates false positives in monitoring. Always terminate error paths with an explicit Fail.

sections.title: Monitoring and Debugging Pipelines A pipeline that runs in production without monitoring is a pipeline waiting to fail silently. Fabric provides multiple tools for observing pipeline behavior. The Monitoring Hub The Monitoring Hub is accessible from the left navigation rail in the Fabric portal. It shows all pipeline runs across all workspaces you have access to. For each run, you see: • Pipeline name, status (Succeeded, Failed, In Progress, Cancelled), and duration. • Trigger type (Manual, Scheduled, Event). • Start time and end time. • The ability to drill into individual activity runs within the pipeline. Activity-Level Drill-Down Clicking into a pipeline run shows the activity graph with real-time status for each activity. For each activity, you can see: • Input and output JSON — the exact parameters, connection details, and results. • Error details — the full error message and stack trace for failed activities. • Duration — how long the activity took. Useful for identifying bottlenecks. • Rows read and rows written — for Copy Activities, the data volume moved. The Gantt Chart View The Gantt chart shows activity execution as horizontal bars on a timeline. This is invaluable for: • Identifying parallelism — Which activities ran in parallel? Could more run in parallel? • Spotting bottlenecks — Which activity took the longest? Is it the copy, the notebook, or a wait between them? • Diagnosing queue delays — If there is a long gap between activities, the pipeline may be waiting for compute resources (capacity throttling). Common Debugging Scenarios

blocks.content: Symptom Likely Cause Diagnostic Step Copy Activity fails with timeout Source is slow, network issue, or DIU setting too low. Check Copy Activity duration history. Increase DIU count. Verify source availability. Notebook Activity fails Code error in the notebook (syntax, data issue, library missing). Click into the Notebook Activity output. Find the Spark error. Open the notebook and debug interactively. Pipeline succeeds but data is wrong Notebook logic produces incorrect results. No data quality check. Add a validation step: count rows, check for NULLs, verify date ranges after the notebook runs. Pipeline is slow but no single activity is slow Queue delays between activities. Capacity throttling. Check the Gantt chart for gaps between activities. Monitor the Capacity Metrics app for throttling events. Pipeline runs but trigger does not fire Trigger is not started, time zone mismatch, or event source not configured. Verify trigger status (started vs. stopped). Check time zone settings. Verify event source configuration.

sections.title: Apache Airflow Job in Fabric Fabric includes native support for Apache Airflow — the open-source workflow orchestration platform used by thousands of organizations. If your team already uses Airflow, you can run your existing DAGs inside Fabric without migrating to the native pipeline format. When to Choose Airflow vs. Native Pipelines Criterion Native Pipelines Apache Airflow Interface Visual drag-and-drop canvas Python code (DAG definitions) Learning curve Low — familiar to ADF users and no-code users Higher — requires Python and Airflow knowledge Version control Git integration via Fabric workspace Git-native (DAG files are Python code) Testing Limited — pipeline testing is manual Full Python unit testing frameworks Ecosystem Fabric-native connectors and activities 2000+ Airflow operators and community plugins Portability Fabric-only Multi-cloud (Airflow runs anywhere) Best for Teams new to orchestration, ADF-experienced teams, visual workflow design Teams with existing Airflow expertise, complex dependency graphs, multi-cloud orchestration

blocks.content:
Creating an Airflow Job
To use Airflow in Fabric:
•  Create an “Apache Airflow Job” item in your workspace.
•  Upload or author your DAG Python files in the Airflow environment.
•  Configure connections to Fabric items (Lakehouses, Warehouses) using Airflow connections.
•  Trigger the DAG manually or on a schedule through the Airflow scheduler.
Airflow in Fabric runs on managed infrastructure — you do not provision Airflow workers or manage the Airflow metadata database. Fabric handles this, just as it handles the compute for native pipelines.
Hybrid Approach
Some organizations use both: native pipelines for simple Copy Activity workflows (where the visual canvas is faster) and Airflow for complex multi-step orchestration (where code-defined DAGs provide better testing and version control). There is no conflict — both run within the same Fabric workspace and can reference the same Lakehouses and Notebooks.

sections.title: Module 4 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: Data Factory in Fabric vs. ADF — what migrated (activities, expressions, parameters), what changed (no linked services, no integration runtimes, OneLake-native), and what’s new (Airflow, Dataflows Gen2, tighter Notebook integration)
Pipeline anatomy — activities (data movement, transformation, control flow, utility), dependency links (succeeded, failed, completed, skipped), and the activity graph
Copy Activity — source/sink configuration, column mapping, performance tuning (DIUs, parallelism), and the choice between Files/ and Tables/ destinations
Parameterization — pipeline parameters, system variables (@pipeline().RunId, @utcnow()), and dynamic content expressions for building flexible, reusable pipelines
Notebook orchestration — calling notebooks from pipelines, passing parameters with getParam(), capturing return values with exit(), and choosing notebooks vs. Spark Job Definitions
Triggers — scheduled (CRON), tumbling window (time-partitioned with backfill), and event-based (file arrival). Time zone configuration and trigger lifecycle.
Error handling — failure dependency paths, retry policies (count and interval), timeout settings, and the Fail Activity for explicit failure marking
Monitoring and debugging — the Monitoring Hub, activity-level drill-down, Gantt chart, and common debugging scenarios
Apache Airflow — native Airflow support in Fabric, when to choose Airflow vs. native pipelines, and the hybrid approach

blocks.content: Build an End-to-End Ingestion Pipeline Before moving to Module 5, build this complete pipeline:

items: Create a pipeline named pl_ingest_orders.
Add a pipeline parameter: load_date (String, default: today’s date).

Add a Copy Activity that reads from a sample data source (use a public CSV URL or an ADLS Gen2 container) and writes to your Lakehouse’s Files/raw/orders/{load_date}/ path. Use dynamic content to build the folder path from the parameter.

Add a Notebook Activity that depends on the Copy Activity (Succeeded condition). Pass the load_date parameter to the notebook. The notebook should read the raw CSV, transform it, and write a Delta table to Tables/.
Add an error path: if the Copy Activity fails, add a Web Activity that logs the error (or a Set Variable activity), followed by a Fail Activity.
Create a scheduled trigger that runs the pipeline daily at 02:00 AM UTC.
Run the pipeline manually. Monitor it in the Monitoring Hub. Drill into each activity. Check the Gantt chart.
Intentionally break something (e.g., use an invalid source URL) and verify that the error path fires correctly.

blocks.content: You are not just moving data.

You are building automated, self-healing orchestration that runs without human intervention, handles failures gracefully, and scales from one table to hundreds through parameterization. This is the difference between a script and a platform.
In Module 5, we cover Data Factory — Dataflows Gen2 — the low-code, Power Query-based transformation layer that complements pipelines for analyst-friendly data preparation.

chapters.title: Dataflows Gen2 — Power Query Online
chapters.description: Low-Code Transformation at Scale Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Build data transformations with Dataflows Gen2 using the Power Query Online editor. Master connectors, transformation steps, the M language, data destinations, staging, and how Dataflows Gen2 fits alongside Notebooks and Pipelines.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 5 Module 4 taught you to orchestrate with Pipelines — the automation layer. This module introduces the second half of Data Factory: Dataflows Gen2, the transformation layer for people who think in spreadsheets rather than code. Not every data professional writes PySpark. Many analysts, BI developers, and citizen data engineers are fluent in Power Query — the same transformation engine used in Power BI Desktop and Excel. Dataflows Gen2 brings that engine to Fabric, running at cloud scale and writing directly to Lakehouses and Warehouses. Think of it this way: a Notebook is a code-first transformation tool (Module 6–8 will go deep). A Dataflow Gen2 is a visual-first transformation tool that uses the Power Query editor to connect to data, apply transformation steps, and write the result to a Fabric destination — all without writing a single line of PySpark. That does not mean Dataflows Gen2 are limited. They support 300+ data connectors, hundreds of built-in transformations, a full scripting language (M), staging through a Spark compute engine, and direct output to Lakehouse tables, Warehouse tables, or KQL databases. They are a serious tool for serious data work. This module covers: what Dataflows Gen2 are and how they differ from Gen1, the Power Query Online editor, connectors and data sources, transformations (visual and M language), data destinations, staging and performance, incremental refresh, orchestration from Pipelines, when to use Dataflows Gen2 vs. Notebooks, and a hands-on checkpoint.

sections.title: What Are Dataflows Gen2? A Dataflow Gen2 is a Fabric item that encapsulates a Power Query transformation with a data destination. You define: where to read data from (the source), how to transform it (the Power Query steps), and where to write the result (the destination). When the dataflow runs, it connects to the source, applies every step in order, and writes the output. Gen1 vs. Gen2 If you have used Power BI dataflows (sometimes called Gen1), Dataflows Gen2 are the evolution:

blocks.content: Feature Dataflows Gen1 (Power BI) Dataflows Gen2 (Fabric) Execution engine Power Query mashup engine only Power Query + Spark staging engine Output destination Power BI dataset (import) or Azure Data Lake Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL Database, or Azure targets Performance at scale Limited — single-node mashup engine High — Spark engine for large datasets when staging enabled Scheduling Power BI refresh schedule Pipeline orchestration or standalone schedule M language support Full Full Environment Power BI Service Fabric workspace (any workload) Incremental refresh Basic Advanced, with configurable watermark columns

The critical upgrade is the Spark staging engine. When staging is enabled, Dataflows Gen2 offload heavy transformations to Fabric’s Spark cluster instead of running everything on the mashup engine. This dramatically improves performance for large datasets (millions of rows) and enables transformations that would time out on Gen1. Where Dataflows Gen2 Sit in the Architecture

Data Sources (SQL, REST, files, SaaS apps, ...)
       │
       v
  Dataflow Gen2  (Power Query Online)
       │  ── Connect to source
       │  ── Apply transformation steps
       │  ── Write to destination
       v
  Lakehouse Tables/ | Warehouse | KQL Database
       │
       v
  SQL Analytics Endpoint | Power BI | Notebooks

Dataflows Gen2 are a transformation layer that sits between data sources and Fabric storage. They complement Pipelines (which handle orchestration and data movement) and Notebooks (which handle code-based transformation). Many production platforms use all three: Pipelines for orchestration, Dataflows Gen2 for analyst-friendly transformations, and Notebooks for complex Spark-based processing.

sections.title: Connectors and Data Sources Dataflows Gen2 support 300+ built-in connectors. This is one of their greatest strengths — you can pull data from virtually any source without writing connector code. Connector Categories Category Examples Notes Databases SQL Server, Azure SQL, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, Snowflake, Databricks Full SQL pushdown for filters and joins when supported by the source. Files CSV, Excel, JSON, XML, Parquet, PDF Read from OneLake, ADLS Gen2, SharePoint, local upload, or HTTP endpoints. Cloud services SharePoint Lists, Dynamics 365, Salesforce, Google Analytics, SAP, HubSpot Pre-built connectors with authentication wizards. Web and APIs Web API (REST/OData), Blank Query (custom M) For any REST API. Parse JSON responses with built-in M functions. Fabric items Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL Database, Power BI dataset Query other Fabric items directly. Useful for cross-workspace transformations. Azure services Azure Blob, ADLS Gen2, Cosmos DB, Azure Data Explorer Native Azure integration with managed identity support.

blocks.content:
Connection Credentials
When you connect to a data source, you provide credentials that are stored as a Fabric connection. Connections are reusable across dataflows and can be managed centrally by admins. Supported authentication methods depend on the connector:
•  Anonymous — for public APIs and open data.
•  Basic (username/password) — for databases and legacy systems.
•  OAuth2 — for cloud services (Salesforce, Dynamics, Google).
•  Service Principal — for Azure services (ADLS Gen2, Azure SQL).
•  Organizational Account — for Microsoft 365 services (SharePoint, OneDrive).
•  Key / SAS Token — for Azure storage accounts.
Query Folding
Query folding is the most important performance concept in Power Query. When a transformation can be pushed down to the source (i.e., translated into the source’s native query language like SQL), it is “folded.” Folded steps execute on the source server, which is almost always faster than pulling raw data into the mashup engine and transforming it there.
For example, if your source is an Azure SQL Database and you apply a Filter Rows step, Power Query translates the filter into a SQL WHERE clause. The database server applies the filter, and only the matching rows are transferred to the dataflow. This can reduce data transfer by orders of magnitude.
Not all steps fold. Steps that fold include: filter rows, remove columns, rename columns, change types, sort, group by, merge (join), and append. Steps that typically do not fold include: pivot, unpivot, custom M functions, and most text transformations.
Tip: Check folding status by right-clicking a step and selecting “View Native Query.” If the option is available and shows a SQL query, the step folds. If the option is grayed out, the step does not fold, and all subsequent steps also do not fold. Structure your transformations so that foldable steps come first.

sections.title: Transformations — The Visual Toolkit The Power Query editor provides a rich library of visual transformations accessible through the ribbon, right-click menus, and the Add Column tab. These transformations cover the vast majority of data preparation scenarios without writing code. Core Transformations Transformation What It Does When to Use Filter Rows Keep or remove rows based on conditions (equals, contains, greater than, etc.). Remove nulls, filter date ranges, exclude test data. Remove Columns Drop columns you do not need. Reduce data volume. Remove sensitive columns before writing to destination. Rename Columns Change column names. Align with naming conventions (snake_case, business-friendly names). Change Type Set column data types (text, number, date, boolean, etc.). Ensure correct types for downstream analytics. Fix auto-detected type errors. Replace Values Substitute one value for another within a column. Standardize codes, fix misspellings, replace nulls with defaults. Split Column Split one column into multiple (by delimiter, position, or pattern). Parse combined fields (e.g., 'City, State' into two columns). Merge Columns Combine multiple columns into one. Create composite keys, build display names. Add Custom Column Create a new column using an M expression. Calculated fields, conditional logic, date arithmetic. Group By Aggregate rows (sum, count, average, min, max) by grouping columns. Summarize transactional data into analytical aggregates. Pivot / Unpivot Rotate rows to columns (pivot) or columns to rows (unpivot). Reshape cross-tab reports into normalized form or vice versa. Merge Queries (Join) Join two queries on matching columns (inner, left outer, right outer, full outer, anti). Enrich fact data with dimension attributes. Lookup reference data. Append Queries Stack rows from two or more queries with matching schemas. Combine monthly files into a single table. Union multiple sources.

blocks.content: For more complex logic (nested conditions, calculations, text parsing), use the Add Custom Column feature with a direct M expression.

sections.title: The M Language Every Power Query transformation is powered by M (Power Query Formula Language) — a functional, case-sensitive, expression-based language designed for data transformation. When you click buttons in the visual editor, M code is generated behind the scenes. Understanding M gives you access to capabilities that the visual editor does not expose. M Fundamentals An M query is structured as a let...in expression:

blocks.content: let // Step 1: Connect to source Source = Sql.Database("server.database.windows.net", "sales_db"),
// Step 2: Navigate to table orders_Table = Source{[Schema="dbo", Item="orders"]}[Data],
// Step 3: Filter rows Filtered = Table.SelectRows(orders_Table, each [status] = "active"),
// Step 4: Remove columns Removed = Table.RemoveColumns(Filtered, {"internal_notes", "temp_flag"}),
// Step 5: Change types Typed = Table.TransformColumnTypes(Removed, { {"order_date", type date}, {"amount", type number} }) in Typed

Each step is a variable assignment. Each step references the previous step by name. The in clause specifies which step’s result is the output. This linear, step-by-step structure maps directly to the Applied Steps pane in the visual editor.
Key M Functions
Function
Purpose
Example
Table.SelectRows
Filter rows
Table.SelectRows(t, each [status] = "active")
Table.RemoveColumns
Drop columns
Table.RemoveColumns(t, {"col1", "col2"})
Table.RenameColumns
Rename columns
Table.RenameColumns(t, {{"old", "new"}})
Table.AddColumn
Add calculated column
Table.AddColumn(t, "new_col", each [a] + [b])
Table.TransformColumnTypes
Set data types
Table.TransformColumnTypes(t, {{"col", type text}})
Table.Group
Aggregate by group
Table.Group(t, {"region"}, {{"total", each List.Sum([amount])}})
Table.NestedJoin
Join two tables
Table.NestedJoin(t1, "id", t2, "id", "joined", JoinKind.LeftOuter)
Table.Combine
Append tables
Table.Combine({table1, table2})
Table.Pivot
Pivot rows to columns
Table.Pivot(t, values, "pivot_col", "value_col")
Table.Unpivot
Unpivot columns to rows
Table.UnpivotOtherColumns(t, {"key"}, "attr", "val")

When to Write M Directly
Use the visual editor for standard transformations. Drop into M when you need:
•  Dynamic table references or parameterized sources.
•  Custom error handling (try...otherwise expressions).
•  Complex text parsing with Text.Split, Text.BetweenDelimiters, Text.Contains.
•  List generation and iteration (List.Generate, List.Transform).
•  Calling web APIs with pagination logic (Web.Contents with RelativePath and Query options).
•  Reusable helper functions (e.g., a date parser used across multiple queries).
Important: M is case-sensitive. Table.SelectRows is correct; table.selectrows will fail. Column names are referenced in square brackets: [column_name]. Every M expression must return a value — there are no side effects.

sections.title: Data Destinations A Dataflow Gen2 without a data destination is a transformation that goes nowhere. The data destination is where the output of each query lands. This is the key upgrade from Gen1 — Dataflows Gen2 write directly to Fabric storage. Supported Destinations Destination Format Write Modes Notes Lakehouse Table Delta Replace, Append Most common destination. Data lands as a managed Delta table in the Lakehouse Tables/ section. Warehouse Table Delta (via T-SQL) Replace, Append Writes through the Warehouse’s T-SQL interface. Good for teams using the Warehouse as the Gold layer. KQL Database KQL-native Append Writes to Real-Time Intelligence. Useful for enriched event data. Azure SQL Database SQL Server Replace, Append Write back to an external Azure SQL database. Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 Parquet / CSV Replace Write files to an external ADLS Gen2 container.

blocks.content:
Configuring a Destination
For each query in the dataflow, you configure a destination independently:
•  Select the query in the Queries pane.
•  In the bottom-right corner, click “Add data destination” (or “Choose destination”).
•  Select the destination type (Lakehouse, Warehouse, etc.).
•  Navigate to the specific item and table.
•  Configure column mapping — match source columns to destination columns. Auto-mapping works for matching names; manual mapping handles renaming.
•  Choose the update method: Replace (overwrite the entire table) or Append (add new rows to existing data).
Each query can target a different destination. A single dataflow might write one query to a Bronze Lakehouse table and another query (a cleaned version) to a Gold Lakehouse table.
Replace vs. Append
Replace overwrites the entire destination table on every run. Simple and predictable, but inappropriate for large tables where you only want to add new data. Use Replace for dimension tables, lookup tables, and small reference data that is refreshed in full.
Append adds new rows to the destination table without touching existing rows. Use Append for fact tables and event logs where data accumulates over time. Be careful — without deduplication logic, running the same Append dataflow twice produces duplicate rows.
Warning: Append mode does not handle deduplication. If you re-run a dataflow that was already successful, it will insert the same rows again. For idempotent ingestion, use Replace mode, or combine Append with incremental refresh (covered in Part 8) to process only new data.

sections.title: Staging and Performance Performance is the most common concern with Dataflows Gen2, especially for datasets with millions of rows. Understanding the staging mechanism is the key to fast dataflows. The Two Execution Paths Dataflows Gen2 can execute transformations through two paths: • Mashup engine only (staging disabled): All transformations run on the Power Query mashup engine — a single-node process. Fast for small datasets (thousands to low hundreds of thousands of rows), but it becomes slow for large datasets because it cannot parallelize work. • Mashup engine + Spark staging (staging enabled): The mashup engine handles source connection and initial extraction. Large datasets are then staged (temporarily written) to OneLake as Parquet/CSV files, and subsequent transformations run on Fabric’s Spark engine. This enables parallel processing across multiple nodes, dramatically improving performance for millions of rows. How to Enable Staging Staging is controlled at the query level: • Select a query in the Queries pane. • In the query settings (right pane), find the “Staging” option. • Toggle it on. When staging is enabled, the dataflow writes intermediate data to a staging Lakehouse (automatically provisioned in your workspace). The Spark engine reads from staging and applies the remaining transformations before writing to the final destination. When to Enable Staging

blocks.content: Scenario Staging Why Small dataset (<100K rows), simple transforms Off Mashup engine is fast enough. Staging adds overhead (data serialization and Spark startup). Large dataset (>100K rows) On Spark parallelism is essential for acceptable performance. Complex joins between large tables On Spark handles distributed joins far better than the single-node mashup engine. Query folds completely to source Off If all transforms push down to the database, there is nothing for Spark to do. Staging adds unnecessary overhead. Dataflow takes >10 minutes without staging On The time savings from Spark will almost certainly outweigh the staging overhead.
Performance Best Practices

items: Maximize query folding. Put all foldable steps first (filters, column removal, type changes) before unfoldable steps. This minimizes the data volume entering the mashup engine.
Enable staging for any query that processes more than 100,000 rows.
Reduce column count early. Remove unnecessary columns as one of the first steps to reduce data transfer and memory usage.
Avoid unnecessary sorting. Sorting is expensive and rarely needed during transformation. Let the destination engine handle sorting at query time.
Use parameters for incremental loads (covered next) to avoid processing the entire dataset on every run.

Monitor dataflow refresh duration in the workspace’s Monitoring Hub. If a dataflow consistently takes more than 30 minutes, investigate whether staging, folding, or a Notebook might be more efficient.

sections.title: Incremental Refresh Without incremental refresh, every dataflow run processes the entire source dataset from scratch. For a table with 50 million rows, that means reading, transforming, and writing all 50 million rows every day — even if only 10,000 rows are new. Incremental refresh solves this by processing only new or changed data. How Incremental Refresh Works Incremental refresh in Dataflows Gen2 uses a watermark column — typically a date or timestamp column that indicates when a row was created or last modified. The dataflow tracks the highest watermark value from the previous run and uses it to filter the source query on the next run.

blocks.content: Run 1: Read all rows (initial full load) Watermark saved: 2026-01-15 23:59:59
Run 2: Read only rows WHERE modified_date > '2026-01-15 23:59:59' Watermark saved: 2026-01-16 14:30:00
Run 3: Read only rows WHERE modified_date > '2026-01-16 14:30:00' Watermark saved: 2026-01-17 09:15:00

Configuring Incremental Refresh
•  In the data destination settings, enable “Incremental refresh.”
•  Select the watermark column (e.g., modified_date, updated_at, or a row version column).
•  Choose the comparison operator (greater than, greater than or equal to).
•  The destination update method should be set to “Append.”
On the first run, the dataflow performs a full load. On subsequent runs, it filters the source to only read rows with a watermark value greater than the last saved watermark. The new rows are appended to the destination table.
Incremental Refresh Limitations
•  The source must have a reliable watermark column. If rows are updated without changing the watermark, the dataflow will miss the changes.
•  Incremental refresh handles inserts well but does not handle updates or deletes. If a previously ingested row is modified at the source, the dataflow will not update it in the destination (unless the watermark column changed). For update/delete scenarios, use a Notebook with MERGE logic instead.
•  The watermark is per-query, not per-destination. If a single query targets multiple destinations, they share the same watermark.
Important: Incremental refresh is ideal for append-only fact tables (orders, events, transactions) where new rows arrive with increasing timestamps. It is not suitable for slowly changing dimensions where existing rows are updated. For those, use a full Replace or a Notebook with MERGE.

sections.title: Dataflows Gen2 vs. Notebooks — When to Use Which This is the practical question every Fabric team faces: should we transform data with Dataflows Gen2 or with Spark Notebooks? The answer depends on the team, the data, and the complexity of the transformation. Comparison Criterion Dataflows Gen2 Spark Notebooks Interface Visual (Power Query editor) Code (PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, R) Primary persona Analysts, BI developers, citizen engineers Data engineers, data scientists Learning curve Low — Power Query is familiar to Excel/Power BI users Higher — requires Python/Spark knowledge Transformation complexity Low to medium — filters, joins, pivots, basic logic Unlimited — any logic expressible in code Data volume sweet spot Thousands to low millions of rows Millions to billions of rows Performance ceiling Limited by mashup engine; staging helps but adds overhead Near-unlimited with Spark distributed compute Merge/Upsert support No native MERGE — only Replace or Append Full MERGE support via Delta Lake Testing Manual preview in editor Unit tests, assertions, CI/CD integration Version control Limited (JSON-based, not human-readable diffs) Full Git integration (Python code diffs cleanly) Scheduling Standalone schedule or Pipeline orchestration Pipeline orchestration

blocks.content:
Decision Framework
Choose Dataflows Gen2 when: your team knows Power Query, the transformation is straightforward (filter, join, reshape, aggregate), the dataset is under a few million rows, and you want the fastest path from source to Lakehouse table without writing code.
Choose Notebooks when: the transformation requires complex business logic, MERGE/upsert operations, custom libraries, data quality frameworks, ML feature engineering, or processing billions of rows. Also choose Notebooks when version control and automated testing are requirements.
Use both when: different transformations in the same pipeline have different complexity levels. For example, a Pipeline might use a Dataflow Gen2 to ingest and clean a small reference table, then a Notebook to process a large fact table with MERGE logic. This is the hybrid approach.
Orchestrating Dataflows Gen2 from Pipelines
Dataflows Gen2 can be called as activities within a Pipeline, just like Notebooks:
•  Drag a “Dataflow” Activity onto the pipeline canvas.
•  Select the Dataflow Gen2 to execute.
•  Connect it to other activities with dependency links.
This enables workflows like: Copy Activity (land raw data) → Dataflow Gen2 (transform and write to table) → Notebook (run advanced MERGE logic). The Pipeline orchestrates the entire chain.

sections.title: Module 5 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: What Dataflows Gen2 are — Power Query transformations with data destinations, the evolution of Gen1 with Spark staging and Fabric-native output
The Power Query Online editor — queries pane, diagram view, data preview, applied steps, and the formula bar
Connectors and data sources — 300+ connectors, authentication methods, and the critical concept of query folding for performance
Visual transformations — filter, remove, rename, type, replace, split, merge, group, pivot/unpivot, join, append, conditional columns, and date extractions
The M language — let...in structure, key functions (Table.SelectRows, Table.AddColumn, Table.Group, Table.NestedJoin), and when to write M directly
Data destinations — Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL Database, Azure SQL, ADLS Gen2; Replace vs. Append modes; per-query destination configuration
Staging and performance — mashup engine vs. Spark staging, when to enable staging, folding-first strategy, and performance best practices
Incremental refresh — watermark columns, append-only pattern, limitations for updates/deletes, and when to prefer Notebook MERGE
Dataflows Gen2 vs. Notebooks — decision framework by persona, complexity, volume, and the hybrid Pipeline approach

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Before moving to Module 6, complete these exercises:

items: Create a Dataflow Gen2 in your workspace. Connect to a sample data source (e.g., a public REST API, a CSV file in OneLake, or an Azure SQL database).
Apply at least five transformations: filter rows, remove columns, rename columns, change types, and add a custom calculated column.
Open the Advanced Editor and inspect the generated M code. Modify one step directly in M and verify the result in the data preview.
Set the data destination to a Lakehouse table. Choose Replace mode. Publish and run the dataflow. Verify the table appears in the Lakehouse and is queryable from the SQL analytics endpoint.
Enable staging on the query. Re-run the dataflow and compare the execution time with staging on vs. off.
Create a Pipeline that orchestrates the Dataflow Gen2: add a Dataflow Activity, connect it to a Set Variable activity that logs the completion, and run the pipeline from the Monitoring Hub.

blocks.content: You are not just cleaning data.

You are building a visual transformation layer that empowers analysts and BI developers to prepare data without code, at cloud scale, with direct output to the Fabric storage layer. Combined with Pipelines and Notebooks, you now have the complete ingestion and transformation toolkit.

In Module 6, we shift gears to the Spark Engine and Notebooks — the code-first transformation powerhouse. You will learn how Spark works inside Fabric, how to configure Spark sessions, and how to write your first PySpark transformations.

chapters.title: Spark Engine, Notebooks, and Environments
chapters.description: The Code-First Compute Layer Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Understand Apache Spark inside Fabric — architecture, session configuration, the Notebook experience, multi-language support, Environments for library management, and notebookutils for production workflows.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 6 You have now seen every way data enters Fabric: Pipelines move it, Dataflows Gen2 transform it visually, and the Lakehouse stores it. Starting with this module, we shift from ingestion to computation. Apache Spark is the distributed compute engine that powers all code-based processing in Fabric. Every PySpark transformation you write, every Spark SQL query you execute, every Scala or R script you run in a Notebook — all of it runs on Spark. Spark is not an optional add-on. It is the engine that turns OneLake data into insight. In Fabric, Spark is fully managed and serverless. You do not provision clusters. You do not install software. You do not configure YARN or tune memory allocators. You open a Notebook, attach it to a Lakehouse, and run code. Fabric allocates the compute, manages the cluster lifecycle, and releases the resources when you are done. This module covers: how Spark works inside Fabric (architecture, sessions, pools), the Notebook experience in detail (cells, output, state management), multi-language support (Python, Spark SQL, Scala, R), Environments for managing libraries and Spark configuration, notebookutils for production patterns, the Spark UI for debugging, and performance fundamentals.

sections.title: Apache Spark Inside Fabric — Architecture Apache Spark is a distributed computing framework designed for large-scale data processing. It splits work across multiple machines (nodes), processes partitions of data in parallel, and aggregates the results. This parallelism is why Spark can process billions of rows in minutes — something a single-machine tool like pandas cannot do. Spark’s Logical Architecture

blocks.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    DRIVER                          │
  │  SparkSession  │  Query Plan  │  DAG Scheduler    │
  └─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                    │                 │
          ┌────────┴──────┐  ┌──────┴─────────┐
          │   EXECUTOR 1   │  │   EXECUTOR 2    │
          │  Tasks (cores)  │  │  Tasks (cores)   │
          │  Cache (RAM)    │  │  Cache (RAM)     │
          └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘
                    │                 │
                    v                 v
          ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
          │           OneLake              │
          │  (Delta tables, Parquet files)  │
          └──────────────────────────────────┘

Driver: The control node. It holds your SparkSession, parses your code, creates the query execution plan, and coordinates work across executors. In a Fabric Notebook, the driver runs when your session starts.
Executors: The worker nodes. Each executor has multiple cores (for parallel tasks) and memory (for caching data). Executors read data partitions from OneLake, process them in parallel, and return results to the driver.
Tasks: The smallest unit of work. Each task processes one partition of data on one core of one executor. If your DataFrame has 200 partitions and your cluster has 40 cores total, Spark will run 40 tasks in parallel and cycle through all 200 partitions in five waves.
Understanding this architecture matters because performance tuning in Spark is fundamentally about balancing partitions, cores, and memory. Too few partitions means not enough parallelism. Too many partitions means excessive overhead. The right balance depends on your data size and cluster configuration.
How Fabric Manages Spark
In traditional Spark deployments (Databricks, EMR, HDInsight), you provision and manage clusters explicitly. In Fabric, Spark is serverless:
•  No cluster provisioning. When you run a Notebook cell, Fabric allocates a Spark session from the capacity’s compute pool. Executors are assigned from available resources.
•  Starter pools for fast startup. Fabric maintains pre-warmed “starter pools” — reserved compute that is ready to serve Spark sessions within seconds. Without starter pools, session startup could take 1–3 minutes for cold allocation.
•  Automatic shutdown. Idle sessions are terminated after a configurable timeout (default: 20 minutes). This releases compute back to the capacity pool.
•  CU-based billing. Spark compute consumes Capacity Units (CUs) from your Fabric capacity. There is no separate Spark billing. The CU consumption depends on the number of vCores allocated and the session duration.
Important: Spark sessions in Fabric compete for CUs with all other workloads (SQL, Power BI, Pipelines). If your capacity is heavily loaded, Spark session startup may be delayed or throttled. Monitor the Capacity Metrics app to understand utilization patterns.

sections.title: Spark Session Configuration Every time you run code in a Notebook, a Spark session is created. The session configuration determines how much compute is available and how Spark behaves. Understanding session configuration is essential for both performance and cost control. Session-Level Configuration You can configure the Spark session from the Notebook’s toolbar (the “Session configuration” button) or programmatically at the top of your notebook:

blocks.content:
# Programmatic session configuration (must run before any Spark code)
%%configure
{
    "conf": {
        "spark.sql.shuffle.partitions": "200",
        "spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes": "128m",
        "spark.sql.adaptive.enabled": "true"
    },
    "driverMemory": "28g",
    "driverCores": 4,
    "executorMemory": "28g",
    "executorCores": 4,
    "numExecutors": 2
}

Key Configuration Parameters Parameter Default What It Controls executorCores 4 Number of parallel tasks per executor. Higher values improve parallelism but consume more CUs. executorMemory 28g RAM per executor. Increase for memory-intensive operations (large joins, caching). numExecutors 2 Number of executor nodes. More executors = more parallelism and CU consumption. driverMemory 28g RAM for the driver node. Increase if the driver collects large result sets (.collect(), .toPandas()). spark.sql.shuffle.partitions 200 Number of partitions after a shuffle (joins, group by). Tune to match data volume. spark.sql.adaptive.enabled true Adaptive Query Execution: dynamically adjusts shuffle partitions at runtime. Leave enabled. spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes 128m Target size for each partition when reading files. Controls read parallelism.

Starter Pools and Custom Pools Starter pools are Fabric’s default compute allocation. When you start a session without custom configuration, Fabric uses a starter pool with pre-configured resources. Starter pools are optimized for fast startup (∼10–20 seconds) and general-purpose workloads. Custom Spark pools can be configured at the workspace level by Fabric admins. Custom pools let you define the node size (Small, Medium, Large, X-Large), the number of nodes, and auto-scaling rules. They are useful for teams that need consistent, dedicated compute rather than sharing the default pool. Tip: For development and exploration, starter pools are ideal — fast to start, no configuration needed. For production workloads with predictable resource needs, consider a custom pool to guarantee consistent performance.

sections.title: The Notebook Experience Notebooks are Fabric’s interactive development environment for Spark. If you have used Jupyter notebooks, Databricks notebooks, or Synapse notebooks, the Fabric Notebook will feel familiar. But it has Fabric-specific capabilities that are worth understanding in detail. Notebook Structure A Fabric Notebook is a sequence of cells. Each cell is either a code cell (executable) or a Markdown cell (documentation). Cells execute sequentially — the output of one cell is available to subsequent cells because they share the same Spark session and Python/Scala/R kernel. • Code cells contain PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, or R code. They execute against the attached Spark session. Output (tables, charts, text) renders directly below the cell. • Markdown cells contain formatted text, headers, lists, links, and images. They are documentation — they do not execute code. Use them liberally to explain what each section of your notebook does and why. Attaching a Lakehouse Every Notebook can be attached to a default Lakehouse. When attached: • Relative file paths resolve to the Lakehouse’s OneLake folder (e.g., ‘Files/raw/’ maps to the Lakehouse’s Files/ section). • Tables/ is accessible via Spark SQL table names (e.g., spark.sql('SELECT * FROM orders') reads from Tables/orders). • The Lakehouse explorer panel on the left shows the attached Lakehouse’s files and tables for easy browsing. You can attach additional Lakehouses and switch between them, or reference other Lakehouses using full abfss:// paths. But the default Lakehouse determines the implicit context for relative paths. Multi-Language Support Fabric Notebooks support four languages, switchable per cell using magic commands:

blocks.content: Magic Command Language Use Case %%pyspark (default) Python (PySpark) Primary language for data engineering. DataFrame API, pandas integration, ML libraries. %%sql Spark SQL SQL-first analysis. SELECT, CREATE TABLE, INSERT, MERGE. Ideal for SQL-fluent users. %%scala Scala Native Spark language. Used for performance-critical code and advanced Spark APIs. %%r R (SparkR) Statistical analysis, R-native data frames. Used by data scientists with R backgrounds.

You can mix languages within a single notebook. A common pattern: use %%pyspark for data engineering, switch to %%sql for ad-hoc queries, and use %%pyspark again for visualization. Variables defined in one language cell are not directly accessible in another language cell, but you can use temporary views as a bridge:

# PySpark cell: create a temp view
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/orders')
df.createOrReplaceTempView('v_orders')
%%sql -- SQL cell: query the temp view created in PySpark SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total FROM v_orders GROUP BY region ORDER BY total DESC

Output and Visualization
Spark DataFrames displayed with df.show() render as plain text. For richer output, Fabric Notebooks provide:
•  Built-in table rendering — Use display(df) to render DataFrames as interactive, sortable, filterable tables in the notebook output.
•  Built-in chart rendering — The display() output includes a chart icon that lets you create bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, and pie charts directly from the DataFrame. No library needed.
•  Pandas integration — Convert small DataFrames to pandas for matplotlib, seaborn, or plotly visualizations: pdf = df.toPandas().
Warning: df.toPandas() collects the entire DataFrame to the driver node’s memory. For large DataFrames (millions of rows), this will crash the driver with an OutOfMemoryError. Only use toPandas() on aggregated or filtered data that fits in driver memory (typically < 1 GB).

sections.title: Environments — Library and Configuration Management An Environment is a Fabric item that packages Python/R libraries and Spark configuration into a reusable, version-controlled unit. Instead of installing libraries at the top of every notebook (pip install ...), you create an Environment, install libraries there, and attach it to your notebooks or workspaces. Why Environments Matter Without Environments, library management is chaotic: • Every notebook installs its own libraries, leading to version conflicts across notebooks. • Library installation runs at the start of every session, adding minutes to startup time. • There is no guarantee that two notebooks use the same library version, causing subtle bugs. • Production notebooks depend on libraries that were installed interactively and may not be reproducible. Environments solve all of these problems by centralizing library management. Creating and Configuring an Environment • Navigate to your workspace and click “+ New item” → “Environment.” • Name the Environment (e.g., env_data_engineering, env_ml_production). • In the Environment editor, add libraries from the Public Libraries tab (PyPI packages) or upload custom .whl / .tar.gz files. • Optionally, configure Spark properties (key-value pairs) that apply to any session using this Environment. • Click “Publish” to build the Environment. Publishing installs all libraries and makes the Environment available.

blocks.content:
# Example: Libraries configured in an Environment
# Public Libraries (from PyPI):
#   - great_expectations==0.18.12
#   - delta-spark==3.1.0
#   - mlflow==2.11.0
#   - pyarrow==15.0.0

# Spark Properties:
#   spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 400
#   spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled = true

Attaching Environments to Notebooks
You can attach an Environment to a Notebook from the Notebook’s toolbar (the Environment dropdown). When attached:
•  All libraries defined in the Environment are pre-installed and available for import — no pip install needed.
•  Spark properties from the Environment are applied to the session automatically.
•  If the Notebook also has inline %%configure settings, the notebook settings override the Environment settings where they conflict.
Workspace-Level Default Environment
Admins can set a default Environment at the workspace level. All notebooks in the workspace that do not specify their own Environment will use the workspace default. This ensures consistent library versions across the team without per-notebook configuration.
Important: Publishing an Environment can take several minutes because Fabric builds a container image with all specified libraries. Plan for this when adding or updating libraries. Do not update production Environments during active pipeline runs.

sections.title: notebookutils — The Production Toolkit notebookutils is a built-in Python library available in every Fabric Notebook. It provides utility functions for file operations, secrets, sessions, and notebook orchestration. These are the functions that turn an exploratory notebook into a production-grade component. File Operations (notebookutils.fs) The fs module lets you interact with OneLake files without using Spark’s file APIs:

blocks.content:
# List files in a directory
files = notebookutils.fs.ls('Files/raw/orders/')
for f in files:
    print(f.name, f.size)

# Copy a file
notebookutils.fs.cp(
    'Files/raw/orders/2026-01-15/',
    'Files/archive/orders/2026-01-15/',
    recurse=True
)

# Delete a file or directory
notebookutils.fs.rm('Files/staging/temp/', recurse=True)

# Check if a path exists
exists = notebookutils.fs.exists('Files/raw/orders/2026-01-15/')
Secrets (notebookutils.credentials) Access Azure Key Vault secrets without hard-coding credentials:

# Retrieve a secret from Key Vault
secret = notebookutils.credentials.getSecret(
    'https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/',
    'my-api-key'
)

# Use in a connection
import requests
response = requests.get(url, headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {secret}'})
Notebook Orchestration (notebookutils.notebook) Run notebooks from other notebooks, pass parameters, and capture return values:

# Run another notebook with parameters
result = notebookutils.notebook.run(
    'nb_transform_orders',
    timeout_seconds=600,
    arguments={'load_date': '2026-01-15', 'mode': 'incremental'}
)
print(f'Child notebook returned: {result}')

# Run multiple notebooks in parallel
notebookutils.notebook.runMultiple([
    {'name': 'nb_transform_orders', 'args': {'load_date': '2026-01-15'}},
    {'name': 'nb_transform_customers', 'args': {'load_date': '2026-01-15'}},
    {'name': 'nb_transform_products', 'args': {'load_date': '2026-01-15'}},
])

runMultiple is a powerful pattern for parallelizing independent transformations. Instead of running three notebooks sequentially (taking 3x the time), you run them in parallel, limited only by available compute. Exit Values (notebookutils.notebook.exit) Return a value from the current notebook to its caller (Pipeline or parent notebook):

# At the end of the notebook
row_count = df.count()
notebookutils.notebook.exit(str(row_count))

The exit value must be a string. The calling Pipeline can access it via @activity('NotebookActivity').output.result.exitValue. The calling parent notebook receives it as the return value of notebookutils.notebook.run(). Parameters (notebookutils.notebook.getParam)

# Receive parameters from Pipeline or parent notebook
load_date = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('load_date', '2026-01-01')
mode = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('mode', 'full')
print(f'Running for {load_date} in {mode} mode')
Important: Always provide a default value as the second argument to getParam(). This lets you run the notebook interactively (without a Pipeline) and still have sensible defaults for testing.

sections.title: Reading and Writing Data in Notebooks This section consolidates the core read/write patterns you will use in every data engineering notebook. Think of it as a reference you will return to throughout the course. Reading Data

blocks.content:
# ── Read a Delta table (from attached Lakehouse) ──
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/orders')
# Or via table name:
df = spark.table('orders')

# ── Read CSV from Files/ ──
df = spark.read.csv('Files/raw/orders/', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# ── Read JSON ──
df = spark.read.json('Files/raw/events/')

# ── Read Parquet ──
df = spark.read.parquet('Files/raw/exports/')

# ── Read from another Lakehouse (cross-workspace) ──
df = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'abfss://other_workspace@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/'
    'other_lakehouse.Lakehouse/Tables/customers'
)

# ── Read from a Warehouse ──
# Use Spark connector for Fabric Warehouse
df = spark.read.synapsesql('wh_sales.dbo.fact_orders')
Writing Data

# ── Write a managed Delta table ──
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('clean_orders')

# ── Append to an existing table ──
df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('orders')

# ── Write with partitioning ──
df.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('order_date') \
    .saveAsTable('orders_partitioned')

# ── Write to Files/ (as Parquet) ──
df.write.parquet('Files/staging/clean_orders/')

# ── MERGE (upsert) with Delta Lake ──
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

target = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'customers')
target.alias('t').merge(
    source=updates_df.alias('s'),
    condition='t.customer_id = s.customer_id'
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()

Important: saveAsTable() registers the table in the Lakehouse metastore, making it visible to the SQL analytics endpoint and Power BI. save() writes files to a path but does not register a table. Always use saveAsTable() for tables that downstream consumers need to query.

sections.title: The Spark UI — Debugging and Performance When a Spark job runs, Fabric provides access to the Spark UI — a web-based dashboard that shows every detail of your job’s execution. This is the single most important debugging tool for Spark performance problems. Accessing the Spark UI Click the “View Spark jobs” link that appears below a code cell after it finishes executing. This opens the Spark UI in a new panel or tab, showing all jobs, stages, and tasks for that cell’s execution. Key Spark UI Tabs Tab What It Shows When to Use Jobs All Spark jobs triggered by your code. Each action (count, write, show) creates a job. Overview: see how many jobs ran, which succeeded, and overall duration. Stages Each job is split into stages (separated by shuffles). Shows tasks per stage, data read/written, duration. Identify the slowest stage. Check for data skew (one task takes much longer than others). Tasks Individual tasks within a stage. Shows duration, data size, GC time, shuffle read/write per task. Find the specific task that is slow. Check for skew: one task processing 10x more data than others. Storage Cached DataFrames/tables and their memory usage. Verify that .cache() or .persist() is working and not consuming too much memory. SQL / DataFrame Visual query execution plan with operators, row counts, and timing at each step. Understand the full query plan. Find unnecessary shuffles. Verify partition pruning. Environment Spark configuration, classpath, system properties. Verify that your %%configure settings are applied correctly.

blocks.content:
Reading the Query Plan
The SQL/DataFrame tab shows the physical execution plan as a tree of operators. Reading this tree is a critical skill:
•  Scan (FileScan parquet): How many files and rows were read? If this number is much larger than expected, check whether partition pruning is working.
•  Filter: Was your WHERE clause pushed down to the scan level? If the filter appears after the scan, data is being read unnecessarily.
•  Exchange (shuffle): Data is redistributed across partitions. Shuffles are expensive. Minimize them by structuring joins and aggregations carefully.
•  HashAggregate / SortMergeJoin / BroadcastHashJoin: The join and aggregation strategies. BroadcastHashJoin is fast (small table is broadcast to all executors). SortMergeJoin is slower but handles large-large joins.
Tip: If one side of a join is small (< 10 MB), Spark should use a BroadcastHashJoin automatically. If it is not, you can hint: df_large.join(broadcast(df_small), 'key'). This eliminates the shuffle for the small table.

sections.title: Spark Performance Fundamentals Spark performance is a course-length topic on its own. This section covers the essential principles that apply to every Fabric Notebook. The Five Performance Pillars

items: Minimize shuffles. A shuffle redistributes data across all partitions — every row may move to a different executor. Shuffles are triggered by joins, group by, distinct, repartition, and window functions. Each shuffle writes data to disk and reads it back. Reduce shuffles by: pre-partitioning data on join keys, using broadcast joins for small tables, and combining multiple aggregations into a single group by.

Right-size partitions. Target 128 MB per partition for a balance of parallelism and overhead. Too few partitions (e.g., 1 partition for 10 GB) underutilize your cluster. Too many partitions (e.g., 10,000 partitions for 1 GB) create excessive task scheduling overhead. Use spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to control post-shuffle partition count, and spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes to control read-time partitioning.

Push filters early. Apply WHERE clauses as early as possible so Spark’s Catalyst optimizer can push them down to the file scan. On Delta tables with partitioning, this enables partition pruning — entire directories are skipped without reading any data. On Z-Ordered tables, this enables file pruning based on min/max statistics.

Cache judiciously. If you read a DataFrame multiple times (e.g., once for analysis, once for writing), cache it: df.cache(). This stores the DataFrame in executor memory, avoiding re-reading from OneLake. But do not cache everything — cache consumes memory that could be used for computation. Cache only DataFrames that are reused and expensive to recompute.

Use Adaptive Query Execution (AQE). AQE is enabled by default in Fabric. It dynamically adjusts shuffle partitions, converts sort-merge joins to broadcast joins when one side is small, and coalesces small partitions after shuffles. Leave it enabled unless you have a specific reason to disable it.

blocks.content: Common Anti-Patterns Anti-Pattern Why It Hurts Fix df.collect() on large DataFrames Pulls all data to driver memory. Crashes with OOM. Use .show(n), .take(n), or .limit(n) instead. Only collect aggregated results. UDFs (User Defined Functions) Serialize data row-by-row between JVM and Python. Extremely slow. Use built-in Spark functions (pyspark.sql.functions). Rewrite logic with Column expressions. for loop over rows Python loops negate Spark’s parallelism. Processes rows sequentially. Express logic as DataFrame operations (withColumn, when, otherwise). Think in columns, not rows. Reading entire table when only a subset is needed Wastes I/O and compute on data you will filter out later. Apply filters in the read statement or immediately after. Use partitioning and Z-Order. Too many small writes Creates thousands of tiny Parquet files (small file problem). Use coalesce(n) before writing to control output file count. Run OPTIMIZE afterward.

sections.title: Notebook Best Practices for Production A notebook that works interactively is not necessarily ready for production. Production notebooks must be reliable, maintainable, and monitorable. Structure and Organization • Start every notebook with a Markdown header: title, purpose, author, last modified date, and parameter descriptions. • Organize code into logical sections with Markdown cells: Configuration, Read, Transform, Validate, Write, Cleanup. • Use parameters (notebookutils.notebook.getParam) for all values that change between runs: dates, table names, file paths, modes (full vs. incremental). • End every notebook with notebookutils.notebook.exit() to return a status or metric to the calling Pipeline. Error Handling

blocks.content:
try:
    df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/orders')
    row_count = df.count()
    if row_count == 0:
        raise ValueError('Orders table is empty — aborting transformation')

    # ... transformation logic ...

    result_count = result_df.count()
    result_df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('clean_orders')
    notebookutils.notebook.exit(f'SUCCESS: {result_count} rows written')

except Exception as e:
    print(f'ERROR: {str(e)}')
    notebookutils.notebook.exit(f'FAILED: {str(e)}')
    raise  # Re-raise so Pipeline sees the failure

Data Quality Checks
Add validation between the transform and write steps:
•  Row count checks — Is the output count within expected bounds?
•  Null checks — Are required columns free of NULLs?
•  Range checks — Are dates within expected ranges? Are amounts non-negative?
•  Uniqueness checks — Are primary key columns unique?
•  Schema checks — Does the output DataFrame match the expected schema?
If any check fails, raise an exception to abort the write and signal failure to the Pipeline.
Version Control
Fabric Notebooks support Git integration through workspace Git settings. When enabled:
•  Notebook changes are committed to a Git repository (Azure DevOps or GitHub).
•  You can create branches, review pull requests, and merge changes just like application code.
•  Rollbacks are straightforward — revert to any previous commit.
