Important: Treat notebooks as production code. Use Git, write clear documentation, add error handling, and never hard-code values that should be parameters. The difference between a disposable notebook and a production notebook is discipline, not technology.

sections.title: Module 6 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: Spark architecture in Fabric — driver/executor model, tasks and partitions, serverless provisioning, starter pools, and CU-based billing
Session configuration — %%configure for executors, memory, cores, and shuffle partitions; starter pools vs. custom pools
The Notebook experience — code and Markdown cells, Lakehouse attachment, multi-language support (PySpark, SQL, Scala, R), temp views for cross-language bridging, display() for visualization
Environments — centralized library management (PyPI, custom wheels), Spark property configuration, workspace-level defaults, and publishing lifecycle
notebookutils — fs (file operations), credentials (Key Vault secrets), notebook.run/runMultiple (orchestration), notebook.exit/getParam (Pipeline integration)
Reading and writing data — Delta, CSV, JSON, Parquet reads; saveAsTable vs. save; MERGE with DeltaTable; cross-Lakehouse access via abfss://
Spark UI — Jobs, Stages, Tasks, SQL/DataFrame tabs; reading query plans; identifying shuffles, skew, and pruning
Performance fundamentals — five pillars (minimize shuffles, right-size partitions, push filters, cache judiciously, use AQE); anti-patterns (collect, UDFs, for loops, small writes)
Production best practices — notebook structure, parameterization, error handling, data quality checks, and Git version control

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Before moving to Module 7, complete these exercises:

items: Create an Environment with at least two PyPI libraries (e.g., great_expectations, pyarrow). Publish it. Attach it to a new Notebook and verify the libraries import without error.
Create a Notebook attached to your Lakehouse. Read a CSV from Files/, apply at least three transformations (filter, add column, type cast), and write the result as a Delta table with saveAsTable().
In the same Notebook, switch to a %%sql cell. Create a temp view from the Delta table and write a GROUP BY query. Verify the result renders with display().
Open the Spark UI after running your transformation. Navigate to the SQL/DataFrame tab and examine the query plan. Identify the file scan, any filters, and any shuffles.

Write a second Notebook that receives a load_date parameter via getParam() and returns a row count via exit(). Call it from a Pipeline Notebook Activity. Verify the return value appears in the Pipeline monitoring output.
Intentionally introduce a data quality issue (e.g., insert a NULL into a required column). Add a validation check that catches the issue, raises an exception, and exits with a failure message.

blocks.content: You are not just writing code in a notebook.

You are operating a distributed compute engine that can process terabytes of data in minutes, orchestrated through Pipelines, governed by Environments, and monitored through the Spark UI. This is industrial-scale data engineering.

In Module 7, we go deep into PySpark Transformations — the DataFrame API that you will use for every transformation in this course. You will master selects, filters, joins, aggregations, window functions, and the complete PySpark toolkit for production data engineering.

chapters.title: PySpark Transformations Deep Dive
chapters.description: Think in Columns, Not in Rows Estimated Time: 150 Minutes Module Objective: Master the PySpark DataFrame API — selects, filters, column expressions, joins, aggregations, window functions, null handling, type casting, UDFs, and the complete transformation toolkit for production data engineering.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 7 Module 6 taught you the Spark engine, the Notebook experience, and the infrastructure. This module is about what you do with that engine: transform data. The PySpark DataFrame API is the primary language of data engineering in Fabric. Every transformation — filtering orders, joining customers to transactions, calculating running totals, deduplicating events, handling nulls, casting types, pivoting reports — is expressed through this API. Mastering it is not optional. It is the core skill. The mental model that unlocks PySpark is: think in columns, not in rows. In a Python for loop, you process one row at a time. In PySpark, you describe what should happen to an entire column, and Spark applies it in parallel across millions of rows. You never touch individual rows. You describe transformations declaratively, and Spark executes them across the cluster. This module is code-heavy by design. Every concept includes a working code example that you should run in a Fabric Notebook. Reading is not enough — you must write and run the code to build muscle memory. We will cover: the DataFrame and Column objects, selecting and renaming columns, filtering rows, adding computed columns, type casting, null handling, string and date functions, joins, aggregations, window functions, pivot/unpivot, deduplication, and writing the output.

sections.title: The DataFrame Object A DataFrame is PySpark’s core data structure. It is a distributed, immutable collection of rows organized into named columns. Every read, every transformation, and every write operates on DataFrames. Key Properties • Distributed: The data is split into partitions stored across multiple executors. You never see the partitions directly — you interact with the DataFrame as if it were a single table. • Immutable: You cannot modify a DataFrame in place. Every transformation returns a new DataFrame. The original is unchanged. This immutability enables Spark’s lazy evaluation and fault tolerance. • Lazily evaluated: Transformations (filter, select, join) are recorded but not executed immediately. Spark builds an execution plan and only runs it when you trigger an action (count, show, write). This lets Spark optimize the entire plan before executing anything. • Typed columns: Each column has a data type (StringType, IntegerType, DateType, etc.). Types are enforced on write to Delta tables. Creating DataFrames

blocks.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import *
from pyspark.sql.types import *

# Read from Delta table (most common in Fabric)
df = spark.table('orders')

# Read from CSV
df = spark.read.csv('Files/raw/orders/', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Create from Python list (useful for testing)
data = [('A001', 'Alice', 150.0), ('A002', 'Bob', 230.0)]
schema = ['order_id', 'customer_name', 'amount']
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, schema)

# Inspect
df.printSchema()     # Shows column names and types
df.show(5)           # Shows first 5 rows as text
display(df)          # Fabric rich rendering (interactive table)
df.count()           # Number of rows (triggers execution)
df.columns           # List of column names
df.dtypes            # List of (name, type) tuples

Transformations vs. Actions Type Examples Behavior Transformation select, filter, join, groupBy, withColumn, drop, orderBy Lazy — recorded but not executed. Returns a new DataFrame. Action show, count, collect, write, first, take, toPandas Triggers execution of all preceding transformations. Returns a result.

Important: Every action triggers the entire chain of transformations from the source read to the action. If you call df.count() and then df.show(), Spark runs the entire chain twice. To avoid this, cache the DataFrame before multiple actions: df.cache().

sections.title: Selecting, Renaming, and Dropping Columns Column selection is the most basic transformation. It controls which columns flow downstream and in what order. Selecting Columns

blocks.content:
# Select specific columns
df.select('order_id', 'customer_id', 'amount')

# Select with Column objects (equivalent)
df.select(col('order_id'), col('customer_id'), col('amount'))

# Select all columns
df.select('*')

# Select with expression (rename inline)
df.select(
    col('order_id'),
    col('customer_id').alias('cust_id'),
    (col('amount') * 1.1).alias('amount_with_tax')
)
Renaming Columns

# Rename a single column
df.withColumnRenamed('customer_id', 'cust_id')

# Rename multiple columns (chain)
df.withColumnRenamed('customer_id', 'cust_id') \
  .withColumnRenamed('order_date', 'date')

# Rename all columns to snake_case (bulk rename pattern)
import re
for c in df.columns:
    new_name = re.sub(r'([A-Z])', r'_\1', c).lower().lstrip('_')
    df = df.withColumnRenamed(c, new_name)
Dropping Columns

# Drop a single column
df.drop('internal_notes')

# Drop multiple columns
df.drop('internal_notes', 'temp_flag', 'debug_info')

Tip: When reading wide tables with many columns, select only the columns you need immediately after reading. This reduces data transfer and memory consumption across all subsequent operations: df = spark.table('orders').select('order_id', 'customer_id', 'amount', 'order_date').

sections.title: Filtering Rows Filtering removes rows that do not match a condition. In PySpark, filtering uses the filter() or where() method (they are interchangeable). Basic Filters

blocks.content:
# Equality
df.filter(col('status') == 'active')

# Comparison
df.filter(col('amount') > 100)

# String contains
df.filter(col('name').contains('Corp'))

# String starts with / ends with
df.filter(col('email').endswith('@company.com'))

# In list
df.filter(col('region').isin('North', 'South', 'West'))

# Date range
df.filter(
    (col('order_date') >= '2026-01-01') &
    (col('order_date') < '2026-02-01')
)

# NOT condition
df.filter(~col('status').isin('cancelled', 'refunded'))
Combining Conditions

# AND: use & (both conditions must be true)
df.filter(
    (col('status') == 'active') &
    (col('amount') > 100)
)

# OR: use | (either condition can be true)
df.filter(
    (col('region') == 'North') |
    (col('region') == 'West')
)

# Complex: combine AND and OR with parentheses
df.filter(
    (col('status') == 'active') &
    ((col('amount') > 100) | (col('priority') == 'high'))
)

Warning: Always wrap each condition in parentheses when combining with & or |. Without parentheses, Python operator precedence may produce unexpected results. (col('a') > 1) & (col('b') > 2) is correct. col('a') > 1 & col('b') > 2 is not. Null Filtering

# Keep rows where column IS NOT NULL
df.filter(col('email').isNotNull())

# Keep rows where column IS NULL
df.filter(col('email').isNull())

# Drop rows where ANY specified column is null
df.dropna(subset=['email', 'phone'])

# Drop rows where ALL columns are null
df.dropna(how='all')

sections.title: Adding and Transforming Columns withColumn() adds a new column or replaces an existing column. It is the workhorse for data transformation. Computed Columns

blocks.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import *

# Arithmetic
df.withColumn('amount_with_tax', col('amount') * 1.1)

# String manipulation
df.withColumn('name_upper', upper(col('customer_name')))
df.withColumn('name_trimmed', trim(col('customer_name')))
df.withColumn('domain', split(col('email'), '@')[1])

# Date extraction
df.withColumn('order_year', year(col('order_date')))
df.withColumn('order_month', month(col('order_date')))
df.withColumn('day_of_week', dayofweek(col('order_date')))

# Date arithmetic
df.withColumn('days_since_order',
    datediff(current_date(), col('order_date')))
df.withColumn('delivery_date',
    date_add(col('order_date'), 7))
Conditional Logic (when / otherwise) when() and otherwise() are PySpark’s equivalent of SQL’s CASE WHEN:

# Simple conditional
df.withColumn('size',
    when(col('amount') >= 1000, 'Large')
    .when(col('amount') >= 100, 'Medium')
    .otherwise('Small')
)

# Conditional with multiple columns
df.withColumn('risk_flag',
    when(
        (col('amount') > 5000) & (col('new_customer') == True),
        'HIGH'
    ).when(
        col('amount') > 5000,
        'MEDIUM'
    ).otherwise('LOW')
)
Type Casting

# Cast string to integer
df.withColumn('quantity', col('quantity').cast('int'))

# Cast string to date
df.withColumn('order_date', to_date(col('order_date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd'))

# Cast string to timestamp
df.withColumn('event_time',
    to_timestamp(col('event_time_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'))

# Cast multiple columns
df.withColumn('amount', col('amount').cast('double')) \
  .withColumn('quantity', col('quantity').cast('int')) \
  .withColumn('order_date', to_date(col('order_date'), 'MM/dd/yyyy'))

Important: Type casting is critical for Delta table writes. Delta enforces schema, so a string column that should be a date must be cast before writing. Mismatched types cause write failures with clear error messages. Always cast types explicitly rather than relying on inferSchema.

sections.title: Null Handling Nulls are inevitable in real-world data. PySpark provides a comprehensive set of functions for detecting, replacing, and handling nulls. Detecting Nulls

blocks.content:
# Count nulls per column
from pyspark.sql.functions import count, when, col

null_counts = df.select([
    count(when(col(c).isNull(), c)).alias(c)
    for c in df.columns
])
display(null_counts)
Replacing Nulls

# Fill nulls with a default value (all columns of matching type)
df.fillna(0)                    # Fills numeric nulls with 0
df.fillna('Unknown')            # Fills string nulls with 'Unknown'

# Fill nulls in specific columns
df.fillna({'region': 'Unknown', 'amount': 0.0, 'quantity': 1})

# Replace null with another column's value
df.withColumn('phone',
    coalesce(col('mobile_phone'), col('work_phone'), lit('N/A'))
)
coalesce() returns the first non-null value from a list of columns. It is the standard pattern for “fall back to an alternative if the primary is null.” Null-Safe Operations

# Null-safe equality (treats NULL == NULL as true)
df.filter(col('region').eqNullSafe('North'))

# NaN handling (for float columns, NaN != NULL)
from pyspark.sql.functions import isnan
df.filter(~isnan(col('amount')) & col('amount').isNotNull())

# Replace NaN with null (then handle uniformly)
from pyspark.sql.functions import nanvl
df.withColumn('amount', nanvl(col('amount'), lit(None)))

Warning: In Spark, NULL and NaN are different. NULL means “no value.” NaN (Not a Number) is a specific float value that means “invalid number.” isNull() does not catch NaN, and isnan() does not catch NULL. Check for both when cleaning numeric columns.

sections.title: Joins Joins combine rows from two DataFrames based on matching column values. Joins are the most common multi-table operation and one of the most expensive in terms of Spark performance. Join Types Type PySpark Keyword Result Inner 'inner' Only rows where the key exists in BOTH DataFrames. Left Outer 'left' or 'left_outer' All rows from left + matched rows from right. Unmatched right columns are NULL. Right Outer 'right' or 'right_outer' All rows from right + matched rows from left. Unmatched left columns are NULL. Full Outer 'outer' or 'full_outer' All rows from both sides. Unmatched columns on either side are NULL. Left Semi 'left_semi' Rows from left where the key exists in right. No right columns in output (like EXISTS in SQL). Left Anti 'left_anti' Rows from left where the key does NOT exist in right (like NOT EXISTS in SQL). Cross 'cross' Cartesian product: every row from left paired with every row from right. Use with extreme caution.

blocks.content: Join Syntax

# Basic inner join on a single key
orders = spark.table('orders')
customers = spark.table('customers')

result = orders.join(customers, orders.customer_id == customers.customer_id, 'inner')

# Simplified syntax when key column names match
result = orders.join(customers, 'customer_id', 'inner')

# Multi-column join
result = orders.join(
    customers,
    (orders.customer_id == customers.customer_id) &
    (orders.region == customers.region),
    'inner'
)

# Left outer join
result = orders.join(customers, 'customer_id', 'left')

# Left anti join (find orders with no matching customer)
orphan_orders = orders.join(customers, 'customer_id', 'left_anti')
The Duplicate Column Problem When both DataFrames have columns with the same name (other than the join key), the result contains ambiguous duplicates. This causes errors in downstream operations.

# Problem: both DataFrames have a 'name' column
# result.select('name')  # ERROR: ambiguous reference

# Solution 1: Alias the DataFrames
result = orders.alias('o').join(
    customers.alias('c'),
    col('o.customer_id') == col('c.customer_id'),
    'inner'
).select('o.*', col('c.name').alias('customer_name'))

# Solution 2: Drop the duplicate column after joining
result = orders.join(customers, 'customer_id', 'inner')
    .drop(customers.name)  # Drop the right side's 'name'

# Solution 3: Rename before joining
customers_renamed = customers.withColumnRenamed('name', 'customer_name')
result = orders.join(customers_renamed, 'customer_id', 'inner')
Broadcast Joins When one side of a join is small (< 10 MB by default), Spark broadcasts it to all executors, eliminating the shuffle of the large table. This is dramatically faster.

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# Force broadcast of the small table
result = orders.join(broadcast(dim_status), 'status_code', 'inner')

Tip: Spark automatically broadcasts tables under 10 MB (controlled by spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold). For tables slightly above the threshold, use broadcast() explicitly. For very large dimension tables (> 100 MB), do not broadcast — it will consume too much executor memory.

sections.title: Aggregations and Grouping Aggregations summarize data: total revenue by region, average order value per customer, count of events per day. They are the bridge between raw transactional data and analytical insight. Basic Aggregations

blocks.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, avg, count, min, max, countDistinct

# Single aggregation
df.groupBy('region').agg(
    sum('amount').alias('total_revenue')
)

# Multiple aggregations
df.groupBy('region', 'product_category').agg(
    sum('amount').alias('total_revenue'),
    avg('amount').alias('avg_order_value'),
    count('order_id').alias('order_count'),
    countDistinct('customer_id').alias('unique_customers'),
    min('order_date').alias('first_order'),
    max('order_date').alias('last_order')
)

Aggregation Functions Reference Function Description Example sum() Sum of values sum('amount') avg() / mean() Average of values avg('amount') count() Count of non-null values count('order_id') countDistinct() Count of unique values countDistinct('customer_id') min() / max() Minimum / maximum value min('order_date') first() / last() First / last value in group first('status') collect_list() All values as array (with duplicates) collect_list('product_id') collect_set() Unique values as array collect_set('region') stddev() / variance() Standard deviation / variance stddev('amount') percentile_approx() Approximate percentile percentile_approx('amount', 0.5)
Aggregation Without Grouping

# Global aggregation (entire DataFrame)
df.agg(
    sum('amount').alias('total_revenue'),
    count('order_id').alias('total_orders'),
    avg('amount').alias('avg_order_value')
).show()
Having (Filtering Aggregated Results)

# Equivalent of SQL HAVING clause
df.groupBy('customer_id').agg(
    sum('amount').alias('total_spent'),
    count('order_id').alias('order_count')
).filter(col('total_spent') > 10000)

sections.title: Window Functions Window functions are the most powerful and most underutilized tool in the PySpark toolkit. They let you compute values across a set of rows that are related to the current row — without collapsing those rows into a group. Unlike aggregations (which reduce rows), window functions add a computed column while keeping every row. The Window Specification Every window function needs a window specification that defines: • partitionBy: Which groups of rows to compute within (like GROUP BY, but without collapsing). • orderBy: How to order rows within each partition (required for ranking and running calculations). • Frame (optional): Which rows relative to the current row to include (e.g., 3 rows before to current row).

blocks.content:
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Define a window partitioned by customer, ordered by date
w = Window.partitionBy('customer_id').orderBy('order_date')
Ranking Functions

# Row number: unique sequence within each partition
df.withColumn('row_num', row_number().over(w))

# Rank: same rank for ties, gaps after ties
df.withColumn('rank', rank().over(w))

# Dense rank: same rank for ties, no gaps
df.withColumn('dense_rank', dense_rank().over(w))

# Percent rank: rank as a percentage (0 to 1)
df.withColumn('pct_rank', percent_rank().over(w))

# NTILE: divide rows into N buckets
df.withColumn('quartile', ntile(4).over(w))
Running Calculations

# Cumulative sum
df.withColumn('running_total',
    sum('amount').over(w.rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, 0))
)

# Moving average (3-row window)
df.withColumn('moving_avg_3',
    avg('amount').over(w.rowsBetween(-2, 0))
)

# Cumulative count
df.withColumn('order_sequence',
    count('order_id').over(w.rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, 0))
)
Offset Functions

# Previous row's value (lag)
df.withColumn('prev_amount', lag('amount', 1).over(w))

# Next row's value (lead)
df.withColumn('next_amount', lead('amount', 1).over(w))

# Difference from previous
df.withColumn('amount_change',
    col('amount') - lag('amount', 1).over(w)
)

# First / last value in partition
w_unordered = Window.partitionBy('customer_id')
df.withColumn('first_order_amount',
    first('amount').over(w)
)

Common Window Function Use Cases Use Case Pattern Window Spec Deduplicate (keep latest) row_number() == 1, ordered by date DESC partitionBy(key).orderBy(desc('date')) Running total sum().over(rows unbounded preceding to current) partitionBy(group).orderBy(date) Previous period comparison lag(metric, 1) partitionBy(entity).orderBy(period) Top N per group row_number() <= N partitionBy(group).orderBy(desc(metric)) Percent of group total amount / sum(amount).over(partition) partitionBy(group) (no orderBy)

sections.title: Deduplication, Pivot, Unpivot, and Other Patterns Deduplication Duplicate rows are one of the most common data quality issues. PySpark provides multiple deduplication strategies:

blocks.content:
# Drop exact duplicates (all columns)
df.dropDuplicates()

# Drop duplicates based on specific columns
df.dropDuplicates(['order_id'])

# Keep the latest record per key (most common pattern)
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

w = Window.partitionBy('order_id').orderBy(col('updated_at').desc())
deduped = df.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w)) \
            .filter(col('rn') == 1) \
            .drop('rn')

The window-based deduplication pattern (partition by key, order by timestamp DESC, keep row_number = 1) is the standard production approach because it gives you explicit control over which record to keep. Pivot Pivot rotates rows into columns — useful for creating cross-tab reports:

# Pivot: each region becomes a column, values are sum of amount
pivoted = df.groupBy('product_category') \
            .pivot('region', ['North', 'South', 'East', 'West']) \
            .agg(sum('amount'))

# Result: product_category | North | South | East | West

Tip: Always specify the pivot values list explicitly (e.g., ['North', 'South', 'East', 'West']). Without it, Spark performs an extra pass over the data to discover all distinct values, which is slow for large datasets. Unpivot (Melt) Unpivot rotates columns into rows — the reverse of pivot. PySpark does not have a built-in unpivot() before Spark 3.4, but you can use stack():

# Unpivot: convert columns back to rows
unpivoted = pivoted.selectExpr(
    'product_category',
    "stack(4, 'North', North, 'South', South, 'East', East, 'West', West)"
    " as (region, total_amount)"
)

# Spark 3.4+: native unpivot
unpivoted = pivoted.unpivot(
    ids=['product_category'],
    values=['North', 'South', 'East', 'West'],
    variableColumnName='region',
    valueColumnName='total_amount'
)
Union / Append

# Union two DataFrames with identical schemas
combined = df_jan.unionByName(df_feb)

# Union with different column order (matches by name)
combined = df_jan.unionByName(df_feb, allowMissingColumns=True)

Important: Always use unionByName() instead of union(). union() matches columns by position (dangerous if column order differs). unionByName() matches by column name, which is safe and explicit. The allowMissingColumns=True flag fills missing columns with NULL. Sorting

# Sort ascending
df.orderBy('order_date')

# Sort descending
df.orderBy(col('amount').desc())

# Multi-column sort
df.orderBy(col('region').asc(), col('amount').desc())

Warning: Sorting is an expensive full-shuffle operation. Avoid sorting intermediate DataFrames. Only sort the final output if the destination requires it. For Delta table writes, sorting is unnecessary — use Z-ORDER at the storage level instead.

sections.title: Module 7 Checkpoint You now have the complete PySpark transformation toolkit:

items: DataFrame fundamentals — distributed, immutable, lazily evaluated; transformations vs. actions; printSchema, show, display, count
Selecting and renaming — select, alias, withColumnRenamed, drop; bulk rename patterns; early column pruning
Filtering — filter/where, comparison operators, isin, contains, startswith/endswith, date ranges, null checks, AND/OR with parentheses
Column transformations — withColumn, arithmetic, string functions (upper, trim, split), date functions (year, month, datediff, date_add), when/otherwise conditional logic
Type casting — cast(), to_date(), to_timestamp(); explicit typing for Delta schema enforcement
Null handling — isNull, isNotNull, fillna, coalesce, dropna; NULL vs. NaN distinction; null-safe equality
Joins — inner, left, right, full outer, left semi, left anti, cross; single-key and multi-key; duplicate column resolution; broadcast joins
Aggregations — groupBy + agg; sum, avg, count, countDistinct, min, max, collect_list/set; global aggregation; HAVING via filter
Window functions — Window.partitionBy().orderBy(); row_number, rank, dense_rank; running sum/average; lag/lead; deduplication and Top N patterns
Dedup, pivot, unpivot, union, sort — dropDuplicates, window-based dedup; pivot/unpivot; unionByName; orderBy with performance warnings

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Build a complete transformation notebook:

items: Read a fact table (e.g., orders) and a dimension table (e.g., customers) from your Lakehouse.
Filter orders to only active status and the last 12 months.
Join orders to customers on customer_id (left join). Handle the duplicate column problem.
Add computed columns: order_year, order_month, amount_with_tax (amount * 1.1), and a size category (Large/Medium/Small based on amount).
Handle nulls: fill missing region with ‘Unknown’, fill missing phone with coalesce(mobile, work, ‘N/A’).
Aggregate: total revenue and order count by region and month.
Window: add a running total of revenue per customer ordered by date. Add a rank column (top customers by total spend).
Deduplicate: if the orders table has duplicates on order_id, keep the most recently updated row.
Write the final result as a Delta table. Verify it appears in the SQL analytics endpoint.

blocks.content: You are not just writing PySpark code.

You are building a declarative transformation pipeline that describes what should happen to every column and every row, expressed in the language of distributed computation. Spark takes your declarations and executes them across hundreds of cores in parallel. That is the power of thinking in columns.

In Module 8, we cover Spark SQL and the SQL Analytics Endpoint — the SQL-first approach to the same transformations, including the Lakehouse SQL interface, T-SQL compatibility, views, and cross-engine querying.

chapters.title: Spark SQL and Advanced SQL Patterns
chapters.description: SQL That Runs on a Distributed Engine Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Write Spark SQL for data engineering — DDL and DML, temporary and persistent views, CTEs, MERGE for upserts, cross-database queries, Delta table management commands, and the relationship between Spark SQL and the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 8 Module 7 taught you the PySpark DataFrame API — the programmatic approach to transformation. This module teaches the same engine from the other side: SQL. Spark SQL is not a separate engine. It is the same Spark engine you used in Module 7, accessed through SQL syntax instead of Python method calls. When you write SELECT * FROM orders WHERE amount > 100 in a %%sql cell, Spark parses it, optimizes it through the same Catalyst optimizer, and executes it on the same executors that run your PySpark code. This means everything you learned about performance in Modules 6 and 7 — partitioning, shuffles, broadcast joins, AQE — applies equally to Spark SQL. The only difference is the syntax. Why dedicate an entire module to SQL? Because SQL is the universal language of analytics. Data engineers, SQL analysts, BI developers, and data scientists all speak SQL. Many Fabric users will spend more time writing Spark SQL than PySpark. And Spark SQL provides capabilities that the DataFrame API does not expose cleanly — particularly DDL (CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE), DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), and Delta management commands (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, DESCRIBE). This module covers: Spark SQL fundamentals in Notebooks, DDL for creating and managing tables, DML including the critical MERGE command, temporary and persistent views, CTEs for readable queries, cross-database queries, Delta table management via SQL, the relationship with the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint, and a production-patterns checkpoint.

sections.title: Running Spark SQL in Notebooks There are two ways to run Spark SQL in a Fabric Notebook: the %%sql magic command and the spark.sql() function. The %%sql Magic Put %%sql at the top of a cell to write pure SQL. The entire cell is treated as a SQL statement:

blocks.content: %%sql SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01' GROUP BY region ORDER BY total_revenue DESC

The result renders as an interactive table in the notebook output. The %%sql magic is ideal for standalone queries, ad-hoc analysis, and DDL/DML commands. The spark.sql() Function When you need to mix SQL with Python — passing variables, capturing results as DataFrames, or embedding SQL in a larger workflow — use spark.sql():

# Execute SQL and capture the result as a DataFrame
result_df = spark.sql("""
    SELECT region,
           SUM(amount) AS total_revenue
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01'
    GROUP BY region
""")

# Now use the result as a normal PySpark DataFrame
result_df.show()
top_region = result_df.first()['region']
print(f'Top region: {top_region}')

# Write the result to a new table
result_df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('revenue_by_region')
Passing Python Variables to SQL Spark SQL in %%sql cells does not directly accept Python variables. Use spark.sql() with f-strings instead:

load_date = '2026-01-15'
target_table = 'orders'

df = spark.sql(f"""
    SELECT * FROM {target_table}
    WHERE order_date = '{load_date}'
""")
display(df)

Warning: Using f-strings to inject values into SQL is convenient but carries SQL injection risk if the values come from untrusted sources. In production notebooks where parameters come from Pipeline inputs, this is acceptable because the Pipeline controls the values. Never use f-strings with user-supplied input. When to Use SQL vs. PySpark Scenario Preferred Approach Why Ad-hoc data exploration %%sql Fastest for quick queries. Immediate table rendering. DDL (CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE) %%sql SQL is the natural syntax for DDL. No PySpark equivalent. DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) %%sql SQL MERGE is cleaner than DeltaTable Python API for many patterns. Complex multi-step transformations PySpark Method chaining is more composable. Easier to debug step by step. Parameterized logic (date ranges, table names) spark.sql() with f-strings Combines SQL clarity with Python flexibility. Window functions Either Both syntaxes are equally capable. Use whichever your team reads more easily.

sections.title: DDL — Creating and Managing Tables Data Definition Language (DDL) commands create, modify, and drop database objects. In Spark SQL within Fabric, DDL operates on Delta tables in the attached Lakehouse. CREATE TABLE

blocks.content:
%%sql
-- Create a managed Delta table with explicit schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dim_customers (
    customer_id   STRING    NOT NULL,
    customer_name STRING,
    email         STRING,
    region        STRING,
    created_date  DATE,
    is_active     BOOLEAN   DEFAULT true
)
USING DELTA
COMMENT 'Customer dimension table for sales analytics'

-- Create table from a query (CTAS) CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold_revenue_summary USING DELTA AS SELECT region, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY region, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
ALTER TABLE
%%sql -- Add a column ALTER TABLE dim_customers ADD COLUMNS ( loyalty_tier STRING COMMENT 'Gold, Silver, Bronze' )
-- Rename a column ALTER TABLE dim_customers RENAME COLUMN email TO email_address
-- Change column data type (must be compatible widening) ALTER TABLE dim_customers ALTER COLUMN customer_name TYPE STRING
-- Add a table comment ALTER TABLE dim_customers SET TBLPROPERTIES ( 'comment' = 'Customer dimension - updated daily by pl_customer_pipeline' )
DROP TABLE
%%sql -- Drop a table (removes metadata AND data from OneLake) DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_staging_orders

Warning: DROP TABLE on a managed Delta table deletes the data files from OneLake permanently. There is no recycle bin. Always double-check the table name. In production, restrict DROP permissions to admin roles. DESCRIBE and SHOW
%%sql -- Show all tables in the current Lakehouse SHOW TABLES
-- Describe table schema DESCRIBE TABLE orders
-- Extended description (includes location, format, properties) DESCRIBE TABLE EXTENDED orders
-- File-level details (size, file count, partitioning) DESCRIBE DETAIL orders
-- Show table history (Delta transaction log) DESCRIBE HISTORY orders

DESCRIBE HISTORY is particularly powerful — it shows every commit to the Delta table: who wrote, when, what operation (INSERT, MERGE, OPTIMIZE), and how many rows were affected. This is your audit trail.

sections.title: DML — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE Data Manipulation Language (DML) commands modify data within tables. Delta Lake supports full DML in Spark SQL — unlike plain Parquet, which only supports append and overwrite. INSERT

blocks.content: %%sql -- Insert specific values INSERT INTO dim_customers VALUES ('C001', 'Acme Corp', 'acme@example.com', 'North', '2025-01-15', true, 'Gold'), ('C002', 'Beta Inc', 'beta@example.com', 'South', '2025-03-20', true, 'Silver')
-- Insert from a query INSERT INTO dim_customers SELECT customer_id, customer_name, email, region, created_date, is_active, loyalty_tier FROM staging_customers WHERE is_valid = true

-- INSERT OVERWRITE (replace all data in the table)
INSERT OVERWRITE dim_customers
SELECT * FROM staging_customers_full

UPDATE
%%sql -- Update specific rows UPDATE dim_customers SET loyalty_tier = 'Gold' WHERE customer_id IN ( SELECT customer_id FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 50000 )
-- Update with a calculated value UPDATE orders SET amount_with_tax = amount * 1.1 WHERE amount_with_tax IS NULL
DELETE
%%sql -- Delete specific rows DELETE FROM orders WHERE status = 'cancelled' AND order_date < '2025-01-01'
-- Delete using a subquery DELETE FROM dim_customers WHERE customer_id NOT IN ( SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders )

Important: UPDATE and DELETE on Delta tables create new file versions (they do not modify files in place). The old files remain until VACUUM removes them. This means UPDATE and DELETE are more expensive than in a traditional RDBMS — they rewrite entire Parquet files. For large-scale updates, prefer MERGE which handles the rewrite more efficiently.

sections.title: MERGE — The Upsert Command MERGE is the single most important DML command for production data engineering. It combines INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE into a single atomic operation. In data warehousing terms, it is the “upsert” — insert new rows and update existing ones in one pass. MERGE Syntax

blocks.content: %%sql MERGE INTO dim_customers AS target USING staging_customers AS source ON target.customer_id = source.customer_id
WHEN MATCHED AND source.is_deleted = true THEN DELETE

WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET
        target.customer_name = source.customer_name,
        target.email         = source.email,
        target.region        = source.region,
        target.is_active     = source.is_active,
        target.loyalty_tier  = source.loyalty_tier

WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (customer_id, customer_name, email, region, created_date, is_active, loyalty_tier) VALUES (source.customer_id, source.customer_name, source.email, source.region, source.created_date, source.is_active, source.loyalty_tier)

MERGE Clauses Explained
Clause
When It Fires
Typical Action
WHEN MATCHED
Source row matches a target row (join condition is true).
UPDATE the target row with new values from source. Or DELETE the target row.
WHEN NOT MATCHED
Source row has no match in target.
INSERT the new row into target.
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE
Target row has no match in source (available in Spark 3.4+).
DELETE orphaned target rows. Or UPDATE them to mark as inactive.
Common MERGE Patterns Pattern 1: Simple Upsert. Insert new rows, update existing rows. No deletes. This is the most common pattern for incrementally loading dimension tables.
%%sql MERGE INTO dim_products AS t USING staging_products AS s ON t.product_id = s.product_id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *

The SET * and INSERT * shortcuts mean “all columns.” They work when source and target schemas match exactly. Pattern 2: Slowly Changing Dimension Type 1 (SCD1). Overwrite the old value with the new value. No history is kept. This is the same as Pattern 1 — the MERGE UPDATE simply overwrites. Pattern 3: Conditional Upsert with Delete. Insert new rows, update changed rows, delete rows marked for deletion in the source. The full MERGE syntax shown above demonstrates this pattern. Pattern 4: Incremental Fact Load. For fact tables where rows are append-only, use MERGE with only WHEN NOT MATCHED to avoid inserting duplicates:
%%sql MERGE INTO fact_orders AS t USING staging_orders AS s ON t.order_id = s.order_id WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *

Important: MERGE is an atomic operation. If it fails partway through, the table is not modified. This is the ACID guarantee of Delta Lake. It is far safer than separate INSERT + UPDATE + DELETE statements, which can leave the table in an inconsistent state if one fails.

sections.title: Views — Temporary and Persistent Views are named queries that can be referenced like tables. They do not store data — they store the SQL definition and execute it when referenced. Views are essential for abstraction, reusability, and security. Temporary Views Temporary views exist only for the duration of the Spark session (the notebook run). They are perfect for intermediate results:

blocks.content: %%sql -- Create a temp view CREATE OR REPLACE TEMP VIEW v_active_customers AS SELECT customer_id, customer_name, region FROM dim_customers WHERE is_active = true
-- Use the temp view in subsequent queries SELECT o.order_id, o.amount, c.customer_name, c.region FROM orders o JOIN v_active_customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id

Temporary views are also the bridge between PySpark and SQL within a notebook (as shown in Module 6): create a temp view from a PySpark DataFrame with df.createOrReplaceTempView('my_view'), then query it in a %%sql cell. Persistent Views in the SQL Analytics Endpoint The Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint supports persistent views — views that survive beyond a session and are visible to all SQL clients:

-- Run this in the SQL analytics endpoint (not in Spark SQL) CREATE VIEW dbo.v_monthly_revenue AS SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS order_year, MONTH(order_date) AS order_month, region, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date), region

Persistent views in the SQL analytics endpoint are visible to Power BI, SSMS, Azure Data Studio, and any JDBC/ODBC client. They are the standard mechanism for creating governed, analyst-friendly abstractions over Lakehouse tables. View Type Where Created Lifetime Visible To TEMP VIEW Spark SQL (%%sql or spark.sql) Current Spark session only Only the current notebook GLOBAL TEMP VIEW Spark SQL Current Spark application All notebooks in the same session cluster Persistent VIEW SQL analytics endpoint (T-SQL) Permanent until dropped All SQL clients, Power BI, external tools

sections.title: CTEs — Common Table Expressions Common Table Expressions (CTEs) are temporary named result sets defined within a single SQL statement using the WITH clause. They make complex queries readable, maintainable, and self-documenting. CTE Syntax

blocks.content:
%%sql
WITH customer_spend AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
           SUM(amount) AS total_spent,
           COUNT(*) AS order_count
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01'
    GROUP BY customer_id
),
customer_tier AS (
    SELECT cs.*,
           CASE
               WHEN total_spent >= 50000 THEN 'Platinum'
               WHEN total_spent >= 10000 THEN 'Gold'
               WHEN total_spent >= 1000  THEN 'Silver'
               ELSE 'Bronze'
           END AS tier
    FROM customer_spend cs
)
SELECT ct.tier,
       COUNT(*) AS customer_count,
       SUM(ct.total_spent) AS tier_revenue,
       AVG(ct.order_count) AS avg_orders
FROM customer_tier ct
GROUP BY ct.tier
ORDER BY tier_revenue DESC

Why CTEs Matter
•  Readability — Each CTE has a descriptive name that explains its purpose. Complex queries become a sequence of clearly named steps.
•  Reusability — A CTE defined once can be referenced multiple times in the same query. No need to duplicate subqueries.
•  Maintainability — Changing the logic for ‘customer_spend’ means editing one CTE, not finding every place a subquery was pasted.
•  Debugging — You can test each CTE independently by running it as a standalone SELECT.
Recursive CTEs
Spark SQL supports recursive CTEs for hierarchical data (org charts, category trees, bill of materials):
%%sql WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS ( -- Anchor: top-level managers (no manager_id) SELECT employee_id, employee_name, manager_id, 1 AS level FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
UNION ALL

-- Recursive: employees who report to someone in the previous level SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, e.manager_id, t.level + 1 FROM employees e JOIN org_tree t ON e.manager_id = t.employee_id ) SELECT * FROM org_tree ORDER BY level, employee_name

Important: CTEs are syntactic — they do not persist data. Spark may re-execute a CTE each time it is referenced. For CTEs that are expensive and referenced multiple times, consider materializing the result into a temp view or cached DataFrame instead.

sections.title: Window Functions in SQL Module 7 covered window functions in PySpark. This section provides the equivalent SQL syntax, which many users find more natural. Ranking

blocks.content:
%%sql
SELECT
    customer_id,
    order_id,
    amount,
    order_date,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn,
    RANK()       OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC)     AS rank,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC)     AS dense_rank
FROM orders
Running Calculations

%%sql SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER ( PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS running_total, AVG(amount) OVER ( PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS moving_avg_3 FROM orders
Lag and Lead

%%sql
SELECT
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    amount,
    LAG(amount, 1)  OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount,
    LEAD(amount, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS next_amount,
    amount - LAG(amount, 1) OVER (
        PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date
    ) AS amount_change
FROM orders
Deduplication with SQL Window The most common deduplication pattern in SQL:
%%sql -- Keep the latest record per order_id SELECT * FROM ( SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY updated_at DESC) AS rn FROM raw_orders ) WHERE rn = 1

Tip: Named windows reduce repetition when the same window spec is used across multiple columns: SELECT ..., SUM(amount) OVER w, AVG(amount) OVER w FROM orders WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date).

sections.title: Cross-Database Queries and the SQL Analytics Endpoint One of Fabric’s most powerful features is the ability to query across Lakehouses and Warehouses using three-part naming. This eliminates the need to copy data between items for cross-domain analysis. Three-Part Naming In Fabric SQL, you reference objects using the pattern: database.schema.object. For Lakehouses, the “database” is the Lakehouse or Warehouse name, and the schema is typically “dbo” (for Warehouses) or implicit (for Lakehouses).

blocks.content: %%sql -- Query a table in the current Lakehouse (no prefix needed) SELECT * FROM orders
-- Query a table in another Lakehouse in the same workspace SELECT * FROM lh_gold_sales.orders
-- Query a table in a Warehouse in the same workspace SELECT * FROM wh_sales.dbo.fact_orders
-- Cross-item join: Lakehouse table joined with Warehouse table SELECT o.order_id, o.amount, c.customer_name FROM orders o JOIN wh_sales.dbo.dim_customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id

Where Cross-Database Queries Work Context Three-Part Naming Supported Notes Spark SQL in Notebooks Yes Cross-Lakehouse queries within the same workspace. Uses the Spark metastore. SQL analytics endpoint Yes Cross-Lakehouse and cross-Warehouse queries. Uses the Fabric SQL engine. Warehouse T-SQL Yes Cross-Warehouse and cross-Lakehouse queries. Full T-SQL DML. Power BI (DirectLake) No — use semantic models DirectLake connects to one Lakehouse/Warehouse. Use a semantic model to combine.
The SQL Analytics Endpoint — Revisited In Module 3, you learned that every Lakehouse auto-generates a SQL analytics endpoint. Now that you understand Spark SQL, here is the critical distinction:

items: Spark SQL runs on the Spark engine. It has full read-write access. It supports DDL, DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), and all Delta management commands. It runs in Notebooks.

The SQL analytics endpoint runs on the Fabric SQL engine. It is read-only for Lakehouse tables (no INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE on Lakehouse tables). It supports views, stored functions, and T-SQL security. It is used by SQL clients, Power BI, and external tools.

blocks.content: Capability Spark SQL (Notebook) SQL Analytics Endpoint SELECT queries Yes Yes DDL (CREATE TABLE) Yes No (tables created via Spark/Pipeline/Dataflow) DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) Yes No (read-only for Lakehouse tables) Persistent views No (only temp views) Yes T-SQL security (GRANT, DENY) No Yes Connection from SSMS / JDBC / ODBC No (Notebook only) Yes Delta commands (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM) Yes No
In practice, data engineers use Spark SQL (in Notebooks) for all write operations and Delta management, and the SQL analytics endpoint for governed read access by SQL analysts and BI tools.

sections.title: Delta Table Management via SQL Delta tables require periodic maintenance to stay performant and cost-efficient. Spark SQL provides the commands for this maintenance. OPTIMIZE

blocks.content: %%sql -- Compact small files into larger ones OPTIMIZE orders
-- Compact and Z-Order by frequently filtered columns OPTIMIZE orders ZORDER BY (customer_id, order_date)
-- Optimize a specific partition only OPTIMIZE orders WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01'

OPTIMIZE should run regularly on tables that receive frequent small appends. Target file sizes of 128 MB to 1 GB. Z-ORDER co-locates related data in the same files, dramatically improving filter performance on the Z-Ordered columns. VACUUM
%%sql -- Remove unreferenced files older than 7 days (default retention) VACUUM orders
-- Remove unreferenced files older than 168 hours (7 days) VACUUM orders RETAIN 168 HOURS
-- Dry run: see what would be deleted without deleting VACUUM orders DRY RUN

Warning: VACUUM permanently deletes files. After vacuuming, time travel to versions that referenced those files will fail. Never set retention below 7 days in production. Always run DRY RUN first to understand the impact. DESCRIBE HISTORY
%%sql -- Show the full transaction history DESCRIBE HISTORY orders
-- Limit to the last 10 operations DESCRIBE HISTORY orders LIMIT 10

Each row in the history shows: version number, timestamp, operation type (WRITE, MERGE, DELETE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM), user, and operation metrics (rows added, rows removed, files added, files removed). Time Travel via SQL
%%sql -- Read a specific version SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 5
-- Read at a specific timestamp SELECT * FROM orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-01-15T00:00:00'
-- Compare current version with a previous version SELECT COUNT(*) AS current_count FROM orders UNION ALL SELECT COUNT(*) AS previous_count FROM orders VERSION AS OF 5
RESTORE
%%sql -- Restore a table to a previous version (undo bad changes) RESTORE TABLE orders TO VERSION AS OF 5
-- Restore to a previous timestamp RESTORE TABLE orders TO TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-01-15T00:00:00'

Important: RESTORE is a lifesaver when a pipeline writes bad data. Instead of debugging and manually fixing, you can restore the table to the last known good version in seconds. But RESTORE depends on the old files still existing — if VACUUM has deleted them, RESTORE will fail.

sections.title: Module 8 Checkpoint You now have the complete Spark SQL toolkit:

items: Running Spark SQL — %%sql magic for standalone queries, spark.sql() for Python integration, f-strings for parameterization
DDL — CREATE TABLE with explicit schema and CTAS, ALTER TABLE for column additions and renames, DROP TABLE, DESCRIBE and SHOW commands
DML — INSERT (values, query, overwrite), UPDATE with subqueries, DELETE with conditions; file-rewrite cost awareness
MERGE — the critical upsert command; WHEN MATCHED / WHEN NOT MATCHED / WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE; patterns for simple upsert, SCD1, conditional delete, and idempotent fact load
Views — temporary views (Spark session), global temp views (application), persistent views (SQL analytics endpoint); cross-language bridging
CTEs — WITH clause for readable multi-step queries; recursive CTEs for hierarchical data; materialization considerations
Window functions in SQL — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK; SUM/AVG OVER frames; LAG/LEAD; deduplication patterns; named windows
Cross-database queries — three-part naming for cross-Lakehouse and cross-Warehouse joins; Spark SQL vs. SQL analytics endpoint capabilities
Delta management — OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER, VACUUM with DRY RUN, DESCRIBE HISTORY, time travel with VERSION AS OF and TIMESTAMP AS OF, RESTORE for rollback

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Build a complete SQL-driven transformation workflow:

items: Create a staging table (staging_customers) using CREATE TABLE ... USING DELTA with an explicit schema. Insert sample rows using INSERT INTO.

Create a target dimension table (dim_customers). Write a MERGE statement that upserts from staging to target: insert new customers, update existing ones, and delete customers marked with is_deleted = true.
Create a CTE-based analytics query: calculate customer spend tiers (Platinum/Gold/Silver/Bronze), then aggregate tier-level metrics (count, revenue, avg orders). Use at least two CTEs.
Write a window function query: rank customers by total spend within each region. Show the running total per customer ordered by date. Use LAG to show the previous order amount.
Run DESCRIBE HISTORY on your dim_customers table. Identify the MERGE operation. Use VERSION AS OF to view the table before the merge.
Run OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER on customer_id. Run VACUUM DRY RUN and inspect what would be deleted. Then run VACUUM.
Create a persistent view in the SQL analytics endpoint that exposes a clean, analyst-friendly summary of your data. Verify it is queryable from an external SQL client.

blocks.content: You now command both PySpark and SQL.

Together, they give you complete control over every transformation, every table lifecycle operation, and every governance surface in the Fabric Lakehouse. You can choose the right tool for each task: PySpark for complex programmatic logic, SQL for declarative queries and Delta management.

In Module 9, we move to the Data Warehouse — Fabric’s T-SQL-native analytical engine with full DML, stored procedures, and enterprise SQL capabilities. You will learn when and how to use the Warehouse alongside or instead of the Lakehouse.

chapters.title: Data Warehouse — T-SQL Analytics Engine
chapters.description: The SQL-First Analytical Store Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Build and operate a Fabric Data Warehouse — T-SQL DDL and DML, stored procedures, security, data loading with COPY INTO, cross-database queries, and how the Warehouse complements the Lakehouse in a unified architecture.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 9 In Modules 3 through 8, the Lakehouse has been your primary item. You loaded data into it, transformed data with PySpark and Spark SQL, and queried it through the SQL analytics endpoint. The Lakehouse serves data engineers brilliantly. But not every team speaks PySpark. Many organizations have decades of investment in T-SQL skills, T-SQL stored procedures, and T-SQL-based tooling. For those teams, the Fabric Data Warehouse is the natural home — a fully managed, T-SQL-native analytical store that provides full DML, stored procedures, views, functions, and fine-grained security, all backed by the same OneLake Delta storage that powers the Lakehouse. The critical insight: the Warehouse and the Lakehouse store data in the same format (Delta) in the same place (OneLake). They are two interfaces to one storage layer. A table created in a Warehouse is a Delta table in OneLake. A Spark notebook can read it. A Lakehouse can create a shortcut to it. The data is the same. Only the access interface differs. This module covers: what the Warehouse is and how it differs from the Lakehouse, creating a Warehouse, T-SQL DDL and DML, data loading with COPY INTO, stored procedures for encapsulated logic, views and functions, security and access control, cross-database queries, monitoring and performance, and how to architect Lakehouse and Warehouse together.

sections.title: What Is the Fabric Data Warehouse? The Fabric Data Warehouse is a fully managed, T-SQL-native analytical engine that stores data as Delta tables in OneLake. It provides the full T-SQL surface that SQL professionals expect: CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, stored procedures, views, functions, schemas, and fine-grained permissions. Warehouse vs. Lakehouse — The Complete Comparison Criterion Lakehouse Data Warehouse Primary language PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, R T-SQL Write interface Spark (Notebooks, Spark Job Definitions) T-SQL (DML: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) Read interface Spark + auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint (read-only T-SQL) T-SQL (full read-write) Stored procedures Not supported Fully supported User-defined functions Not supported in SQL endpoint Supported (T-SQL scalar and table-valued) Schemas Implicit default schema Full schema support (dbo, staging, gold, etc.) Files/ landing zone Yes — unstructured file storage No — all data must be in tables Spark access Native Read-only via Spark connector (spark.read.synapsesql) Storage format Delta Lake in OneLake Delta Lake in OneLake (identical) Data distribution User-controlled partitioning Automatic round-robin distribution Table maintenance OPTIMIZE, VACUUM via Spark SQL Automatic (managed by Fabric) Power BI connection DirectLake via default semantic model DirectLake via semantic model Ideal persona Data engineers, data scientists SQL analysts, analytics engineers, BI developers

blocks.content: Important: The storage is identical. Both items write Delta tables to OneLake. The difference is entirely in the interface: which SQL dialect, which write mechanisms, which procedural capabilities. Choose based on your team’s skills and requirements, not based on storage architecture.

sections.title: Creating a Warehouse and Understanding Schemas Creating a Warehouse Creating a Warehouse is as simple as creating a Lakehouse: • Navigate to your workspace in the Fabric portal. • Click “+ New item” → “Warehouse.” • Enter a name following your naming convention (e.g., wh_sales, wh_gold_analytics). • Click “Create.” The Warehouse appears within seconds. Unlike the Lakehouse, there is no Files/ section — the Warehouse is a pure table store. You interact with it through a SQL editor built into the Fabric portal, or through any T-SQL client (SSMS, Azure Data Studio, DBeaver). Schemas The Warehouse supports T-SQL schemas for organizing tables into logical groups. The default schema is dbo, but you should create additional schemas to separate concerns:

blocks.content:
-- Create tables in specific schemas
CREATE TABLE staging.raw_orders (
    order_id       VARCHAR(50),
    customer_id    VARCHAR(50),
    amount         DECIMAL(18,2),
    order_date     DATE,
    load_timestamp DATETIME2 DEFAULT GETUTCDATE()
);

CREATE TABLE gold.fact_orders (
    order_id       VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    customer_id    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    amount         DECIMAL(18,2),
    amount_with_tax DECIMAL(18,2),
    order_date     DATE,
    order_year     INT,
    order_month    INT
);

Schemas are a governance mechanism. You can grant permissions at the schema level: analysts get SELECT on the gold schema but no access to staging. This is a fundamental advantage over the Lakehouse, where granular SQL-level permissions are limited to the SQL analytics endpoint. The SQL Editor The Warehouse opens in a SQL editor within the Fabric portal. This editor provides:

items: IntelliSense (auto-complete for table names, column names, T-SQL keywords).
Multiple query tabs (run several queries side by side).
Result grid with sorting, filtering, and export to CSV.
Execution plan visualization for query performance analysis.
Object explorer showing schemas, tables, views, stored procedures, and functions.

sections.title: T-SQL DDL in the Warehouse The Warehouse supports the full T-SQL DDL surface. If you have used SQL Server or Azure SQL, the syntax is familiar. CREATE TABLE

blocks.content:
-- Table with constraints and defaults
CREATE TABLE gold.dim_customers (
    customer_id    VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    customer_name  VARCHAR(200),
    email          VARCHAR(200),
    region         VARCHAR(50),
    segment        VARCHAR(50)    DEFAULT 'Standard',
    created_date   DATE,
    is_active      BIT            DEFAULT 1,
    last_updated   DATETIME2      DEFAULT GETUTCDATE()
);

-- Create table from query (CTAS) CREATE TABLE gold.monthly_revenue AS SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS order_year, MONTH(order_date) AS order_month, region, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM gold.fact_orders GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date), region;

Data Types Category Warehouse T-SQL Types Notes Integer INT, BIGINT, SMALLINT, TINYINT Use INT for most integer columns. BIGINT for large identifiers. Decimal DECIMAL(p,s), NUMERIC(p,s) Always specify precision and scale for financial amounts. DECIMAL(18,2) is standard. Floating point FLOAT, REAL Approximate types. Use DECIMAL for exact values (money). FLOAT for scientific data. String VARCHAR(n), CHAR(n) VARCHAR(n) for variable-length text. Fabric does not support NVARCHAR — use VARCHAR with UTF-8. Date/Time DATE, TIME, DATETIME2, DATETIMEOFFSET Use DATE for calendar dates. DATETIME2 for timestamps. Avoid legacy DATETIME. Boolean BIT 0 = false, 1 = true. Binary VARBINARY(n) Binary data. Rare in analytical workloads. Unique ID UNIQUEIDENTIFIER GUID/UUID. Use VARCHAR(36) as an alternative for cross-platform compatibility.
ALTER and DROP
-- Add a column ALTER TABLE gold.dim_customers ADD loyalty_score DECIMAL(5,2);
-- Drop a column ALTER TABLE gold.dim_customers DROP COLUMN loyalty_score;
-- Rename a table RENAME OBJECT gold.dim_customers TO dim_customers_v2;
-- Drop a table DROP TABLE IF EXISTS staging.temp_load;

Important: The Warehouse does not support ALTER COLUMN to change data types. To change a column’s type, you must create a new table with the desired schema and move the data. This is a deliberate trade-off for the distributed Delta storage engine.

sections.title: Loading Data — COPY INTO, INSERT, and Pipelines Data enters the Warehouse through several mechanisms. The right choice depends on the data source, volume, and frequency. COPY INTO — High-Performance Bulk Load COPY INTO is the fastest way to load data into a Warehouse table. It reads files from OneLake (or ADLS Gen2) and bulk-loads them into a table in a single, optimized operation.

blocks.content:
-- Load CSV files from a Lakehouse Files/ section
COPY INTO staging.raw_orders
FROM 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/
      sales_prod/lh_bronze_sales.Lakehouse/Files/raw/orders/'
WITH (
    FILE_TYPE = 'CSV',
    FIRSTROW = 2,          -- Skip header row
    FIELDTERMINATOR = ',',
    ROWTERMINATOR = '\n',
    ENCODING = 'UTF8'
);
-- Load Parquet files COPY INTO staging.raw_orders FROM 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/ sales_prod/lh_bronze_sales.Lakehouse/Files/raw/orders/' WITH ( FILE_TYPE = 'PARQUET' );

COPY INTO is optimized for large-volume loading. It parallelizes the read across multiple files and uses bulk insert mechanics for fast writes. For initial loads of millions of rows, COPY INTO is significantly faster than INSERT...SELECT from an external source. INSERT...SELECT For transformations within the Warehouse, INSERT...SELECT moves data between tables:

-- Transform staging data into gold table INSERT INTO gold.fact_orders SELECT order_id, customer_id, amount, amount * 1.1 AS amount_with_tax, order_date, YEAR(order_date) AS order_year, MONTH(order_date) AS order_month FROM staging.raw_orders WHERE amount > 0 AND order_date IS NOT NULL;

Pipeline Integration
Pipelines can load data into the Warehouse through:
•  Copy Activity — Move data from any supported source directly into a Warehouse table. Supports 300+ connectors.
•  Dataflow Gen2 — Configure a Warehouse table as the data destination for a Power Query transformation.
•  Stored Procedure Activity — Call a Warehouse stored procedure from a Pipeline to execute T-SQL transformation logic.
A common pattern: Pipeline Copy Activity loads raw data into a staging schema, then a Stored Procedure Activity calls a procedure that runs MERGE to upsert into gold tables.
Loading Methods Compared
Method
Best For
Performance
Notes
COPY INTO
Bulk loading files (CSV, Parquet) from OneLake / ADLS Gen2
Fastest for file-based loads
Requires files in a supported format at a known path.
INSERT...SELECT
Transforming data between Warehouse tables
Fast for in-Warehouse moves
Standard T-SQL. Good for staging → gold transformations.
Pipeline Copy Activity
Loading from external sources (SQL, SaaS, REST)
Good for medium volumes
300+ connectors. No T-SQL required.
Pipeline Stored Procedure
Executing Warehouse T-SQL from orchestration
N/A (orchestration)
Calls existing procedures. Enables parameterized pipelines.
Dataflow Gen2
Low-code ingestion with transformation
Moderate
Power Query for analyst-friendly loading.

sections.title: T-SQL DML — UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE The Warehouse provides full DML capabilities through T-SQL. This is the primary advantage over the Lakehouse’s read-only SQL analytics endpoint. UPDATE

blocks.content: -- Simple update UPDATE gold.dim_customers SET segment = 'Premium' WHERE customer_id IN ( SELECT customer_id FROM gold.fact_orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 100000 );

-- Update with a JOIN UPDATE t SET t.region = s.new_region, t.last_updated = GETUTCDATE() FROM gold.dim_customers t JOIN staging.customer_updates s ON t.customer_id = s.customer_id WHERE s.change_type = 'region_update';
DELETE
-- Delete with a condition DELETE FROM staging.raw_orders WHERE load_timestamp < DATEADD(day, -30, GETUTCDATE());
-- Delete based on another table DELETE t FROM gold.dim_customers t WHERE NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM gold.fact_orders f WHERE f.customer_id = t.customer_id );

MERGE
MERGE in the Warehouse uses standard T-SQL syntax, identical to SQL Server:

MERGE INTO gold.dim_customers AS target
USING staging.customer_updates AS source
ON target.customer_id = source.customer_id
WHEN MATCHED AND source.is_deleted = 1 THEN DELETE

WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET
        target.customer_name = source.customer_name,
        target.email         = source.email,
        target.region        = source.region,
        target.segment       = source.segment,
        target.is_active     = source.is_active,
        target.last_updated  = GETUTCDATE()

WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT (customer_id, customer_name, email, region, segment, created_date, is_active, last_updated) VALUES (source.customer_id, source.customer_name, source.email, source.region, source.segment, source.created_date, source.is_active, GETUTCDATE());
-- T-SQL MERGE requires a semicolon terminator

The Warehouse MERGE is functionally identical to Spark SQL MERGE (Module 8, Part 4). The syntax differs slightly (WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET vs. WHEN NOT MATCHED; semicolon terminator), but the patterns are the same: simple upsert, conditional delete, incremental fact load. Important: If your team’s primary skill is T-SQL, the Warehouse MERGE is a more natural fit than the Spark SQL or PySpark DeltaTable MERGE. The logic is identical; only the syntax changes. Choose based on team fluency.

sections.title: Stored Procedures and Functions Stored procedures are the Warehouse’s most significant capability advantage over the Lakehouse. They encapsulate T-SQL logic into reusable, parameterized, version-controlled units that can be called from Pipelines, from other procedures, or interactively. Creating a Stored Procedure

blocks.content: -- Step 1: MERGE staging into gold MERGE INTO gold.dim_customers AS t USING staging.customer_updates AS s ON t.customer_id = s.customer_id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.customer_name = s.customer_name, t.email = s.email, t.region = s.region, t.last_updated = GETUTCDATE() WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (customer_id, customer_name, email, region, created_date, is_active, last_updated) VALUES (s.customer_id, s.customer_name, s.email, s.region, @load_date, 1, GETUTCDATE());
-- Step 2: Log the operation INSERT INTO audit.refresh_log (table_name, load_date, row_count, status) VALUES ('dim_customers', @load_date, @@ROWCOUNT, 'SUCCESS'); END;
Calling Stored Procedures
-- Interactive call EXEC gold.usp_refresh_customers @load_date = '2026-01-15';
-- Call with default parameter EXEC gold.usp_refresh_customers;

-- From a Pipeline: use the Stored Procedure Activity
-- Select the Warehouse, select the procedure, pass parameters

Why Stored Procedures Matter
•  Encapsulation: All logic for refreshing a table is in one place. If the MERGE logic changes, you update the procedure, not every pipeline that references it.
•  Parameterization: Procedures accept parameters (@load_date, @mode, @source_table), making them reusable across different pipeline runs and scenarios.
•  Transaction control: Procedures can wrap multiple statements in explicit transactions (BEGIN TRANSACTION / COMMIT / ROLLBACK) for atomic multi-table operations.
•  Security: You can grant EXECUTE permission on a procedure without granting direct table access. Users can run the procedure but cannot write arbitrary SQL against the underlying tables.
•  Pipeline integration: The Pipeline Stored Procedure Activity calls a procedure directly, passing parameters from the pipeline. This is the cleanest way to integrate Warehouse transformations into automated workflows.
User-Defined Functions
-- Scalar function: returns a single value CREATE FUNCTION gold.fn_calculate_tax(@amount DECIMAL(18,2)) RETURNS DECIMAL(18,2) AS BEGIN RETURN @amount * 0.1; END;
-- Usage in a query SELECT order_id, amount, gold.fn_calculate_tax(amount) AS tax_amount FROM gold.fact_orders;

sections.title: Security and Access Control The Warehouse provides T-SQL-native security that goes beyond what the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint offers. For organizations with strict data governance requirements, this is often the deciding factor for choosing a Warehouse. Security Layers Layer Mechanism Granularity Workspace Workspace roles (Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer) All items in the workspace Item Item-level sharing and permissions Single Warehouse Schema GRANT/DENY on schemas All tables in a schema Table GRANT/DENY SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on tables Single table Column Column-level permissions via GRANT SELECT on specific columns Specific columns within a table Row Row-Level Security (RLS) via security policies Specific rows based on user identity Procedure GRANT EXECUTE on stored procedures Execution rights without table access

blocks.content: Schema-Level Permissions
Row-Level Security (RLS) RLS restricts which rows a user can see based on their identity. This is essential for multi-tenant or regional data access:

-- Step 1: Create a function that defines the filter
CREATE FUNCTION gold.fn_region_filter(@region VARCHAR(50))
RETURNS TABLE
WITH SCHEMABINDING
AS
RETURN
    SELECT 1 AS result
    WHERE @region = USER_NAME()     -- Map user to their region
       OR USER_NAME() = 'admin';    -- Admins see all rows
-- Step 2: Create a security policy that binds the function CREATE SECURITY POLICY gold.RegionFilter ADD FILTER PREDICATE gold.fn_region_filter(region) ON gold.fact_orders WITH (STATE = ON);

When RLS is active, a user from the “North” region only sees rows where region = ‘North’. The filter is transparent — the user’s SELECT * returns only their rows without any explicit WHERE clause. This works for all query tools: SQL editor, SSMS, Power BI, JDBC. Column-Level Security

Column-level security prevents analysts from seeing sensitive data (email, phone, SSN) without restricting their access to the rest of the table. It is simpler than creating views that exclude sensitive columns and provides stronger enforcement.

sections.title: Cross-Database Queries Fabric’s cross-database query capability lets you join data across Warehouses and Lakehouses using three-part naming. This is one of the platform’s most powerful integration features. Querying Across Items

blocks.content: -- Join a Warehouse table with a Lakehouse table SELECT w.order_id, w.amount, l.customer_name, l.region FROM gold.fact_orders w JOIN lh_gold_sales.dbo.dim_customers l ON w.customer_id = l.customer_id;

-- Join two Warehouse tables from different Warehouses SELECT s.order_id, s.amount, p.product_name, p.category FROM wh_sales.gold.fact_orders s JOIN wh_products.gold.dim_products p ON s.product_id = p.product_id;

Cross-database queries work within the same workspace. They enable a federated architecture where different teams own different Warehouses and Lakehouses, but analysts can join across them seamlessly. Practical Architecture Patterns Pattern 1: Lakehouse Bronze/Silver + Warehouse Gold. Data engineers use Lakehouses and PySpark for raw ingestion and intermediate transformations (Bronze and Silver layers). The Gold layer lives in a Warehouse, where SQL analysts query governed tables through stored procedures and views. Cross-database queries connect the two. Pattern 2: Multiple Domain Warehouses. Each business domain (Sales, Finance, HR) has its own Warehouse. Cross-database queries allow ad-hoc analysis across domains without data duplication. Pattern 3: Warehouse + Lakehouse Shortcuts. The Warehouse owns Gold tables. Lakehouses create shortcuts to those tables for Spark-based ML and data science workflows. The data stays in one place; both interfaces access it.

sections.title: Monitoring, Performance, and Table Maintenance Monitoring Query Performance The Warehouse provides several tools for monitoring and debugging: • Query Insights: Built-in DMVs (Dynamic Management Views) expose query execution history, duration, resource consumption, and wait statistics. Query the queryinsights schema for historical analysis. • Execution Plans: The SQL editor’s “Explain” button shows the estimated execution plan. Look for table scans (bad), index usage, and distribution-aware operations. • Capacity Metrics app: Monitor Warehouse CU consumption at the capacity level. Identify heavy queries and peak usage periods.

blocks.content: -- Query recent execution history SELECT distributed_statement_id, start_time, end_time, DATEDIFF(second, start_time, end_time) AS duration_seconds, command FROM queryinsights.exec_requests_history WHERE start_time > DATEADD(hour, -24, GETUTCDATE()) ORDER BY duration_seconds DESC;

Performance Best Practices
•  Write selective queries. Always include WHERE clauses to minimize data scanned. Delta Lake’s file-level statistics enable pruning, but only if you filter.
•  Avoid SELECT *. Specify only the columns you need. Delta’s columnar format means unused columns are not read from disk.
•  Use CTAS for large transformations. CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT is more efficient than INSERT INTO ... SELECT for full table rebuilds because it avoids row-by-row insert overhead.
•  Prefer MERGE over separate UPDATE + INSERT. MERGE processes the source once and applies all changes atomically. Separate statements process the source multiple times.
•  Monitor long-running queries with queryinsights. Set alerts for queries exceeding expected thresholds.
Table Maintenance
Unlike the Lakehouse (where you manually run OPTIMIZE and VACUUM via Spark SQL), the Warehouse handles table maintenance automatically:
•  Automatic file compaction: The Warehouse engine periodically compacts small Delta files into larger ones. You do not need to run OPTIMIZE.
•  Automatic statistics management: The engine maintains column statistics for query optimization. You do not need to create or update statistics manually.
•  V-Order applied by default: All Warehouse writes use V-Order optimization for optimal read performance.
Tip: The automatic maintenance means you spend less time managing the Warehouse compared to a Lakehouse. This is a significant operational advantage for teams that do not have dedicated data engineering staff for table maintenance tasks.

sections.title: Module 9 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: What the Warehouse is — a T-SQL-native analytical engine storing Delta tables in OneLake; same storage as Lakehouse, different interface
Warehouse vs. Lakehouse — full 13-point comparison; choose by team skill (T-SQL vs. PySpark) and write requirements (DML, stored procedures)
Schemas — organizational containers (staging, gold, audit) with schema-level permissions for governance
T-SQL DDL — CREATE TABLE with constraints and defaults, data types reference, ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE, CTAS
Data loading — COPY INTO for high-performance bulk loads from OneLake/ADLS Gen2, INSERT...SELECT for in-Warehouse transforms, Pipeline integration with Copy Activity and Stored Procedure Activity
T-SQL DML — UPDATE with joins, DELETE with subqueries, MERGE with full upsert/delete syntax; T-SQL MERGE vs. Spark SQL MERGE comparison
Stored procedures and functions — encapsulated logic, parameterization, transaction control, Pipeline integration, and scalar/table-valued functions
Security — schema-level GRANT/DENY, table-level permissions, column-level security, Row-Level Security with security policies and filter predicates
Cross-database queries — three-part naming across Warehouses and Lakehouses; federated architecture patterns (Lakehouse Bronze/Silver + Warehouse Gold)
Monitoring and maintenance — queryinsights DMVs, execution plans, automatic compaction, automatic statistics, V-Order defaults

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Build a complete Warehouse workflow:

items: Create a Warehouse named wh_workshop in your workspace. Create three schemas: staging, gold, and audit.
Create a staging table (staging.raw_orders) with an explicit schema. Use COPY INTO to load a CSV or Parquet file from your Lakehouse’s Files/ section.
Create a gold table (gold.fact_orders) with computed columns (amount_with_tax, order_year, order_month). Write an INSERT...SELECT to transform and load data from staging to gold.
Create a dim_customers table in the gold schema. Write a MERGE statement that upserts from a staging source: insert new rows, update existing ones, delete customers marked for removal.
Create a stored procedure (gold.usp_refresh_orders) that encapsulates the full load: MERGE from staging to gold, log the operation to an audit table. Call the procedure with EXEC.
Set up security: create a schema-level GRANT that gives an analyst group SELECT on gold but denies access to staging. Test that the analyst can query gold tables but not staging tables.
Write a cross-database query that joins your Warehouse’s gold.fact_orders with your Lakehouse’s dim_customers table. Verify the join works across items.
Query the queryinsights.exec_requests_history to find your slowest query in the last hour. Examine the execution plan and identify optimization opportunities.

blocks.content: You now command both the Lakehouse and the Warehouse.

Together, they give you the complete analytical storage layer in Fabric: Lakehouses for data engineering with PySpark, Warehouses for SQL analytics with T-SQL, both reading and writing Delta tables in OneLake. Choose the right tool for each workload, and use cross-database queries to connect them seamlessly.

In Module 10, we go deeper into T-SQL Advanced Patterns — complex queries, dynamic SQL, error handling in stored procedures, slowly changing dimensions, and production-grade Warehouse patterns that separate junior SQL from senior analytics engineering.

chapters.title: T-SQL Analytics in Fabric
chapters.description: Advanced SQL Patterns for Production Analytics Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Master advanced T-SQL patterns for analytics across Fabric’s SQL surfaces — advanced joins and set operations, window function mastery, CTEs and recursion, PIVOT/UNPIVOT, JSON handling, OPENROWSET for external data, query performance tuning, and Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 patterns.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 10 Module 9 gave you the Warehouse: the T-SQL-native engine with full DDL, DML, stored procedures, and security. You can create tables, load data, write MERGE statements, and govern access. That is the infrastructure. This module is about what you build on that infrastructure. Advanced T-SQL patterns are what separate a database user from an analytics engineer. CROSS APPLY to unnest nested data. Window functions with frame specifications for running calculations.
