Recursive CTEs for hierarchical queries. PIVOT for cross-tab reports. JSON parsing for semi-structured data. OPENROWSET for querying files without loading them. SCD Type 2 for historical dimension tracking. These patterns apply to all three SQL surfaces in Fabric: the Warehouse (full DML), the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint (read-only), and the SQL Database (OLTP). The syntax is the same T-SQL dialect. The difference is which surface supports which operations. The single most important skill in this module is knowing which pattern to reach for. When you see a problem — unnest a JSON array, compare current vs. previous period, deduplicate, build a hierarchy — the right pattern should come to mind immediately. This module gives you that pattern library.

sections.title: The Three SQL Surfaces in Fabric Before diving into advanced patterns, you need clarity on where each pattern can run. Fabric has three distinct T-SQL surfaces, and they differ in capability.

blocks.content: Surface Engine DML Support Stored Procedures Best For Warehouse Fabric SQL (distributed) Full: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE Yes Analytical workloads, star schemas, Gold layer, governed reporting. Lakehouse SQL Analytics Endpoint Fabric SQL (distributed, read-only) Read-only: SELECT only. No INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE. No Ad-hoc queries on Lakehouse Delta tables. Views for Power BI. Cross-database reads. SQL Database SQL Server engine (single-node OLTP) Full: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE + triggers, indexes Yes + triggers, jobs Transactional / operational workloads. Application backends. Auto-mirrors to OneLake.

Every T-SQL pattern in this module works on all three surfaces for SELECT queries. DML patterns (MERGE, UPDATE) only work on the Warehouse and SQL Database. When a pattern has surface-specific limitations, this module calls them out. Choosing the Right Surface Use the Warehouse when you need full DML, stored procedures, and governed schema-level security for analytical data. Use the Lakehouse SQL Analytics Endpoint when you want to query Lakehouse Delta tables with T-SQL without moving data. Ideal for ad-hoc analysis and creating views for Power BI DirectLake. Use the SQL Database when your workload is transactional (OLTP): high-frequency inserts, point lookups, application-serving queries. Data automatically mirrors to OneLake for analytics.

sections.title: Advanced Joins and Set Operations Module 8 covered standard joins (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER). This section adds the advanced join patterns that solve problems standard joins cannot. CROSS APPLY and OUTER APPLY APPLY is T-SQL’s operator for correlated subqueries that return multiple rows. It evaluates a table-valued expression for each row of the outer table. Think of it as a “for-each join.”

blocks.content: -- CROSS APPLY: for each customer, get their top 3 orders SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name, top_orders.order_id, top_orders.amount FROM gold.dim_customers c CROSS APPLY ( SELECT TOP 3 o.order_id, o.amount FROM gold.fact_orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id ORDER BY o.amount DESC ) AS top_orders;

-- OUTER APPLY: same, but include customers with no orders (NULLs) SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name, top_orders.order_id, top_orders.amount FROM gold.dim_customers c OUTER APPLY ( SELECT TOP 3 o.order_id, o.amount FROM gold.fact_orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id ORDER BY o.amount DESC ) AS top_orders;

CROSS APPLY is like INNER JOIN for table-valued expressions — rows without matches are excluded. OUTER APPLY is like LEFT JOIN — rows without matches produce NULLs. APPLY is essential for unnesting arrays, calling table-valued functions per row, and “Top N per group” queries. Set Operations Set operations combine result sets from two or more queries:
-- UNION ALL: stack all rows (including duplicates) SELECT order_id, amount, 'Online' AS channel FROM online_orders UNION ALL SELECT order_id, amount, 'Retail' AS channel FROM retail_orders;
-- UNION: stack rows, deduplicate (slower than UNION ALL) SELECT customer_id FROM high_value_customers UNION SELECT customer_id FROM loyalty_members;

-- EXCEPT: rows in left but NOT in right
SELECT customer_id FROM dim_customers
EXCEPT
SELECT customer_id FROM fact_orders;
-- Result: customers who have never ordered

-- INTERSECT: rows in BOTH left and right
SELECT customer_id FROM high_value_customers
INTERSECT
SELECT customer_id FROM loyalty_members;
-- Result: customers who are both high-value AND loyalty members

Tip: EXCEPT and INTERSECT are cleaner alternatives to LEFT ANTI JOIN and SEMI JOIN patterns. They compare entire rows (all columns) unless you project specific columns. Use them when you need set-based membership logic.

sections.title: Window Functions Masterclass Module 8 introduced window functions in Spark SQL. This section deepens that knowledge with T-SQL-specific syntax, frame specifications, and advanced patterns that arise in production analytics. Frame Specifications: ROWS vs. RANGE The frame defines which rows relative to the current row are included in the window calculation. This is the most misunderstood aspect of window functions.

blocks.content: Frame Behavior Example ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW Physical rows: counts rows by position regardless of value. Running total: counts each row exactly once. RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW Logical rows: includes all rows with the SAME ORDER BY value as the current row. Running total: if two rows have the same date, both are included in each other’s calculation. ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW Exactly 3 rows: current + 2 before. 3-period moving average. ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING Current row through the last row in the partition. Reverse running total. ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING 3-row centered window. Centered moving average (smoothing).

Warning: The default frame when ORDER BY is specified is RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW — not ROWS. This means ties are grouped together, which can produce unexpected results for SUM and AVG. Always specify ROWS explicitly when you want physical row counting. Advanced Window Patterns
-- Running total with explicit ROWS frame SELECT order_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER ( ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS running_total FROM gold.fact_orders;

-- Year-over-year comparison with LAG SELECT order_year, order_month, total_revenue, LAG(total_revenue, 12) OVER (ORDER BY order_year, order_month) AS revenue_same_month_last_year, total_revenue - LAG(total_revenue, 12) OVER (ORDER BY order_year, order_month) AS yoy_change FROM gold.monthly_revenue;

-- Percent of group total (no ORDER BY = entire partition) SELECT region, customer_id, total_spent, CAST(total_spent AS FLOAT) / SUM(total_spent) OVER (PARTITION BY region) * 100 AS pct_of_region_total FROM gold.customer_summary;

-- Gap detection: find missing dates SELECT order_date, LEAD(order_date) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_date, DATEDIFF(day, order_date, LEAD(order_date) OVER (ORDER BY order_date)) AS gap_days FROM ( SELECT DISTINCT order_date FROM gold.fact_orders ) dates WHERE DATEDIFF(day, order_date, LEAD(order_date) OVER (ORDER BY order_date)) > 1;
FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE

-- First and last order amount per customer SELECT DISTINCT customer_id, FIRST_VALUE(amount) OVER ( PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING ) AS first_order_amount, LAST_VALUE(amount) OVER ( PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING ) AS last_order_amount FROM gold.fact_orders;

Important: LAST_VALUE requires an explicit frame that extends to UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING. Without it, the default frame stops at CURRENT ROW, and LAST_VALUE just returns the current row’s value — which is almost never what you want.

sections.title: PIVOT, UNPIVOT, and Dynamic Crosstabs PIVOT rotates rows into columns. UNPIVOT does the reverse. These operations are essential for building cross-tab reports, reshaping data for BI, and normalizing denormalized sources. PIVOT

blocks.content:
-- Revenue by region as columns
SELECT product_category, [North], [South], [East], [West]
FROM (
    SELECT product_category, region, amount
    FROM gold.fact_orders
) AS source_data
PIVOT (
    SUM(amount)
    FOR region IN ([North], [South], [East], [West])
) AS pivoted;

-- Result:
-- product_category | North  | South  | East   | West
-- Electronics      | 45000  | 32000  | 28000  | 51000
-- Clothing         | 12000  | 15000  | 9000   | 11000
UNPIVOT

-- Convert columnar report back to rows
SELECT product_category, region, revenue
FROM (
    SELECT product_category, [North], [South], [East], [West]
    FROM gold.revenue_crosstab
) AS source_data
UNPIVOT (
    revenue FOR region IN ([North], [South], [East], [West])
) AS unpivoted;

-- Result:
-- product_category | region | revenue
-- Electronics      | North  | 45000
-- Electronics      | South  | 32000
-- ...
Dynamic PIVOT Static PIVOT requires you to hard-code the column values. When the values are unknown or change over time (e.g., new regions appear), you need dynamic PIVOT using dynamic SQL:
-- Build the column list dynamically DECLARE @columns NVARCHAR(MAX); DECLARE @sql NVARCHAR(MAX);

SELECT @columns = STRING_AGG(QUOTENAME(region), ', ')
FROM (SELECT DISTINCT region FROM gold.fact_orders) AS regions;

SET @sql = N' SELECT product_category, ' + @columns + N' FROM ( SELECT product_category, region, amount FROM gold.fact_orders ) AS source_data PIVOT ( SUM(amount) FOR region IN (' + @columns + N') ) AS pivoted;';
EXEC sp_executesql @sql;

Warning: Dynamic SQL (sp_executesql) is powerful but carries SQL injection risk. Always use QUOTENAME() around values injected into column identifiers. Never concatenate user-supplied strings directly into dynamic SQL.

sections.title: JSON and Semi-Structured Data in T-SQL Modern data pipelines frequently ingest JSON from REST APIs, event streams, and NoSQL sources. T-SQL in Fabric provides native functions for parsing, querying, and transforming JSON without leaving SQL. Core JSON Functions Function Purpose Returns JSON_VALUE(json, path) Extract a scalar value (string, number, boolean) NVARCHAR(4000) — scalar only JSON_QUERY(json, path) Extract an object or array NVARCHAR(MAX) — JSON fragment ISJSON(string) Test if a string is valid JSON 1 (valid) or 0 (invalid) OPENJSON(json) Parse JSON into a relational rowset Table with key, value, type columns FOR JSON PATH Convert query results to JSON JSON string

blocks.content: Extracting Scalar Values

SELECT
    event_id,
    JSON_VALUE(event_data, '$.user_id')     AS user_id,
    JSON_VALUE(event_data, '$.action')       AS action,
    JSON_VALUE(event_data, '$.page.url')     AS page_url
FROM staging.raw_events
WHERE ISJSON(event_data) = 1;
Unnesting Arrays with OPENJSON OPENJSON is the key to flattening nested JSON arrays into relational rows:

SELECT
    JSON_VALUE(raw_json, '$.order_id') AS order_id,
    item.product,
    item.qty,
    item.price
FROM staging.raw_orders_json
CROSS APPLY OPENJSON(raw_json, '$.items')
    WITH (
        product VARCHAR(100) '$.product',
        qty     INT          '$.qty',
        price   DECIMAL(10,2) '$.price'
    ) AS item;

OPENJSON with a WITH clause maps JSON properties to typed relational columns. Combined with CROSS APPLY, it unnests one JSON array row per element — turning a single JSON document with 5 line items into 5 relational rows. Generating JSON Output
-- Convert query results to JSON SELECT order_id, customer_id, amount, order_date FROM gold.fact_orders WHERE region = 'North' FOR JSON PATH;
-- Result: [{"order_id":"O001","customer_id":"C001",...}, ...]

Tip: JSON parsing is a common pattern for landing API responses. The Pipeline Copy Activity can land raw JSON into a Lakehouse Files/ section or a Warehouse staging table. Then T-SQL JSON functions parse and flatten it into relational tables for the Gold layer.

sections.title: OPENROWSET and External Data Access OPENROWSET lets you query files directly from T-SQL without loading them into a table first. It is a federated query mechanism: the SQL engine reads Parquet, CSV, or Delta files from OneLake or ADLS Gen2 and returns them as a relational result set. Querying Parquet Files

blocks.content: -- Query Parquet files from a Lakehouse Files/ section SELECT * FROM OPENROWSET( BULK 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/ my_workspace/lh_bronze.Lakehouse/Files/raw/orders/*.parquet', FORMAT = 'PARQUET' ) AS orders WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01';

-- Query with explicit schema
SELECT *
FROM OPENROWSET(
    BULK 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/
          my_workspace/lh_bronze.Lakehouse/Files/raw/orders/*.parquet',
    FORMAT = 'PARQUET'
) WITH (
    order_id    VARCHAR(50),
    customer_id VARCHAR(50),
    amount      DECIMAL(18,2),
    order_date  DATE
) AS orders;
Querying CSV Files

SELECT *
FROM OPENROWSET(
    BULK 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/
          my_workspace/lh_bronze.Lakehouse/Files/raw/customers/*.csv',
    FORMAT = 'CSV',
    PARSER_VERSION = '2.0',
    HEADER_ROW = TRUE,
    FIELDTERMINATOR = ','
) AS customers;
Querying Delta Tables
-- Query Delta format directly via OPENROWSET SELECT * FROM OPENROWSET( BULK 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/ my_workspace/lh_bronze.Lakehouse/Tables/orders/', FORMAT = 'DELTA' ) AS orders;
When to Use OPENROWSET

items: Data exploration: Preview files before loading them into a table. Check schema, data quality, and volume without running a Notebook or Pipeline.
Ad-hoc federated queries: Join files with Warehouse tables for one-off analysis without creating staging tables.
Creating views over files: Create a view that wraps an OPENROWSET query. Analysts query the view as if it were a table, but the data lives as files in OneLake.

blocks.content: -- Create a view over external files CREATE VIEW staging.v_raw_orders AS SELECT * FROM OPENROWSET( BULK 'https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/ my_workspace/lh_bronze.Lakehouse/Files/raw/orders/*.parquet', FORMAT = 'PARQUET' ) WITH ( order_id VARCHAR(50), customer_id VARCHAR(50), amount DECIMAL(18,2), order_date DATE ) AS orders;

Important: OPENROWSET reads files on every execution — there is no caching. For frequently queried data, load it into a table with COPY INTO or INSERT...SELECT for better performance. Use OPENROWSET for exploration and low-frequency queries.

sections.title: Query Performance Tuning Advanced SQL patterns are useless if they run slowly. This section covers the diagnostic and optimization techniques for T-SQL in Fabric. Reading Execution Plans The Warehouse SQL editor provides estimated execution plans (click “Explain” before running). Key operators to look for: • Table Scan / Full Scan: The engine reads every file in the table. If your query has a WHERE clause that should filter data, a full scan means the filter is not being pushed down. Check for implicit type conversions or functions on the filter column that prevent pushdown. • Shuffle (Exchange): Data is redistributed across nodes for joins or aggregations. Shuffles are expensive. Minimize them by structuring joins to use the same distribution key. • Broadcast Move: A small table is broadcast to all nodes for a join. This is efficient for small dimension tables. If a large table is being broadcast, the optimizer misjudged the table size. • Sort: Data is sorted for ORDER BY, SortMergeJoin, or window functions. Sorting is expensive on large datasets. Statistics and File Pruning Delta Lake stores min/max statistics per column per file. When your WHERE clause filters on a column, the engine can skip files where the filter value falls outside the min/max range. This is called file pruning. • File pruning works best on columns with natural ordering (dates, sequential IDs). • V-Order (applied by default in the Warehouse) further optimizes file layout for read performance. • If queries are slow despite narrow filters, the table may have too many small files. Let the Warehouse’s automatic compaction handle this, or load with CTAS to create optimally sized files. Result Set Caching The Warehouse can cache query results for repeated identical queries. When caching is enabled, re-running the same query returns the cached result instantly without re-scanning data.

blocks.content:
-- Result set caching is managed at the Warehouse level
-- Cached results are automatically invalidated when
-- underlying data changes (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE)

Performance Anti-Patterns Anti-Pattern Why It Hurts Fix SELECT * FROM large_table Reads all columns. Delta is columnar — unreferenced columns are free to skip. SELECT only the columns you need. Functions on filter columns: WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2026 Prevents file pruning. The engine cannot use min/max stats on a computed value. Rewrite as: WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01' AND order_date < '2027-01-01'. Implicit type conversions in joins Joining INT to VARCHAR forces a conversion on every row, preventing pushdown. Ensure join columns have matching types. ORDER BY without TOP or pagination Sorts the entire result set. Expensive and usually unnecessary. Remove ORDER BY unless the consumer requires sorted output. Correlated subqueries in SELECT Executes the subquery once per row. Extremely slow on large tables. Rewrite as a JOIN or window function.

sections.title: Temporal Patterns — SCD Type 2 in T-SQL Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCDs) are the cornerstone of dimensional modeling. SCD Type 2 is the history-preserving pattern: when a dimension attribute changes, you do not overwrite the old value. Instead, you expire the current row and insert a new row with the updated value. This preserves the full history of every change. SCD Type 2 Table Structure

blocks.content:
CREATE TABLE gold.dim_customers_scd2 (
    customer_sk     INT IDENTITY(1,1),  -- Surrogate key
    customer_id     VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- Business key
    customer_name   VARCHAR(200),
    email           VARCHAR(200),
    region          VARCHAR(50),
    segment         VARCHAR(50),
    effective_from  DATE NOT NULL,
    effective_to    DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT '9999-12-31',
    is_current      BIT NOT NULL DEFAULT 1
);

•  customer_sk: The surrogate key. Auto-incrementing. Used for joins with fact tables. Each version of a customer gets a unique surrogate key.
•  customer_id: The business key (natural key). Stays the same across all versions of the same customer.
•  effective_from / effective_to: The validity period. The current row has effective_to = 9999-12-31. When a change arrives, the current row is expired (effective_to set to yesterday) and a new row is inserted.
•  is_current: A convenience flag. 1 = current version, 0 = historical version. Simplifies queries that only need the latest state.
The SCD2 MERGE Pattern

-- Step 1: Identify changes (new customers + changed attributes)
-- Step 2: Expire current rows that have changes
-- Step 3: Insert new rows for changes and brand-new customers

-- Step 1: Find rows with changes WITH changes AS ( SELECT s.customer_id, s.customer_name, s.email, s.region, s.segment FROM staging.customer_updates s JOIN gold.dim_customers_scd2 t ON s.customer_id = t.customer_id AND t.is_current = 1 WHERE s.customer_name <> t.customer_name OR s.email <> t.email OR s.region <> t.region OR s.segment <> t.segment )
-- Step 2: Expire old rows UPDATE gold.dim_customers_scd2 SET effective_to = CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE), is_current = 0 WHERE customer_id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM changes) AND is_current = 1;

-- Step 3a: Insert new versions for changed customers INSERT INTO gold.dim_customers_scd2 (customer_id, customer_name, email, region, segment, effective_from, effective_to, is_current) SELECT customer_id, customer_name, email, region, segment, CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE), '9999-12-31', 1 FROM changes;

-- Step 3b: Insert brand-new customers (not in target at all) INSERT INTO gold.dim_customers_scd2 (customer_id, customer_name, email, region, segment, effective_from, effective_to, is_current) SELECT s.customer_id, s.customer_name, s.email, s.region, s.segment, CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE), '9999-12-31', 1 FROM staging.customer_updates s WHERE NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM gold.dim_customers_scd2 t WHERE t.customer_id = s.customer_id );
Querying SCD2 Tables
-- Current state only (equivalent to SCD1 view) SELECT * FROM gold.dim_customers_scd2 WHERE is_current = 1;
-- Point-in-time lookup: what was the customer's region on 2025-06-15? SELECT * FROM gold.dim_customers_scd2 WHERE customer_id = 'C001' AND '2025-06-15' BETWEEN effective_from AND effective_to;

-- Join fact table to SCD2 dimension (point-in-time) SELECT f.order_id, f.amount, d.customer_name, d.region FROM gold.fact_orders f JOIN gold.dim_customers_scd2 d ON f.customer_id = d.customer_id AND f.order_date BETWEEN d.effective_from AND d.effective_to;

Important: The point-in-time join (fact.date BETWEEN dim.effective_from AND dim.effective_to) ensures that each fact row joins to the dimension version that was current when the event occurred. This is the correct way to analyze historical data with SCD2 dimensions.

sections.title: Production T-SQL Patterns This section collects patterns that appear repeatedly in production analytics engineering. Pattern: Running Deduplication in a Stored Procedure

blocks.content:
CREATE PROCEDURE staging.usp_dedup_orders
AS
BEGIN
    -- Keep latest version per order_id based on updated_at
    WITH ranked AS (
        SELECT *,
               ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
                   PARTITION BY order_id
                   ORDER BY updated_at DESC
               ) AS rn
        FROM staging.raw_orders
    )
    DELETE FROM staging.raw_orders
    WHERE order_id IN (
        SELECT order_id FROM ranked WHERE rn > 1
    );
Pattern: Calendar/Date Dimension Generator

-- Generate a date dimension with a recursive CTE
WITH dates AS (
    SELECT CAST('2020-01-01' AS DATE) AS calendar_date
    UNION ALL
    SELECT DATEADD(day, 1, calendar_date)
    FROM dates
    WHERE calendar_date < '2030-12-31'
)
SELECT
    calendar_date,
    YEAR(calendar_date)           AS calendar_year,
    MONTH(calendar_date)          AS calendar_month,
    DAY(calendar_date)            AS calendar_day,
    DATENAME(weekday, calendar_date) AS day_name,
    DATENAME(month, calendar_date)   AS month_name,
    DATEPART(quarter, calendar_date) AS calendar_quarter,
    DATEPART(week, calendar_date)    AS week_of_year,
    CASE WHEN DATENAME(weekday, calendar_date)
         IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday') THEN 0 ELSE 1
    END AS is_weekday
INTO gold.dim_calendar
FROM dates
OPTION (MAXRECURSION 10000);
Pattern: Data Quality Check Framework
-- Check 1: Row count SET @row_count = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gold.fact_orders WHERE order_date = @load_date);
-- Check 2: NULL primary keys SET @null_count = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gold.fact_orders WHERE order_id IS NULL);
-- Check 3: Duplicate primary keys SET @dupe_count = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ( SELECT order_id FROM gold.fact_orders GROUP BY order_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 ) AS dupes);

-- Log results INSERT INTO audit.data_quality_log (table_name, check_date, row_count, null_pk_count, dupe_pk_count, status) VALUES (@table_name, @load_date, @row_count, @null_count, @dupe_count, CASE WHEN @null_count = 0 AND @dupe_count = 0 THEN 'PASSED' ELSE 'FAILED' END);
-- Fail if quality checks fail IF @null_count > 0 OR @dupe_count > 0 THROW 50001, 'Data quality check FAILED', 1; END;
Pattern: Parameterized Dynamic Date Ranges

-- Stored procedure with flexible date range CREATE PROCEDURE gold.usp_revenue_report @start_date DATE = NULL, @end_date DATE = NULL AS BEGIN SET @start_date = ISNULL(@start_date, DATEADD(month, -3, CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE))); SET @end_date = ISNULL(@end_date, CAST(GETUTCDATE() AS DATE));

SELECT
        YEAR(order_date) AS yr, MONTH(order_date) AS mo,
        region,
        SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
        COUNT(*) AS order_count,
        COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers
    FROM gold.fact_orders
    WHERE order_date BETWEEN @start_date AND @end_date
    GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date), region
    ORDER BY yr, mo, region;
END;

sections.title: Module 10 Checkpoint You now have the complete advanced T-SQL toolkit:

items: Three SQL surfaces — Warehouse (full DML), Lakehouse SQL endpoint (read-only), SQL Database (OLTP with auto-mirror); capability differences and when to use each
Advanced joins and set operations — CROSS APPLY / OUTER APPLY for correlated table-valued expressions and Top-N-per-group; EXCEPT and INTERSECT for set-based membership logic

Window functions mastery — ROWS vs. RANGE frame specifications with the default-frame gotcha; year-over-year with LAG; percent of group total; gap detection; FIRST_VALUE / LAST_VALUE with correct frames
PIVOT and UNPIVOT — static pivot with hard-coded values, UNPIVOT for normalization, dynamic PIVOT with STRING_AGG and sp_executesql for unknown column values
JSON handling — JSON_VALUE for scalars, JSON_QUERY for fragments, OPENJSON with CROSS APPLY for array unnesting, WITH clause for typed relational output, FOR JSON PATH for generation
OPENROWSET — federated queries on Parquet, CSV, and Delta files from OneLake without loading; views over external files; exploration vs. production usage
Query performance tuning — execution plan reading, file pruning via statistics, result set caching, and five anti-patterns with fixes
SCD Type 2 — table structure (surrogate key, effective dates, is_current flag), the expire-and-insert MERGE pattern, current-state queries, and point-in-time joins with fact tables
Production patterns — stored procedure deduplication, recursive CTE date dimension generator, data quality check framework with THROW, and parameterized dynamic date ranges

blocks.content: T-SQL Analytics Challenge Solve these five progressively harder problems using the advanced T-SQL patterns from this module:

items: Challenge 1 (APPLY): Write a query that returns each customer and their top 3 orders by amount using CROSS APPLY. Include customers with fewer than 3 orders using OUTER APPLY.

Challenge 2 (Window + Frame): Calculate a 7-day moving average of daily revenue and the year-over-year change for each month. Use explicit ROWS frames. Identify the default-frame gotcha by comparing ROWS vs. RANGE for a running total where ties exist.

Challenge 3 (PIVOT + JSON): A staging table has a JSON column containing order line items as an array. Unnest the JSON array with OPENJSON + CROSS APPLY into relational rows. Then PIVOT the result so each product category is a column, with SUM(amount) as the value.

Challenge 4 (OPENROWSET + CTE): Write a federated query using OPENROWSET to read Parquet files from your Lakehouse Files/ section. Join the result with a Warehouse gold table using a CTE. Calculate running totals with a window function.

Challenge 5 (SCD Type 2): Implement a complete SCD2 flow: create the dimension table, load initial data, then process a staging update that contains one new customer, one changed customer (region changed), and one unchanged customer. Verify that the history is preserved with a point-in-time query.

blocks.content: You have now mastered both sides of the SQL equation.

Spark SQL (Module 8) gives you SQL on the Spark engine with Delta management. T-SQL (Modules 9–10) gives you SQL on the Warehouse engine with stored procedures, security, and production patterns. Together, they provide complete SQL coverage across every Fabric surface. Choose the right engine for each workload, and use cross-database queries to connect them.
In Module 11, we explore the SQL Database in Fabric — the transactional engine for operational workloads that automatically mirrors data to OneLake for analytics. The zero-ETL vision becomes real.

chapters.title: SQL Database in Fabric
chapters.description: OLTP Meets Analytics — The Zero-ETL Vision Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Understand Fabric’s OLTP SQL Database — the transactional engine for operational data. Learn schema design for transactional workloads, CRUD operations, automatic replication to OneLake, cross-workload integration, stored procedures and triggers, security, and how it completes the three-item SQL architecture alongside Lakehouse and Warehouse.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 11 Modules 9 and 10 taught you the Warehouse — Fabric’s analytical SQL engine. The Warehouse is designed for analytics: aggregations, window functions, star schemas, large scans. It is not designed for the other half of the SQL world: transactional workloads. Transactional workloads look different from analytical workloads. An e-commerce application inserts one order at a time, updates a single inventory row, and looks up a customer by primary key — thousands of times per second. These are OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) operations: small, fast, point reads and writes with strict consistency guarantees. The Warehouse’s distributed columnar engine is not optimized for this pattern. Fabric’s SQL Database fills this gap. It is a fully managed SQL Server database running inside Fabric — the same SQL Server engine that powers Azure SQL Database, with the same T-SQL surface, the same indexing, the same transactional guarantees. But with one transformative capability that changes everything: Automatic replication to OneLake. When you write a row to a SQL Database table, it is automatically, continuously replicated as a Delta table in OneLake. No ETL pipeline. No CDC configuration. No mirroring setup. The data just appears in OneLake, queryable by Spark Notebooks, Warehouses, Power BI DirectLake, and every other Fabric workload. This is the zero-ETL vision: operational data flows into analytics automatically. The application writes to the SQL Database. The analytics team reads from OneLake. The gap between operations and analytics — historically bridged by complex ETL pipelines running on overnight schedules — disappears. This module covers: what SQL Database in Fabric is, creating and configuring one, OLTP schema design, CRUD operations and transactional integrity, automatic OneLake replication, cross-workload querying, stored procedures and triggers, security, the three-way comparison (SQL Database vs. Warehouse vs. Lakehouse), and a hands-on checkpoint.

sections.title: What Is SQL Database in Fabric? SQL Database in Fabric is a fully managed SQL Server-compatible transactional database that runs inside a Fabric workspace. Under the hood, it is the same engine as Azure SQL Database — the SQL Server engine with automatic tuning, intelligent query processing, and enterprise-grade reliability. Key Characteristics • SQL Server engine: Full T-SQL support including stored procedures, triggers, user-defined functions, views, indexes (clustered, nonclustered, columnstore), constraints (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, UNIQUE, DEFAULT), and transaction isolation levels. • OLTP-optimized: Row-store engine optimized for single-row reads and writes, point lookups by primary key, and high-concurrency transactional workloads. The opposite of the Warehouse’s columnar scan engine. • Fully managed: Fabric handles provisioning, patching, backups, high availability, and scaling. You create a database and start writing T-SQL. No server management. • Automatic OneLake replication: Every table is continuously replicated to OneLake as Delta tables. Near-real-time latency (typically seconds). No configuration required. • Capacity-based billing: Compute is drawn from your Fabric capacity (CUs), just like Warehouses and Spark sessions. No separate Azure SQL pricing. What It Is NOT SQL Database in Fabric is not a replacement for the Warehouse. It is not designed for large analytical scans, star schema joins, or data warehouse workloads. It is designed for transactional patterns: application backends, operational data stores, microservice databases, and any workload that requires row-level CRUD with strict ACID guarantees. The analytics happen downstream, via the automatic OneLake replication.

blocks.content:
Application / API / Microservice
       │
       │  INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (OLTP)
       v
  SQL Database in Fabric  (SQL Server engine)
       │
       │  Automatic replication (near-real-time)
       v
  OneLake  (Delta tables)
       │
       ├──→  Spark Notebooks (PySpark analysis)
       ├──→  Warehouse (cross-database T-SQL)
       ├──→  Power BI DirectLake (real-time dashboards)
       └──→  KQL Database (real-time analytics)

sections.title: Creating and Configuring a SQL Database Creating a SQL Database • Navigate to your workspace in the Fabric portal. • Click “+ New item” → “SQL Database.” • Enter a name following your convention (e.g., sqldb_ecommerce, sqldb_inventory). • Click “Create.” The database is provisioned within seconds. Once created, the database opens in a SQL editor identical to the Warehouse editor — IntelliSense, multiple query tabs, object explorer, and execution plans. You can also connect from external tools using the connection string provided in the database settings. Connection Strings SQL Database provides a standard TDS (Tabular Data Stream) connection string compatible with: • SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) • Azure Data Studio • Any JDBC/ODBC client (Java, Python pyodbc, .NET SqlClient) • Entity Framework and other ORMs • Application frameworks (Node.js mssql, Python SQLAlchemy)

blocks.content:
# Python connection example
import pyodbc

conn = pyodbc.connect(
    'Driver={ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server};'
    'Server=your_server.database.fabric.microsoft.com;'
    'Database=sqldb_ecommerce;'
    'Authentication=ActiveDirectoryInteractive;'
    'Encrypt=yes;'
)

Auto-Tuning and Intelligent Features
SQL Database in Fabric inherits the intelligent performance features from Azure SQL Database:
•  Automatic tuning: The engine monitors query performance and automatically creates, validates, and applies indexes. It also identifies and reverts execution plan regressions.
•  Intelligent Query Processing: Adaptive joins, memory grant feedback, interleaved execution for multi-statement table-valued functions. These optimizations run transparently.
•  Automatic plan correction: When the engine detects that a query plan has regressed (a previously fast query is now slow), it automatically forces the last known good plan.
Tip: Auto-tuning means you spend less time manually creating and managing indexes compared to traditional SQL Server. The engine learns from your query patterns and optimizes itself. Monitor the auto-tuning recommendations in the database settings to see what the engine has applied.

sections.title: Schema Design for Transactional Workloads OLTP schema design follows different principles than analytical schema design. While the Warehouse uses star schemas (wide fact tables, denormalized dimensions), the SQL Database uses normalized schemas optimized for write performance and data integrity. Normalization Principles • Third Normal Form (3NF) is the standard for OLTP schemas. Every non-key column depends on the primary key, the whole key, and nothing but the key. • Separate entities into distinct tables (customers, orders, order_items, products, inventory) with foreign key relationships. • Minimize data redundancy. A customer’s name is stored once in the customers table, not repeated in every order row. • This design optimizes writes: updating a customer’s name requires changing one row in one table, not hundreds of rows across denormalized tables. Creating a Transactional Schema

blocks.content:
-- Customers table
CREATE TABLE dbo.customers (
    customer_id    INT IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name  NVARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    email          NVARCHAR(200) UNIQUE,
    phone          NVARCHAR(50),
    region         NVARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'Unassigned',
    created_at     DATETIME2 DEFAULT GETUTCDATE(),
    updated_at     DATETIME2 DEFAULT GETUTCDATE()
);

-- Products table
CREATE TABLE dbo.products (
    product_id     INT IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name   NVARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    category       NVARCHAR(100),
    unit_price     DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL CHECK (unit_price >= 0),
    stock_quantity INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (stock_quantity >= 0)
);

-- Orders table
CREATE TABLE dbo.orders (
    order_id       INT IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id    INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dbo.customers(customer_id),
    order_date     DATETIME2 DEFAULT GETUTCDATE(),
    status         NVARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending'
        CHECK (status IN ('pending','confirmed','shipped','delivered','cancelled')),
    total_amount   DECIMAL(18,2) DEFAULT 0
);

-- Order items (junction table)
CREATE TABLE dbo.order_items (
    item_id        INT IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id       INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dbo.orders(order_id),
    product_id     INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dbo.products(product_id),
    quantity       INT NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0),
    unit_price     DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    line_total     AS (quantity * unit_price) PERSISTED
);
Indexes for OLTP OLTP indexes serve a different purpose than analytical column statistics. They accelerate point lookups and foreign key joins:

-- Clustered index on primary key (auto-created by PRIMARY KEY)
-- Nonclustered indexes for common query patterns
-- Orders by customer (for customer order history lookups) CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_orders_customer ON dbo.orders (customer_id) INCLUDE (order_date, status, total_amount);
-- Orders by date (for date-range queries) CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_orders_date ON dbo.orders (order_date) INCLUDE (customer_id, status);
-- Order items by order (for order detail lookups) CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_orderitems_order ON dbo.order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (product_id, quantity, unit_price);

Important: In the Warehouse, you do not create indexes — the engine handles optimization automatically. In the SQL Database, indexes are essential for OLTP performance. Without them, point lookups degrade to table scans, and your application slows to a crawl under load.

sections.title: CRUD Operations and Transactional Integrity SQL Database supports the full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) pattern that applications require, with ACID transactional guarantees. INSERT (Create)

blocks.content: -- Insert a new customer INSERT INTO dbo.customers (customer_name, email, phone, region) VALUES ('Acme Corporation', 'orders@acme.com', '+1-555-0100', 'North');
-- Insert with OUTPUT to capture the auto-generated ID DECLARE @new_order_id TABLE (order_id INT);

-- Use the captured ID for order items INSERT INTO dbo.order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price) VALUES ((SELECT TOP 1 order_id FROM @new_order_id), 1, 3, 9.99), ((SELECT TOP 1 order_id FROM @new_order_id), 2, 1, 24.99);
SELECT (Read)
-- Point lookup by primary key (fast, uses clustered index) SELECT * FROM dbo.customers WHERE customer_id = 42;
-- Order history for a customer (uses IX_orders_customer) SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, o.status, o.total_amount FROM dbo.orders o WHERE o.customer_id = 42 ORDER BY o.order_date DESC;

-- Order with line items (join pattern) SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, i.product_id, p.product_name, i.quantity, i.unit_price, i.line_total FROM dbo.orders o JOIN dbo.order_items i ON o.order_id = i.order_id JOIN dbo.products p ON i.product_id = p.product_id WHERE o.order_id = 1001;
UPDATE
-- Update order status UPDATE dbo.orders SET status = 'shipped', updated_at = GETUTCDATE() WHERE order_id = 1001;

-- Decrement inventory after order confirmation UPDATE p SET p.stock_quantity = p.stock_quantity - i.quantity FROM dbo.products p JOIN dbo.order_items i ON p.product_id = i.product_id WHERE i.order_id = 1001;
Transactions ACID transactions ensure that multi-step operations are atomic — either all steps succeed, or all are rolled back:
BEGIN TRANSACTION; BEGIN TRY -- Step 1: Insert the order INSERT INTO dbo.orders (customer_id, status) VALUES (42, 'confirmed');
DECLARE @oid INT = SCOPE_IDENTITY();
-- Step 2: Insert line items INSERT INTO dbo.order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price) VALUES (@oid, 1, 3, 9.99);
-- Step 3: Decrement inventory UPDATE dbo.products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 3 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- Step 4: Update order total UPDATE dbo.orders SET total_amount = ( SELECT SUM(line_total) FROM dbo.order_items WHERE order_id = @oid ) WHERE order_id = @oid;

Important: Transactions in the SQL Database use the SQL Server locking model (READ COMMITTED by default). This provides row-level locking for high concurrency. The Warehouse uses snapshot isolation. Understanding the difference matters when designing for concurrent application workloads.

sections.title: Automatic Replication to OneLake This is the feature that makes SQL Database in Fabric transformative. Every table in the SQL Database is automatically and continuously replicated to OneLake as a Delta table. There is no configuration, no pipeline, no mirroring setup. It just works. How It Works • Change tracking: The SQL Server engine tracks changes at the row level. When an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE modifies data, the change is captured. • Delta conversion: Changes are continuously converted to Delta format and written to the database’s OneLake folder. Each table gets its own Delta table in OneLake. • Near-real-time latency: Replication typically completes within seconds of the commit. For most workloads, the OneLake Delta table is less than a minute behind the SQL Database. • Schema synchronization: When you add or modify columns (ALTER TABLE), the Delta table schema is updated automatically. What Gets Replicated Replicated Not Replicated All user tables (data rows and schema) Views (they are query definitions, not data) Column additions and compatible type changes Stored procedures, functions, triggers (code objects) Inserts, updates, and deletes Indexes (OneLake uses Delta statistics, not B-tree indexes) Computed columns (persisted values) Temporary tables, table variables

blocks.content:
Where the Replicated Data Appears
The replicated Delta tables appear in the Fabric portal under the SQL Database item, in a section similar to the Lakehouse’s Tables/ section. They are also visible in the OneLake Catalog and can be discovered by any Fabric workload.
Other items can read the replicated data through:
•  Spark Notebooks — Read the Delta table directly via abfss:// path or by creating a Lakehouse shortcut to the SQL Database’s OneLake folder.
•  Warehouse — Cross-database queries using three-part naming.
•  Power BI DirectLake — Connect a semantic model to the replicated Delta tables for real-time dashboards.
•  Other Lakehouses — Create a shortcut to reference the SQL Database’s tables without copying data.
The Zero-ETL Impact
Consider the traditional architecture: an application writes to a SQL Server database. A nightly ETL pipeline extracts the data, transforms it, and loads it into a data warehouse. Analysts query the warehouse the next morning. Data is at least 12–24 hours stale.
With SQL Database in Fabric: the application writes to the SQL Database. The data appears in OneLake within seconds. Analysts query it via Spark, the Warehouse, or Power BI immediately. The nightly ETL pipeline for basic data movement is eliminated.
Warning: Automatic replication handles data movement, not transformation. If your analytics require cleaning, enriching, or reshaping the data, you still need a transformation layer (Notebooks, Dataflows Gen2, Warehouse stored procedures). Zero-ETL means zero extraction and loading pipelines — not zero data engineering.

sections.title: Querying SQL Database from Other Workloads The replicated Delta tables in OneLake are the integration bridge. Every Fabric workload can read SQL Database data through its preferred interface. From Spark Notebooks

blocks.content:
# Option 1: Read the replicated Delta table via shortcut
# (create a Lakehouse shortcut pointing to the SQL Database's OneLake path)
df = spark.table('customers')  # If shortcut is in attached Lakehouse

# Option 2: Read via abfss:// path directly
df = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'abfss://my_workspace@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/'
    'sqldb_ecommerce.SQLDatabase/dbo/customers'
)

# Option 3: Query the SQL Database directly via JDBC
df = spark.read \
    .format('jdbc') \
    .option('url', 'jdbc:sqlserver://server.database.fabric.microsoft.com') \
    .option('dbtable', 'dbo.customers') \
    .option('authentication', 'ActiveDirectoryInteractive') \
    .load()

Options 1 and 2 read the replicated Delta tables from OneLake — fast, no impact on the SQL Database’s compute. Option 3 queries the SQL Database engine directly via JDBC — useful for transactional consistency but consumes SQL Database compute. From the Warehouse

-- Cross-database query from Warehouse to SQL Database SELECT w.order_date, w.total_revenue, s.customer_count FROM gold.daily_revenue w JOIN sqldb_ecommerce.dbo.customer_summary s ON w.summary_date = s.summary_date;

From Power BI
Power BI can connect to SQL Database data in two ways:
•  DirectLake: Connect the semantic model to the replicated Delta tables in OneLake. This provides the fastest dashboard performance with near-real-time data, no import required.
•  DirectQuery: Connect directly to the SQL Database engine via the TDS endpoint. Queries run against the live database. Useful when you need transactional-level consistency (not even seconds of lag).
Tip: For most dashboards, DirectLake on the replicated Delta tables is the best choice: it combines real-time-ish data (seconds of lag) with fast read performance (Delta columnar storage). Reserve DirectQuery for scenarios that require absolute transactional consistency (e.g., real-time inventory displays).

sections.title: Stored Procedures, Triggers, and Jobs SQL Database supports the full SQL Server programmability surface — a significantly richer set of features than the Warehouse. Stored Procedures SQL Database stored procedures support everything that Warehouse procedures do, plus additional capabilities from the SQL Server engine:

blocks.content: -- Validate stock SELECT @price = unit_price, @stock = stock_quantity FROM dbo.products WHERE product_id = @product_id;
IF @stock < @quantity BEGIN THROW 50001, 'Insufficient stock', 1; RETURN; END;
BEGIN TRANSACTION; BEGIN TRY -- Create order INSERT INTO dbo.orders (customer_id, status, total_amount) VALUES (@customer_id, 'confirmed', @quantity * @price); SET @order_id = SCOPE_IDENTITY();
-- Add line item INSERT INTO dbo.order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price) VALUES (@order_id, @product_id, @quantity, @price);
-- Decrement inventory UPDATE dbo.products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - @quantity WHERE product_id = @product_id;
COMMIT TRANSACTION; SELECT @order_id AS new_order_id; END TRY BEGIN CATCH ROLLBACK TRANSACTION; THROW; END CATCH; END;
Triggers Triggers are a SQL Database capability not available in the Warehouse. They execute automatically in response to data modifications:

-- Automatically update the updated_at timestamp on any customer change CREATE TRIGGER dbo.trg_customers_updated ON dbo.customers AFTER UPDATE AS BEGIN SET NOCOUNT ON; UPDATE c SET c.updated_at = GETUTCDATE() FROM dbo.customers c JOIN inserted i ON c.customer_id = i.customer_id; END;

-- Audit trigger: log every order status change CREATE TRIGGER dbo.trg_orders_audit ON dbo.orders AFTER UPDATE AS BEGIN SET NOCOUNT ON; INSERT INTO dbo.order_audit_log (order_id, old_status, new_status, changed_at) SELECT d.order_id, d.status, i.status, GETUTCDATE() FROM deleted d JOIN inserted i ON d.order_id = i.order_id WHERE d.status <> i.status; END;

Warning: Triggers fire on every DML operation and add latency to each transaction. Use them sparingly for audit logging and computed-field updates. Avoid complex business logic in triggers — it makes debugging difficult and can cause cascading performance issues under load. Scheduled Jobs SQL Database supports scheduled jobs for maintenance tasks: purging old records, recalculating aggregates, rebuilding indexes. Jobs are defined and scheduled within the database using the SQL Agent-compatible interface, running on a cron-like schedule without external orchestration.

sections.title: Security and Access Control SQL Database uses the SQL Server security model: logins, users, roles, and granular permissions. Authentication is through Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory). Authentication • Microsoft Entra ID: The primary authentication method. Users and service principals authenticate with their Entra ID credentials. No SQL logins with passwords — all access is identity-based. • Application authentication: Applications authenticate using service principals or managed identities via Entra ID. This integrates with standard Azure identity patterns. Authorization

blocks.content: -- Create a database user mapped to an Entra ID account CREATE USER [analyst@company.com] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER;
-- Create a custom role CREATE ROLE app_readonly; GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA::dbo TO app_readonly; DENY INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON SCHEMA::dbo TO app_readonly;
Row-Level Security Just like the Warehouse (Module 9), SQL Database supports Row-Level Security via security policies and filter predicates. The syntax is identical:

-- Same RLS pattern as the Warehouse CREATE FUNCTION dbo.fn_customer_filter(@customer_id INT) RETURNS TABLE WITH SCHEMABINDING AS RETURN SELECT 1 AS result WHERE @customer_id = SESSION_CONTEXT(N'customer_id') OR IS_MEMBER('db_admin') = 1;

CREATE SECURITY POLICY dbo.CustomerFilter
ADD FILTER PREDICATE dbo.fn_customer_filter(customer_id)
ON dbo.orders
WITH (STATE = ON);

A common pattern for multi-tenant applications: the application sets SESSION_CONTEXT with the tenant’s ID at connection time, and RLS transparently filters all queries to that tenant’s data. Important: Security applied in the SQL Database does not automatically apply to the replicated Delta tables in OneLake. OneLake access is governed by Fabric workspace roles and OneLake security policies. If you restrict access in the SQL Database, verify that the same restrictions apply to the OneLake replicas.

sections.title: SQL Database vs. Warehouse vs. Lakehouse — The Three-Way Comparison You now know all three SQL-capable items in Fabric. This is the definitive comparison to guide your architecture decisions.

blocks.content: Criterion SQL Database Data Warehouse Lakehouse Engine SQL Server (row-store OLTP) Fabric SQL (distributed columnar) Spark + auto-generated SQL endpoint Primary workload Transactional (OLTP) Analytical (OLAP) Data engineering + analytics Write interface Full T-SQL DML Full T-SQL DML PySpark / Spark SQL Read interface T-SQL (full SQL Server) T-SQL (Fabric SQL) PySpark + read-only T-SQL endpoint Stored procedures Yes (full SQL Server) Yes (Fabric T-SQL subset) No Triggers Yes No No Indexes Yes (clustered, nonclustered, columnstore) No (automatic optimization) No (Delta statistics + Z-ORDER) Foreign keys Yes (enforced) No No Schemas Yes Yes Implicit OneLake storage Auto-replicated Delta Native Delta Native Delta Files/ section No No Yes Spark access Read via JDBC or OneLake replica Read via connector Native Auto table maintenance Auto-tuning, auto-indexing Auto compaction, auto stats Manual OPTIMIZE, VACUUM Best persona Application developers, DBAs SQL analysts, analytics engineers Data engineers, data scientists

Decision Framework Choose SQL Database when: your workload is transactional — high-frequency single-row INSERTs and UPDATEs, application backends, microservice databases, operational data stores. You need foreign key enforcement, triggers, row-level locking, and the data should automatically flow to OneLake for analytics. Choose Data Warehouse when: your workload is analytical — large table scans, star schema joins, aggregations, window functions. Your team writes T-SQL. You need stored procedures for encapsulated transformation logic and schema-level security for governed access. Choose Lakehouse when: your workload is data engineering — Spark/PySpark transformations, semi-structured data processing, ML feature engineering. You need a Files/ landing zone for raw data. Your team writes Python or Scala. Use all three when: your organization has operational applications (SQL Database), analytical reporting (Warehouse), and data engineering/ML (Lakehouse). Data flows from SQL Database to OneLake automatically, is transformed by Notebooks, lands in the Warehouse Gold layer, and is served by Power BI. This is the full Fabric architecture.

sections.title: Module 11 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: What SQL Database in Fabric is — a fully managed SQL Server-compatible OLTP engine inside Fabric with automatic OneLake replication
Creating and configuring — provisioning, connection strings for external tools, auto-tuning, intelligent query processing, and automatic plan correction
OLTP schema design — 3NF normalization, primary/foreign keys, CHECK constraints, IDENTITY columns, and OLTP-specific indexes (clustered, nonclustered, INCLUDE columns)
CRUD operations and transactions — INSERT with OUTPUT, point-lookup SELECT, UPDATE with joins, explicit transactions with BEGIN TRY/CATCH and ROLLBACK, ACID guarantees with row-level locking

Automatic OneLake replication — how it works (change tracking → Delta conversion), near-real-time latency, what replicates (tables, columns) vs. what does not (views, procedures, indexes), and the zero-ETL vision
Cross-workload querying — reading replicated Delta tables from Spark Notebooks (shortcut, abfss://, JDBC), Warehouse (three-part naming), and Power BI (DirectLake vs. DirectQuery)
Stored procedures, triggers, and jobs — full transactional procedures with TRY/CATCH, audit triggers on UPDATE, scheduled maintenance jobs
Security — Entra ID authentication, database users/roles, schema-level GRANT/DENY, RLS with SESSION_CONTEXT for multi-tenant applications, OneLake security gap awareness
Three-way comparison — 14-point SQL Database vs. Warehouse vs. Lakehouse table; decision framework by workload type (OLTP, OLAP, data engineering)

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab Build a complete operational database with analytics integration:

items: Create a SQL Database named sqldb_workshop in your workspace.
Design and create a transactional schema: customers, products, orders, and order_items tables with appropriate primary keys, foreign keys, CHECK constraints, and indexes.

Write a stored procedure (dbo.usp_place_order) that creates an order, inserts line items, decrements inventory, and calculates the order total — all within a single transaction with TRY/CATCH error handling.
Create a trigger on the orders table that logs every status change to an audit table.
Insert sample data: at least 5 customers, 10 products, and 20 orders with line items. Execute the stored procedure for some orders.
Verify automatic OneLake replication: navigate to the SQL Database’s replicated tables in the Fabric portal. Confirm that all tables appear as Delta tables.
Create a Lakehouse shortcut to the SQL Database’s replicated customers table. Open a Notebook and read the shortcut as a Spark DataFrame. Run a groupBy aggregation on region.
Write a cross-database query from your Warehouse that joins the SQL Database’s customer data with the Warehouse’s analytical summary tables.

blocks.content: You have now completed the entire SQL stack in Fabric.

SQL Database for operations. Warehouse for analytics. Lakehouse for data engineering. Three engines, one storage layer (OneLake), one format (Delta), and one governance boundary (Fabric workspace). Data flows between them automatically, without the extraction and loading pipelines that traditionally consumed 80% of a data team’s time.

In Module 12, we shift to an entirely different paradigm: Real-Time Intelligence — Eventhouse and KQL. Streaming data, time-series analysis, the Kusto Query Language, and the real-time analytics workload that completes Fabric’s data platform story.

chapters.title: Real-Time Intelligence — Eventhouse & KQL
chapters.description: Streaming Analytics at the Speed of Events Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Ingest, store, and analyze streaming and time-series data using Eventhouse (KQL Database). Master Kusto Query Language for real-time analytics — filtering, projection, aggregation, time-series functions, joins, advanced operators, and Real-Time Dashboards.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 12 Everything you have built so far — Lakehouses, Warehouses, SQL Databases, Pipelines — operates in batch or near-batch mode. Data arrives in files or scheduled extracts. Pipelines run daily or hourly. Dashboards refresh on a timer. This is how most analytical platforms work, and for most use cases, it is sufficient. But some use cases cannot wait. A logistics company needs to detect delayed shipments as they happen, not tomorrow morning. A cybersecurity team needs to correlate login anomalies within seconds of occurrence. An IoT platform needs to identify sensor failures before they cause equipment damage. These are real-time analytics problems, and they require a fundamentally different engine. That engine is Real-Time Intelligence — Fabric’s workload for streaming data. At its core is the Eventhouse, a container for one or more KQL Databases that store and query time-series data using the Kusto Query Language (KQL) — a pipe-based query language purpose-built for exploring massive volumes of timestamped data at interactive speeds. KQL is not SQL. It does not look like SQL. It does not think like SQL. Where SQL describes the shape of a result set, KQL describes a data flow: start with a table, pipe it through a filter, pipe it through a projection, pipe it through an aggregation, pipe it through a visualization. This functional, left-to-right pipeline model is what makes KQL extraordinarily productive for exploratory time-series analysis. This module covers: the Real-Time Intelligence workload overview, Eventhouse architecture, creating a KQL Database, data ingestion, KQL fundamentals, time-series functions, joins and lookups, advanced KQL operators, Real-Time Dashboards, and a hands-on checkpoint.

sections.title: Real-Time Intelligence Overview Real-Time Intelligence is one of Fabric’s seven workloads. It provides end-to-end streaming analytics through five integrated components:

blocks.content: Component What It Does Analogy Eventhouse Container for KQL Databases. Stores and serves time-series data. Like a Lakehouse, but optimized for streaming and time-series. KQL Database The actual database inside an Eventhouse. Tables, functions, materialized views. Like a Warehouse database, but with KQL instead of T-SQL. Eventstream Streaming ingestion and routing service. Connects sources to destinations. Like a Pipeline, but for continuous streaming rather than batch. KQL Queryset A notebook-like editor for writing and saving KQL queries. Like a Notebook, but for KQL instead of PySpark. Real-Time Dashboard Interactive dashboards built directly from KQL queries. Auto-refreshing. Like a Power BI dashboard, but native to KQL with lower latency.
Where Real-Time Intelligence Fits

Event Sources (IoT, logs, clickstream, APIs, Event Hubs)
       │
       v
  Eventstream  (ingest, route, transform in-flight)
       │
       ├──→  Eventhouse / KQL Database  (store + query)
       │         │
       │         ├──→  Real-Time Dashboard (visualize)
       │         ├──→  Activator (alert + automate)
       │         └──→  OneLake shortcut (for Spark / Warehouse / Power BI)
       │
       └──→  Lakehouse / Warehouse  (long-term batch analytics)

The typical flow is: events stream in through an Eventstream, land in a KQL Database for real-time querying, and optionally route to a Lakehouse for long-term batch analytics. Real-Time Dashboards visualize the KQL data. Activator triggers alerts when conditions are met.
When to Use Real-Time Intelligence
•  IoT telemetry — sensor readings, device health, equipment monitoring.
•  Application logs — web server logs, API call traces, error tracking.
•  Security events — login attempts, firewall logs, threat detection.
•  Clickstream analytics — user behavior, page views, session analysis.
•  Financial events — transaction monitoring, fraud detection, market data.
•  Any scenario where data has a timestamp and you need to analyze patterns over time at low latency.

sections.title: Eventhouse Architecture An Eventhouse is a Fabric item that contains one or more KQL Databases. Think of the Eventhouse as a container (like a SQL Server instance) and the KQL Database as the database inside it (like a SQL Server database). Most implementations have one Eventhouse per domain with one or two KQL Databases inside. Storage Architecture KQL Databases use a columnar storage engine fundamentally different from Delta Lake: • Append-optimized: Data is ingested in batches (even from streaming sources) and written as immutable columnar extents. There are no UPDATE or DELETE operations on individual rows. This append-only model enables extremely fast ingestion. • Automatic indexing: Every column is automatically indexed. String columns get full-text indexes. Datetime columns get time-based indexes. You never create or manage indexes manually. • Hot and warm caching: Recent data lives in hot cache (SSD/RAM) for sub-second query latency. Older data moves to warm cache (remote storage) with slightly higher latency. The caching policy determines the boundary. • Automatic compaction: The engine continuously merges small extents into larger ones, optimizing storage and query performance without manual OPTIMIZE commands. Retention and Caching Policies Two policies control data lifecycle:

blocks.content: Policy What It Controls Default Example Retention How long data is kept before automatic deletion. Unlimited RETAIN 365 days: data older than 1 year is deleted automatically. Caching How much recent data stays in hot cache (fast SSD/RAM). Unlimited CACHE 30 days: last 30 days are in hot cache. Older data is in warm storage.
// Set retention and caching policies on a table .alter table SensorReadings policy retention ``` { "SoftDeletePeriod": "365.00:00:00" } ```
.alter table SensorReadings policy caching ``` { "DataHotSpan": "30.00:00:00" } ```

Important: Caching and retention policies are the primary cost and performance controls for KQL Databases. Hot cache is fast but expensive (CU consumption). Warm storage is cheaper but slower. Set caching policies based on your query patterns: if analysts typically query the last 7 days, a 7–14 day hot cache is sufficient.

sections.title: Creating an Eventhouse and KQL Database Creating an Eventhouse • Navigate to your workspace and click “+ New item” → “Eventhouse.” • Enter a name (e.g., eh_iot_monitoring, eh_security_events). • Click “Create.” A default KQL Database is created inside the Eventhouse automatically. KQL Database Configuration Once created, configure the database: • OneLake availability — Enable OneLake integration to make KQL tables available as Delta tables in OneLake. This lets Spark Notebooks and Power BI access the data. • Default retention policy — Set the database-level retention (applied to all tables unless overridden at the table level). • Default caching policy — Set the database-level caching window. Creating Tables KQL tables can be created through the Get Data wizard (schema inferred from sample data) or through KQL management commands:

blocks.content: // Create a table with explicit schema .create table SensorReadings ( DeviceId: string, Temperature: real, Humidity: real, Pressure: real, Timestamp: datetime, Location: string )

// Create an ingestion mapping (for CSV/JSON sources) .create table SensorReadings ingestion json mapping 'SensorMapping' '[ {"column": "DeviceId", "path": "$.device_id"}, {"column": "Temperature", "path": "$.temp"}, {"column": "Humidity", "path": "$.humidity"}, {"column": "Pressure", "path": "$.pressure"}, {"column": "Timestamp", "path": "$.timestamp"}, {"column": "Location", "path": "$.location"} ]'

Ingestion mappings define how source fields map to table columns. They are essential when the source schema does not match the table schema — different field names, different nesting levels, or different formats.

sections.title: Data Ingestion KQL Databases ingest data through multiple mechanisms, optimized for different scenarios. Ingestion Methods Method Pattern Best For Eventstream Continuous streaming from Event Hubs, custom apps, IoT Hub. Real-time event ingestion. Primary production method. Get Data wizard One-click ingestion from files, Azure storage, or sample data. Initial data loading, testing, and exploration. Queued ingestion (.ingest) KQL management command. Batches data for efficient ingestion. Programmatic bulk loading from scripts or notebooks. Streaming ingestion Low-latency ingestion (sub-second). Data available immediately. Ultra-low-latency scenarios. Requires explicit enablement. OneLake shortcut Read-only access to Delta tables from Lakehouses or Warehouses. Enriching KQL queries with batch data without copying. Connector ingestion Built-in connectors for Azure Blob, ADLS Gen2, Cosmos DB, and more. Periodic or one-time loads from Azure sources.

blocks.content:
The Get Data Wizard
The fastest way to get started. From the KQL Database editor:
•  Click “Get Data” in the toolbar.
•  Select a source: local file (CSV, JSON, Parquet), Azure Storage, Event Hub, or sample data.
•  Preview the data. The wizard auto-detects the schema.
•  Adjust column names and types if needed.
•  Choose a target table (existing or new).
•  Click “Finish.” The data is ingested and immediately queryable.
Eventstream Integration
For production streaming, Eventstreams (covered in detail in Module 13) connect event sources to KQL Databases continuously:

  Event Hub  ──→  Eventstream  ──→  KQL Database (Eventhouse)
                       │
                       └──→  Lakehouse (long-term storage)

The Eventstream handles schema mapping, format conversion, and routing. The KQL Database receives a continuous flow of events and makes them queryable within seconds of arrival. Ingestion Batching Even “streaming” ingestion into KQL Databases uses micro-batching: events are collected in small batches (typically 1 second or 1000 events, whichever comes first) and written as columnar extents. This batching is why KQL can sustain millions of events per second — it never writes one row at a time. Tip: For the Get Data wizard, use a sample dataset to follow along with this module’s KQL examples. Fabric provides built-in sample data options when creating a KQL Database — choose one to practice with immediately.

sections.title: KQL Fundamentals — Thinking in Pipelines KQL is a pipe-based query language. You start with a table name, then chain operators with the pipe character (|). Data flows left to right, top to bottom, through each operator. This is fundamentally different from SQL, where you describe the shape of the result and the engine figures out how to get there. The Basic Pattern

blocks.content: // Every KQL query starts with a table name // followed by a pipeline of operators

SensorReadings              // Start: the table
| where Timestamp > ago(1h) // Filter: last hour
| where Temperature > 30    // Filter: high temp
| project DeviceId, Temperature, Timestamp  // Select columns
| order by Temperature desc // Sort
| take 10                   // Limit to 10 rows

Read it like a sentence: “Take the SensorReadings table, keep only the last hour, keep only temperatures above 30, show DeviceId, Temperature, and Timestamp, sort by temperature descending, and return the top 10.” Core Operators Operator Purpose SQL Equivalent where Filter rows by condition WHERE project Select and rename columns SELECT (column list) extend Add a calculated column (keeps existing columns) SELECT *, new_col = ... summarize Aggregate (group by) GROUP BY + aggregate functions order by / sort by Sort rows ORDER BY take / limit Return first N rows TOP / LIMIT count Count rows COUNT(*) distinct Unique values DISTINCT top Return top N by a column ORDER BY + TOP combined join Combine two tables JOIN union Stack rows from multiple tables UNION ALL render Visualize results as a chart (no SQL equivalent)
Filtering with where
// Equality SensorReadings | where DeviceId == 'sensor-001'
// Comparison SensorReadings | where Temperature > 30
// Time-based (the most common filter in KQL) SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(24h) SensorReadings | where Timestamp between (datetime(2026-01-01) .. datetime(2026-01-31))

// String operations SensorReadings | where Location has 'warehouse' // contains (case-insensitive) SensorReadings | where Location startswith 'US' SensorReadings | where DeviceId matches regex @'sensor-\d{3}'
// Combine conditions SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(1h) and Temperature > 30 | where Location in ('warehouse-A', 'warehouse-B')
Projection with project and extend
// Select specific columns SensorReadings | project DeviceId, Temperature, Timestamp
// Rename columns SensorReadings | project device = DeviceId, temp = Temperature
// Add a calculated column (extend keeps all existing columns) SensorReadings | extend TempFahrenheit = Temperature * 9/5 + 32 | extend IsHigh = Temperature > 30
Aggregation with summarize
// Count events per device SensorReadings | summarize EventCount = count() by DeviceId

// Multiple aggregations SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | summarize AvgTemp = avg(Temperature), MaxTemp = max(Temperature), MinTemp = min(Temperature), ReadingCount = count() by DeviceId | order by AvgTemp desc
// Time-binned aggregation (the signature KQL pattern) SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | summarize AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) by bin(Timestamp, 1h), DeviceId | order by Timestamp asc

Important: bin() is one of KQL’s most important functions. It rounds datetime values to a specified interval (1m, 5m, 1h, 1d), enabling time-bucketed aggregations. “Summarize by bin(Timestamp, 5m)” gives you 5-minute averages — the fundamental building block of time-series analysis.

sections.title: KQL Time-Series Functions Time-series analysis is KQL’s superpower. These functions go far beyond what SQL can express concisely. Time Functions Function Returns Example ago(timespan) Datetime N time ago from now ago(1h), ago(7d), ago(30m) now() Current UTC datetime now() bin(value, roundTo) Value rounded to nearest interval bin(Timestamp, 5m) datetime_diff(part, dt1, dt2) Difference between two datetimes datetime_diff('hour', start, end) format_datetime(dt, fmt) Formatted string from datetime format_datetime(Timestamp, 'yyyy-MM-dd') startofday() / startofweek() / startofmonth() Truncate to period start startofday(Timestamp) endofday() / endofweek() / endofmonth() Truncate to period end endofmonth(Timestamp)

blocks.content: make-series: Building Time Series make-series is KQL’s killer feature for time-series analytics. It creates a regular time series array by filling gaps in irregular event data. If a sensor reports every 5 minutes but misses some intervals, make-series fills the gaps with defaults, producing a clean, evenly-spaced array suitable for trend analysis.

// Create a time series: average temperature per hour, per device SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(7d) | make-series AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) default=real(null) on Timestamp step 1h by DeviceId
// Result: each row is one device with arrays: // DeviceId | AvgTemp (array of 168 values) | Timestamp (array of 168 hours)
The result is one row per device, where AvgTemp and Timestamp are arrays of values. This array format is the input for KQL’s powerful series analysis functions. Series Analysis Functions

// Detect anomalies in the time series SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(7d) | make-series AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) default=real(null) on Timestamp step 1h by DeviceId | extend anomalies = series_decompose_anomalies(AvgTemp)

// Decompose into trend, seasonality, and residual SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(30d) | make-series AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) default=real(null) on Timestamp step 1h by DeviceId | extend (trend, seasonal, residual) = series_decompose(AvgTemp)

// Forecast future values SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(30d) | make-series AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) default=real(null) on Timestamp step 1h by DeviceId | extend forecast = series_decompose_forecast(AvgTemp, 24) // Forecasts 24 hours into the future

These functions — anomaly detection, decomposition, and forecasting — run at query time, directly on the time-series arrays. No ML model training required. No separate compute. Just a KQL query that returns actionable insight in seconds.

sections.title: KQL Joins and Lookups KQL supports joins between tables within the same KQL Database and between KQL tables and external data via shortcuts. Join Syntax

blocks.content: // Inner join: events enriched with device metadata SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(1h) | join kind=inner ( DeviceRegistry | project DeviceId, DeviceName, Floor, Building ) on DeviceId | project Timestamp, DeviceName, Floor, Temperature
// Left outer join: keep all readings, enrich where possible SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(1h) | join kind=leftouter ( DeviceRegistry ) on DeviceId

Join Kinds Kind Behavior KQL Syntax inner Only matching rows from both sides. kind=inner leftouter All left rows + matching right rows. kind=leftouter rightouter All right rows + matching left rows. kind=rightouter fullouter All rows from both sides. kind=fullouter leftanti Left rows with NO match in right. kind=leftanti leftsemi Left rows that HAVE a match in right. kind=leftsemi innerunique Inner join but deduplicated on the left key. Default. kind=innerunique (default)
The lookup Operator lookup is an optimized operator for enriching a large table with a small dimension table:
// lookup: faster than join for dimension enrichment SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(1h) | lookup DeviceRegistry on DeviceId

lookup is semantically equivalent to a left outer join but is optimized for the common pattern where the right table is small and fits in memory. Use lookup for dimension enrichment and join for large-to-large joins. Materialized Views For expensive aggregations that are queried frequently, KQL supports materialized views — pre-computed results that update automatically as new data arrives:

// Create a materialized view for hourly device summaries .create materialized-view HourlySummary on table SensorReadings { SensorReadings | summarize AvgTemp = avg(Temperature), MaxTemp = max(Temperature), ReadingCount = count() by DeviceId, bin(Timestamp, 1h) }
Materialized views are the KQL equivalent of pre-computed aggregation tables. They are automatically maintained by the engine — no refresh schedule needed.

sections.title: KQL Advanced — render, mv-expand, parse Visualization with render KQL queries can include visualization instructions with the render operator:

blocks.content: // Line chart: temperature over time per device SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | summarize AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) by bin(Timestamp, 1h), DeviceId | render timechart
// Bar chart: event count per device SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | summarize Count = count() by DeviceId | order by Count desc | render barchart
// Pie chart: events by location SensorReadings | summarize Count = count() by Location | render piechart

render is what makes KQL self-contained for analytics: query the data, aggregate it, and visualize it — all in one statement. This is especially powerful in KQL Querysets and Real-Time Dashboards. Expanding Multi-Valued Columns with mv-expand When a column contains a JSON array or dynamic array, mv-expand explodes it into individual rows:
// Source: a column 'Tags' containing arrays like ["urgent", "floor-3"] SensorAlerts | mv-expand Tags | summarize AlertCount = count() by tostring(Tags)

// Expand array from make-series back to rows SensorReadings | where Timestamp > ago(7d) | make-series AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) on Timestamp step 1h by DeviceId | mv-expand AvgTemp to typeof(real), Timestamp to typeof(datetime)
Parsing Semi-Structured Text with parse parse extracts structured fields from unstructured text strings:
// Log line: "2026-01-15 14:30:22 [ERROR] Server=web-03 Message=Timeout"

AppLogs | parse LogLine with Timestamp:datetime ' [' Level '] Server=' Server ' Message=' Message | where Level == 'ERROR' | summarize ErrorCount = count() by Server, bin(Timestamp, 1h) | render timechart

// parse_json: for JSON strings in a column AppEvents | extend parsed = parse_json(EventPayload) | extend UserId = tostring(parsed.user_id), Action = tostring(parsed.action), Duration = toreal(parsed.duration_ms)

Important: parse, mv-expand, and dynamic column operations (parse_json, bag_unpack) are what make KQL exceptionally productive for log analytics. You can go from raw, unstructured log lines to structured, aggregated, visualized insight in a single query.

sections.title: Real-Time Dashboards Real-Time Dashboards are Fabric’s native visualization layer for KQL data. They provide interactive, auto-refreshing dashboards built directly from KQL queries. Creating a Real-Time Dashboard • Navigate to your workspace and click “+ New item” → “Real-Time Dashboard.” • Name the dashboard (e.g., rtd_iot_monitoring). • Add a tile: each tile is a KQL query with a visualization. • Write the KQL query for the tile, configure the visualization type (table, chart, stat card, map), and set formatting options. • Repeat for each tile. Arrange tiles on the canvas. Dashboard Features • Auto-refresh: Dashboards can refresh automatically at intervals you define (every 30 seconds, 1 minute, 5 minutes). Each refresh re-executes the underlying KQL queries. • Parameters: Define dashboard-level parameters (e.g., a time range dropdown, a device selector) that inject values into KQL queries. Users interact with parameters to filter the entire dashboard. • Cross-filter: Clicking a value in one tile filters other tiles that share the same parameter. Click a device in a device list tile, and the temperature chart updates to show only that device. • Drill-through: Configure tiles to navigate to a detail page or another dashboard when clicked. • Sharing: Share dashboards with workspace members. Viewers can interact with parameters and cross-filters but cannot edit queries. Real-Time Dashboards vs. Power BI Criterion Real-Time Dashboard Power BI Dashboard Data source KQL Databases (Eventhouse) Any Fabric item + 300+ connectors Query language KQL DAX + data model Refresh latency Seconds (auto-refresh) Minutes (scheduled refresh or DirectQuery) Visualization richness Moderate (charts, tables, stats, maps) Very rich (custom visuals, bookmarks, drill-through) Best for Real-time operational monitoring Business reporting and self-service analytics Persona Data engineers, SecOps, DevOps Business analysts, BI developers

blocks.content: Use Real-Time Dashboards for operational monitoring where low-latency refresh is critical. Use Power BI for business reporting where visualization richness and self-service features matter more than refresh speed.

sections.title: Module 12 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: Real-Time Intelligence overview — five components (Eventhouse, KQL Database, Eventstream, KQL Queryset, Real-Time Dashboard) and when to use real-time analytics
