print(f'Precision: {precision_score(y_test, y_pred):.4f}')
print(f'Recall:    {recall_score(y_test, y_pred):.4f}')
print(f'F1:        {f1_score(y_test, y_pred):.4f}')
print(f'AUC-ROC:   {roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob):.4f}')
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))
Training with LightGBM

import lightgbm as lgb

train_data = lgb.Dataset(X_train, label=y_train)
val_data = lgb.Dataset(X_test, label=y_test, reference=train_data)

params = {
    'objective': 'binary',
    'metric': 'auc',
    'num_leaves': 31,
    'learning_rate': 0.05,
    'feature_fraction': 0.8,
    'bagging_fraction': 0.8,
    'bagging_freq': 5,
    'verbose': -1
}

model = lgb.train(
    params, train_data,
    num_boost_round=500,
    valid_sets=[val_data],
    callbacks=[lgb.early_stopping(50), lgb.log_evaluation(100)]
)

Choosing the Right Algorithm Algorithm Strengths Best For Logistic Regression Simple, fast, interpretable. Baseline model. Binary classification with linear relationships. Random Forest Robust, handles nonlinearity, feature importance. Tabular data without extensive tuning. Gradient Boosting (sklearn) Strong accuracy, sequential learning. Smaller datasets where training speed is not critical. LightGBM Very fast, handles large datasets, native categoricals. Tabular data at scale. Competition-winning performance. XGBoost High accuracy, regularization, GPU support. Tabular data with complex interactions. Spark MLlib Distributed training on the full cluster. Datasets too large for single-node memory.

sections.title: MLflow Experiment Tracking MLflow is Fabric’s integrated experiment tracking system. It automatically logs every training run — parameters, metrics, model artifacts, and environment details — so you can compare, reproduce, and audit every model you train. How MLflow Integrates with Fabric • Pre-configured: MLflow is pre-installed and connected to your Fabric workspace. No setup, no tracking server to deploy, no database to configure. • Autologging: Fabric enables MLflow autologging by default for popular frameworks (scikit-learn, LightGBM, XGBoost, TensorFlow, PyTorch). Autologging captures parameters, metrics, and model artifacts without any code changes. • Experiment UI: The Fabric portal provides a visual experiment interface: run comparison charts, metric curves, parameter tables, and artifact browsing. Manual Logging

blocks.content:
import mlflow

# Set the experiment (creates if not exists)
mlflow.set_experiment('churn_prediction')

# Start a run
with mlflow.start_run(run_name='gbm_v1') as run:
    # Log parameters
    mlflow.log_param('n_estimators', 200)
    mlflow.log_param('max_depth', 5)
    mlflow.log_param('learning_rate', 0.1)
    mlflow.log_param('algorithm', 'GradientBoostingClassifier')

    # Train model
    model = GradientBoostingClassifier(
        n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, learning_rate=0.1
    )
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    # Log metrics
    mlflow.log_metric('accuracy', accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))
    mlflow.log_metric('precision', precision_score(y_test, y_pred))
    mlflow.log_metric('recall', recall_score(y_test, y_pred))
    mlflow.log_metric('f1', f1_score(y_test, y_pred))
    mlflow.log_metric('auc_roc', roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob))

    # Log the model
    mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'churn_model')

    # Log artifacts (feature importance plot, confusion matrix, etc.)
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
    # ... create feature importance plot ...
    mlflow.log_figure(fig, 'feature_importance.png')
print(f'Run ID: {run.info.run_id}')

Comparing Runs
The Fabric experiment UI lets you:
•  Compare metrics across runs in a table or chart. See which hyperparameters produced the best AUC.
•  Visualize metric curves: if you log metrics at each boosting round, the UI shows learning curves.
•  Download artifacts: model files, plots, and custom artifacts logged during training.
•  Reproduce any run: the logged parameters and environment details enable exact reproduction.
Tip: Always log at least these items: algorithm name, key hyperparameters, evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC), and the model artifact. This minimum set enables meaningful comparison and reproducibility.

sections.title: Model Registry and Versioning The MLflow Model Registry in Fabric is a centralized repository for production-ready models. It provides versioning, stage management, and lineage tracking. Registering a Model

blocks.content:
# Register a model from an experiment run
model_uri = f'runs:/{run.info.run_id}/churn_model'
registered_model = mlflow.register_model(
    model_uri=model_uri,
    name='churn_prediction_model'
)
print(f'Model version: {registered_model.version}')

# Or register directly in the Fabric portal:
# Navigate to the experiment run > click 'Register model'

Version Management and Stages Each registered model can have multiple versions. Versions move through stages: Stage Meaning When to Use None Default stage for newly registered versions. Just registered, not yet evaluated. Staging Under validation. Being tested against production criteria. A/B testing, shadow deployment, integration testing. Production Actively serving predictions. The current best model. Live batch scoring via PREDICT. Archived Retired from active use. Kept for audit and rollback. Replaced by a newer version.

# Transition a model version to production
from mlflow.tracking import MlflowClient

client = MlflowClient()
client.transition_model_version_stage(
    name='churn_prediction_model',
    version=2,
    stage='Production'
)

Model Lineage
The registry tracks the full lineage of each model version:
•  Which experiment run produced it (parameters, metrics, code).
•  Which training data was used (logged as artifacts or parameters).
•  Who registered it and when.
•  Stage transition history (who promoted it, when, from which stage to which).
Important: Always register models that will be used for batch scoring. The registry provides the stable reference point that the PREDICT function uses. Without registration, you would need to manually track model file paths and versions — error-prone and unauditable.

sections.title: Batch Predictions with PREDICT PREDICT is Fabric’s zero-infrastructure batch scoring function. It applies a registered ML model to a table and returns predictions — no serving endpoint, no REST API, no Docker container. Just a function call. PREDICT in PySpark (Notebooks)

blocks.content:
import mlflow

# Load the production model
model = mlflow.sklearn.load_model('models:/churn_prediction_model/Production')

# Load the scoring data
scoring_df = spark.table('gold.customer_features_current')
scoring_pdf = scoring_df.select(
    'customer_id', 'total_spend', 'order_count',
    'days_since_last_order', 'region_idx', 'segment_idx'
).toPandas()

# Generate predictions
scoring_pdf['churn_probability'] = model.predict_proba(
    scoring_pdf.drop('customer_id', axis=1)
)[:, 1]
scoring_pdf['churn_prediction'] = (scoring_pdf['churn_probability'] > 0.5).astype(int)

# Write predictions back to Lakehouse
predictions_spark = spark.createDataFrame(scoring_pdf)
predictions_spark.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable(
    'gold.customer_churn_predictions'
)
PREDICT in T-SQL (Warehouse)

-- Use the PREDICT function in the Warehouse SELECT customer_id, p.churn_prediction, p.churn_probability FROM PREDICT( MODEL 'churn_prediction_model', DATA gold.customer_features_current, RUNTIME spark ) AS p JOIN gold.customer_features_current c ON p.customer_id = c.customer_id;

Operationalizing with Pipelines
For production, schedule batch scoring in a Pipeline:
•  Pipeline Activity 1: Notebook that refreshes feature tables (PySpark transformations from the Gold Lakehouse).
•  Pipeline Activity 2: Notebook that loads the production model and generates predictions, writing them to a predictions table.
•  Pipeline Activity 3: Dataflow Gen2 or Notebook that merges predictions into the Warehouse Gold layer.
•  Pipeline trigger: daily schedule, or event-based (triggered by data arrival).
•  Power BI DirectLake semantic model reads the predictions table. Reports show churn risk scores, segmented by region, product, and customer tier.
Important: The key architectural decision is where predictions land. Write them to a Delta table in the Lakehouse or Warehouse so Power BI can read them via DirectLake. Predictions become just another dimension or fact table in your star schema — fully integrated with the analytical platform.

sections.title: SynapseML and Distributed Training SynapseML (formerly MMLSpark) is a library that extends Spark with distributed machine learning, deep learning, and Cognitive Services integration. It is pre-installed in Fabric Spark environments. Distributed LightGBM with SynapseML Single-node LightGBM (Part 4) works for datasets that fit in memory. For larger datasets, SynapseML provides a distributed LightGBM implementation that trains across all Spark executor nodes:

blocks.content:
from synapse.ml.lightgbm import LightGBMClassifier
from pyspark.ml.feature import VectorAssembler

# Assemble features (stays in Spark, no toPandas needed)
assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=['total_spend', 'order_count', 'days_since_last_order',
               'region_idx', 'segment_idx'],
    outputCol='features'
)
assembled_df = assembler.transform(df)

# Split
train_df, test_df = assembled_df.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

# Train LightGBM on the full cluster
lgbm = LightGBMClassifier(
    featuresCol='features',
    labelCol='churn',
    numLeaves=31,
    numIterations=200,
    learningRate=0.05,
    featureFraction=0.8
)

lgbm_model = lgbm.fit(train_df)

# Predict on test set
predictions = lgbm_model.transform(test_df)
display(predictions.select('customer_id', 'churn', 'prediction', 'probability'))

Cognitive Services Integration SynapseML includes connectors to Azure Cognitive Services (now Azure AI Services), enabling no-code AI directly in Spark: Service Capability SynapseML Class Text Analytics Sentiment analysis, key phrase extraction, language detection. AnalyzeText Computer Vision Image description, OCR, object detection. AnalyzeImage Translator Text translation between languages. Translate Anomaly Detector Time-series anomaly detection. DetectAnomalies OpenAI (via Azure) GPT text completion, embeddings. OpenAICompletion

from synapse.ml.services import AnalyzeText

# Sentiment analysis on customer reviews
sentiment = AnalyzeText()
    .setTextCol('review_text')
    .setOutputCol('sentiment')
    .setKind('SentimentAnalysis')

results = sentiment.transform(reviews_df)
display(results.select('review_id', 'review_text', 'sentiment'))

sections.title: AI Functions and LLM Integration Fabric provides integration with Azure OpenAI and large language models (LLMs) directly within the data platform, enabling AI-augmented data engineering and analytics. Fabric AI Functions AI Functions are pre-built functions that apply LLM capabilities to data columns: • Summarize: Condense long text (support tickets, emails, documents) into concise summaries. • Classify: Categorize text into predefined labels (sentiment, topic, priority). • Extract: Pull structured information from unstructured text (names, dates, amounts). • Translate: Convert text between languages at scale. Using Azure OpenAI in Notebooks

blocks.content:
# Call Azure OpenAI from a Fabric Notebook
import openai

# Configure with your Azure OpenAI endpoint
openai.api_type = 'azure'
openai.api_base = 'https://your-instance.openai.azure.com/'
openai.api_version = '2024-02-01'
openai.api_key = 'your-key'  # Or use Key Vault

# Process a column of text
def classify_ticket(text):
    response = openai.ChatCompletion.create(
        engine='gpt-4',
        messages=[
            {'role': 'system', 'content': 'Classify the support ticket into:
             High Priority, Medium Priority, or Low Priority. Respond with
             only the category.'},
            {'role': 'user', 'content': text}
        ],
        max_tokens=20
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content.strip()

# Apply to pandas DataFrame
pdf['priority'] = pdf['ticket_text'].apply(classify_ticket)

When to Use LLMs vs. Traditional ML Use Case Best Approach Why Churn prediction from structured features Traditional ML (LightGBM, XGBoost) Tabular data. LLMs are slower, more expensive, and less accurate for structured prediction. Sentiment analysis of free text LLM or Cognitive Services Unstructured text. LLMs excel at language understanding. Text classification with many examples Fine-tuned traditional model or LLM Either can work. LLMs need fewer examples. Data extraction from documents LLM Parsing unstructured documents into structured fields. Feature generation from text LLM embeddings + traditional ML Use LLMs for embeddings, traditional ML for prediction.

Warning: LLM API calls are slow (seconds per request) and expensive (per-token pricing). For batch processing thousands of records, use Cognitive Services APIs (optimized for batch) or process in parallel. Never call an LLM in a hot loop on millions of rows — use traditional ML or pre-computed embeddings instead.

sections.title: Module 16 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: Data Science workload overview — six components (Notebooks, Experiments, ML Models, PREDICT, SynapseML, SemPy); the ML lifecycle fully inside Fabric using existing Spark compute and OneLake storage

Exploratory data analysis — loading Delta tables into pandas, profiling with describe/info/isnull, visualization with matplotlib and seaborn, SemPy for reading Power BI semantic model data and DAX measures in Notebooks

Feature engineering — seven common transformations (scaling, encoding, imputation, feature crosses, binning, text vectorization); PySpark ML Pipelines for reproducible, reusable preprocessing; training/serving skew prevention
Model training — single-node with scikit-learn and LightGBM; train-test splits with stratification; evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC); six-algorithm selection guide
MLflow experiment tracking — pre-configured integration; autologging; manual logging of parameters, metrics, model artifacts, and figures; run comparison in the Fabric UI
Model registry — registering models from experiment runs; version management; stage transitions (None → Staging → Production → Archived); lineage tracking

Batch predictions with PREDICT — scoring in PySpark Notebooks and T-SQL Warehouse; writing predictions to Delta tables; operationalizing with Pipeline schedules; integrating predictions into Power BI star schema
SynapseML and distributed training — distributed LightGBM across Spark cluster for large datasets; Cognitive Services integration (sentiment, vision, translation, anomaly detection)
AI Functions and LLM integration — Azure OpenAI in Notebooks; text classification, summarization, extraction at scale; LLM vs. traditional ML decision guide; cost and performance warnings

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab: End-to-End ML Pipeline Build a complete ML pipeline:

items: Load a dataset from your Gold Lakehouse into a Notebook. Perform EDA: check distributions, correlations, null rates, and class balance. Create at least two visualizations.
Build a PySpark ML Pipeline for feature engineering: impute missing values, encode categoricals, assemble features. Save the fitted pipeline for reuse at scoring time.
Train two models (e.g., Gradient Boosting and LightGBM). Use MLflow to log parameters, metrics, and model artifacts for each. Compare the runs in the Fabric experiment UI.
Register the best-performing model in the ML Model registry. Transition it to the Production stage.

Create a batch scoring Notebook: load the Production model, apply it to a current customer features table, generate churn predictions, and write the results to a gold.customer_churn_predictions Delta table.
Schedule the scoring Notebook in a Pipeline to run daily. Add the feature refresh Notebook as a preceding activity.
Add the predictions table to your Power BI semantic model. Create a report page showing churn risk distribution, high-risk customer list, and churn rate by region.

blocks.content: You have closed the loop. Data flows from sources through the Lakehouse, is transformed by Notebooks, analyzed by Warehouses, predicted by ML models, and visualized by Power BI. The entire lifecycle — from raw event to business prediction — happens inside one platform.

In Module 17, we step back and look at the big picture: Medallion Architecture and Data Modeling — the design principles that organize everything you have built into a scalable, governed, production-grade data platform.

chapters.title: Medallion Architecture & Data Modeling
chapters.description: Designing the Layers That Scale Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Implement the Bronze → Silver → Gold medallion pattern in Fabric. Design star schemas with surrogate keys and advanced dimensional modeling. Handle slowly changing dimensions, enforce data quality, trace lineage, and build a governed, layered data platform.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 17 You have now learned every tool in the Fabric platform: Lakehouses, Warehouses, SQL Databases, Pipelines, Dataflows, Notebooks, KQL, Eventstreams, Power BI, and Data Science. You can build anything. But building individual components is not the same as building a platform. This module is about architecture — the design decisions that determine whether your platform scales gracefully or collapses under complexity. The medallion architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold) is the organizing principle that separates a collection of ad-hoc notebooks from a governed data platform. It gives every table a clear purpose, every transformation a defined stage, and every consumer a trusted data product. Combined with dimensional modeling (star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, data quality rules), the medallion architecture creates a data platform that is maintainable, auditable, and extensible. When a new data source arrives or a new business question emerges, you know exactly where to add it and how it flows through the system. This module covers: the medallion architecture principles, Bronze layer design, Silver layer patterns, Gold layer star schemas, implementing medallion in Fabric Lakehouses, star schema design principles, slowly changing dimensions in Delta Lake, data quality rules and validation, lineage and documentation, and a hands-on checkpoint.

sections.title: The Medallion Architecture The medallion architecture organizes data into three layers based on quality and purpose: Bronze (raw), Silver (cleansed), and Gold (business-ready). Each layer has a clear contract: what enters, what transforms, and what exits.

blocks.content:
External Sources (APIs, databases, files, streams)
       │
       v
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  BRONZE  (Raw Landing Zone)                   │
  │  • Exact copy of source data                  │
  │  • Append-only                                │
  │  • Metadata columns added (load_time, source) │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │  Cleanse, deduplicate, conform
       v
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SILVER  (Cleansed & Conformed)               │
  │  • Deduplicated, null-handled, type-cast      │
  │  • Conformed dimensions across sources        │
  │  • Business key integrity enforced             │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │  Aggregate, model, enrich
       v
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  GOLD  (Business-Ready)                       │
  │  • Star schema (fact + dimension tables)       │
  │  • Summary aggregation tables                  │
  │  • Optimized for BI, ML, and APIs              │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ├──→  Power BI (DirectLake semantic models)
       ├──→  Warehouse (T-SQL analytics)
       └──→  ML models (feature tables)

Why Three Layers?
•  Isolation of concern: Each layer has one job. Bronze preserves. Silver cleanses. Gold serves. When something breaks, you know exactly which layer to investigate.
•  Reprocessability: Because Bronze preserves the original data, you can always reprocess Silver and Gold from scratch if business logic changes or a bug is discovered.
•  Incremental complexity: Bronze is dead simple (just land the data). Silver adds standard quality rules. Gold adds business-specific modeling. This layering makes the system understandable and debuggable.
•  Multiple consumers: Silver tables serve data scientists (who need clean but un-modeled data). Gold tables serve BI analysts (who need star schemas). Different consumers read from different layers without interfering.
Important: The medallion architecture is not rigid. Some teams use four layers (adding a “Platinum” or “Presentation” layer). Others collapse Bronze and Silver into two layers for simple sources. The principle — progressive refinement from raw to business-ready — is what matters, not the exact number of layers.

sections.title: Bronze Layer — Raw Ingestion The Bronze layer’s contract is simple: land a faithful copy of the source data, add metadata, and never delete anything. Bronze Design Principles • Schema-on-read: Accept whatever the source sends. If the source adds a new column, Bronze should not break. Use flexible schemas or land as raw files first, then read with inference. • Append-only: Never UPDATE or DELETE in Bronze. Every load appends new rows. This creates a complete audit trail of everything the source ever sent. • Metadata columns: Every Bronze table adds system columns that track provenance:

blocks.content:
# Standard metadata columns for every Bronze table
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit, input_file_name

bronze_df = raw_df \
    .withColumn('_load_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
    .withColumn('_source_system', lit('erp_oracle')) \
    .withColumn('_source_file', input_file_name()) \
    .withColumn('_load_batch_id', lit(batch_id))

Bronze Ingestion Patterns Pattern How It Works When to Use Full snapshot append Copy the entire source table each load. Append to Bronze with load_timestamp. Small reference tables. Sources that do not provide change tracking. Incremental append Extract only new/changed rows since last load (watermark). Append to Bronze. Large fact tables with a reliable watermark column (modified_date, sequence ID). File landing Land raw files (CSV, JSON, Parquet) into Files/ section. Process into Tables/ with metadata. File-based sources, API responses, external data drops. CDC stream Capture INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations as individual events. Each event is one Bronze row. Database sources with Change Data Capture enabled.

Handling Late-Arriving Data Late-arriving data is data that belongs to a past business period but arrives after that period’s processing is complete. In Bronze, the solution is simple: append it with the current load_timestamp. The business date is in the data; the load_timestamp records when it arrived. Silver and Gold layer logic must account for late arrivals by using the business date, not the load date, for partitioning and aggregation. Warning: Never modify Bronze tables after they are written. If source data needs correction, land the corrected version as a new append with a new load_timestamp. The Silver layer handles deduplication and “last version wins” logic. Bronze is your immutable audit trail.

sections.title: Silver Layer — Cleansing and Conforming The Silver layer’s contract: one trusted, deduplicated, type-conformant version of each entity. Silver tables are the “single source of truth” for cleansed data. Standard Silver Transformations Transformation What It Does Example Deduplication Remove duplicate rows. Keep the latest version per business key. ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY _load_timestamp DESC), keep rn = 1 Type casting Enforce correct data types. Source may send dates as strings or amounts as text. cast(amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)), to_date(order_date_str, 'yyyy-MM-dd') Null handling Replace nulls with defaults, drop rows with null primary keys, coalesce fallback values. fillna, coalesce, dropna(subset=['order_id']) Standardization Normalize values across sources. Trim whitespace, uppercase codes, unify date formats. upper(trim(region)), standardize country codes to ISO-3166 Renaming and mapping Rename source-specific columns to conform to enterprise naming standards. Rename 'cust_nm' to 'customer_name', 'ord_dt' to 'order_date' Validation filtering Flag or exclude rows that fail business rules. Invalid dates, negative amounts, orphan keys. Filter where amount >= 0, flag where order_date > current_date as suspect

blocks.content: The Silver MERGE Pattern

# Silver deduplication + merge pattern (PySpark)
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, col

# Step 1: Read new Bronze data since last watermark
new_bronze = spark.table('bronze.raw_customers') \
    .filter(col('_load_timestamp') > last_watermark)

# Step 2: Deduplicate - keep latest version per business key
window = Window.partitionBy('customer_id').orderBy(col('_load_timestamp').desc())
deduped = new_bronze \
    .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(window)) \
    .filter(col('rn') == 1) \
    .drop('rn')

# Step 3: Apply cleansing transformations
cleaned = deduped \
    .withColumn('customer_name', upper(trim(col('customer_name')))) \
    .withColumn('email', lower(trim(col('email')))) \
    .withColumn('region', coalesce(col('region'), lit('Unknown'))) \
    .select('customer_id', 'customer_name', 'email',
            'region', 'segment', '_load_timestamp')

# Step 4: MERGE into Silver table
silver_table = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.customers')
silver_table.alias('target').merge(
    cleaned.alias('source'),
    'target.customer_id = source.customer_id'
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()

Conformed Dimensions When multiple source systems describe the same entity differently (ERP calls it “client,” CRM calls it “account,” web calls it “user”), the Silver layer creates one conformed dimension that reconciles all representations. This conformed dimension becomes the single reference for the Gold layer and all downstream consumers. Conforming requires a mapping table or business rules that link source-specific identifiers to a single enterprise key. This is often the hardest data engineering problem — and it must be solved in Silver, not deferred to Gold or reporting.

sections.title: Gold Layer — Business Aggregates and Star Schemas The Gold layer’s contract: business-ready tables optimized for specific consumer use cases. Gold tables are not generic — they are purpose-built for BI dashboards, ML feature stores, API responses, or executive summaries. Gold Table Types Type Purpose Example Fact table Records of business events. Grain = one event per row. fact_orders (one row per order), fact_page_views (one row per view) Dimension table Descriptive attributes for filtering and grouping. dim_customers, dim_products, dim_date, dim_geography Aggregate table Pre-computed summaries for common queries. agg_daily_revenue (one row per day per region) Wide table (OBT) One Big Table: denormalized fact + dimensions for simple consumption. obt_orders_enriched (fact with all dimensions flattened in) Feature table ML-ready feature sets derived from Silver data. feature_customer_churn (features per customer for ML)

blocks.content: Building Gold from Silver

# Gold fact table: one row per order with conformed foreign keys
fact_orders = spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        o.order_id,
        c.customer_sk,     -- surrogate key from dim_customers
        p.product_sk,      -- surrogate key from dim_products
        d.date_key,        -- date key from dim_date
        o.quantity,
        o.unit_price,
        o.quantity * o.unit_price AS line_amount,
        o.discount_amount,
        o.quantity * o.unit_price - o.discount_amount AS net_amount
    FROM silver.orders o
    JOIN gold.dim_customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
        AND c.is_current = 1
    JOIN gold.dim_products p ON o.product_id = p.product_id
    JOIN gold.dim_date d ON o.order_date = d.calendar_date
""")
fact_orders.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable( 'gold.fact_orders' )
Aggregate Tables Pre-compute common aggregations that would otherwise require expensive full-table scans:

# Daily revenue aggregate
daily_revenue = spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        d.calendar_date,
        d.calendar_year,
        d.calendar_month,
        c.region,
        p.category,
        SUM(f.net_amount) AS total_revenue,
        COUNT(*) AS order_count,
        COUNT(DISTINCT f.customer_sk) AS unique_customers
    FROM gold.fact_orders f
    JOIN gold.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
    JOIN gold.dim_customers c ON f.customer_sk = c.customer_sk
    JOIN gold.dim_products p ON f.product_sk = p.product_sk
    GROUP BY d.calendar_date, d.calendar_year, d.calendar_month,
             c.region, p.category
""")
daily_revenue.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable( 'gold.agg_daily_revenue' )

Tip: Aggregate tables dramatically improve Power BI DirectLake performance. Instead of scanning millions of fact rows for a monthly revenue chart, the semantic model reads thousands of pre-aggregated rows. Create aggregate tables for every metric that appears on a frequently-used dashboard.

sections.title: Implementing Medallion in Fabric Lakehouses There are several ways to organize medallion layers within Fabric. The right choice depends on your team size, security requirements, and operational complexity. Layout Options Option Structure Pros Cons One Lakehouse per layer lh_bronze, lh_silver, lh_gold in the same workspace Clearest separation. Independent permissions per layer. Easy to understand. More items to manage. Cross-Lakehouse queries needed. One Lakehouse with schemas Single Lakehouse. Tables named bronze_*, silver_*, gold_* Simpler management. All data in one item. No permission isolation between layers. Naming conventions must be enforced manually. One Lakehouse per domain lh_sales_gold, lh_marketing_gold — domains own their Gold Domain autonomy. Each team manages their Gold layer. Requires governance to prevent data silos. Shortcut management for cross-domain access. Lakehouse + Warehouse hybrid Lakehouse for Bronze/Silver (PySpark). Warehouse for Gold (T-SQL). Best of both engines. SQL analysts work in Warehouse. Engineers in Lakehouse. Two items to manage. Data moves between them (or use cross-database queries).

blocks.content: Recommended: One Lakehouse Per Layer For most organizations, one Lakehouse per layer provides the best balance of clarity and governance:

Workspace: ws_data_platform
  ├── lh_bronze       (Landing zone. Engineers write, nobody else reads)
  │     ├── Tables/    (Bronze Delta tables with metadata columns)
  │     └── Files/     (Raw files: CSV, JSON, Parquet from sources)
  │
  ├── lh_silver       (Cleansed. Engineers write, data scientists read)
  │     └── Tables/    (Silver Delta tables, conformed dimensions)
  │
  ├── lh_gold         (Business-ready. Engineers write, analysts + BI read)
  │     └── Tables/    (Facts, dimensions, aggregates)
  │
  ├── wh_gold         (Optional Warehouse mirror of Gold for T-SQL analysts)
  ├── sm_analytics    (Semantic model on lh_gold via DirectLake)
  └── rpt_executive   (Power BI report on sm_analytics)

Naming Conventions
•  Lakehouses: lh_bronze, lh_silver, lh_gold (or lh_{domain}_bronze for domain-specific).
•  Tables: {entity}_name in snake_case. No layer prefix in the table name (the Lakehouse name provides the layer context).
•  Warehouses: wh_{purpose} (e.g., wh_gold, wh_reporting).
•  Notebooks: nb_{layer}_{entity}_{verb} (e.g., nb_silver_customers_merge, nb_gold_fact_orders_build).
•  Pipelines: pl_{purpose} (e.g., pl_daily_refresh, pl_bronze_ingest_erp).
Important: Naming conventions are governance. When every item follows a predictable pattern, any team member can find any artifact without documentation. Enforce conventions from day one — renaming hundreds of items after the fact is painful.

sections.title: Star Schema Design Principles Star schema design was introduced throughout this course. This section consolidates the advanced dimensional modeling concepts that production star schemas require. Grain Definition The grain is the most critical decision in dimensional modeling. It defines what one row in the fact table represents. Every fact table must have a clearly stated grain before any columns are added. • fact_orders: one row per order line item (order + product combination). • fact_page_views: one row per page view event. • fact_daily_inventory: one row per product per warehouse per day (periodic snapshot). If the grain is ambiguous, measures will be wrong. A fact table with mixed granularity (some rows are orders, some rows are order-items) produces incorrect aggregations because SUM(amount) double-counts or undercounts. Surrogate Keys Surrogate keys are meaningless integer identifiers assigned by the data warehouse, used instead of source business keys for joining facts to dimensions. They enable SCD Type 2 (where one business key can have multiple dimension rows, each with a unique surrogate key).

blocks.content:
# Generate surrogate keys in PySpark
from pyspark.sql.functions import monotonically_increasing_id, row_number
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Option 1: monotonically_increasing_id (unique but not sequential)
dim_df = dim_df.withColumn('customer_sk', monotonically_increasing_id())

# Option 2: row_number for sequential keys
window = Window.orderBy('customer_id')
dim_df = dim_df.withColumn('customer_sk', row_number().over(window))

Advanced Dimension Types Type Description Example Degenerate dimension A dimension key stored in the fact table with no corresponding dimension table. The key itself is the attribute. Order number, invoice number, transaction ID in the fact table. Junk dimension A single table combining low-cardinality flags and indicators to avoid cluttering the fact table. Combine is_priority, is_gift_wrap, payment_type into dim_order_flags. Role-playing dimension One dimension table used multiple times with different relationships. Typically dates. dim_date joined to order_date, ship_date, and delivery_date. Conformed dimension A dimension shared identically across multiple fact tables and subject areas. dim_date and dim_customer used by both Sales and Marketing facts. Bridge table Resolves many-to-many relationships between facts and dimensions. One order can have multiple promotions. Bridge links order to promotion. Outrigger dimension A dimension joined to another dimension (not to the fact). Rarely used. dim_geography joined to dim_customer (geographic attributes of the customer).

sections.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions in Delta Lake Module 10 covered SCD Type 2 in T-SQL. This section implements SCDs in Delta Lake using PySpark — the native Fabric pattern for Lakehouse-based star schemas. SCD Type Comparison Type Strategy History Preserved? Complexity Type 0 Never update. Dimension values are fixed at initial load. N/A (values never change) Lowest Type 1 Overwrite the old value. No history. No Low Type 2 Add a new row with effective dates. Expire the old row. Yes — full history High Type 3 Add a previous_value column. Keeps only one prior version. Partial (one prior version) Medium Type 6 (Hybrid) Combine Type 1 + Type 2 + Type 3. Current value column on all rows plus history rows. Yes + current value accessible Highest

blocks.content: SCD Type 2 in PySpark + Delta

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import *

# Load incoming changes
updates = spark.table('silver.customers')

# Load current dimension
dim = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'gold.dim_customers')

# Step 1: Identify changed rows
changes = updates.alias('s').join(
    dim.toDF().alias('t'),
    (col('s.customer_id') == col('t.customer_id')) & (col('t.is_current') == True),
    'inner'
).filter(
    (col('s.customer_name') != col('t.customer_name')) |
    (col('s.region') != col('t.region')) |
    (col('s.segment') != col('t.segment'))
).select('s.*')

# Step 2: Expire old rows (set is_current = False, effective_to = today)
dim.alias('t').merge(
    changes.alias('s'),
    'target.customer_id = source.customer_id AND target.is_current = true'
).whenMatchedUpdate(set={
    'is_current': lit(False),
    'effective_to': current_date()
}).execute()

# Step 3: Insert new versions
new_rows = changes.select(
    monotonically_increasing_id().alias('customer_sk'),
    col('customer_id'),
    col('customer_name'),
    col('email'),
    col('region'),
    col('segment'),
    current_date().alias('effective_from'),
    lit('9999-12-31').cast('date').alias('effective_to'),
    lit(True).alias('is_current')
)
new_rows.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('gold.dim_customers')

# Step 4: Insert brand-new customers (not in dimension at all)
new_customers = updates.alias('s').join(
    dim.toDF().alias('t'),
    col('s.customer_id') == col('t.customer_id'),
    'leftanti'
)
# ... similar insert logic for new_customers

Important: SCD Type 2 in Delta Lake uses the same MERGE semantics as T-SQL (Module 10), but expressed in PySpark. The pattern is: identify changes, expire old rows, insert new versions, handle brand-new entities. The choice between PySpark and T-SQL for SCD2 depends on your team’s skill set and which engine hosts the Gold layer.

sections.title: Data Quality Rules and Validation Data quality is not something you check after the pipeline runs. It is something you build into the pipeline. Every layer transition (Bronze → Silver, Silver → Gold) should include explicit quality checks. Quality Dimensions Dimension What It Checks Example Rule Completeness Are required fields present and non-null? order_id IS NOT NULL, customer_name IS NOT NULL Uniqueness Are primary keys unique? No duplicate business keys? COUNT(order_id) = COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) Referential integrity Do foreign keys match primary keys in dimension tables? Every fact_orders.customer_id exists in dim_customers.customer_id Range validity Are values within expected bounds? order_date BETWEEN '2020-01-01' AND current_date, amount >= 0 Format conformity Do values match expected formats? email matches regex pattern, phone number has correct length Freshness Is data arriving on time? MAX(load_timestamp) within last 24 hours

blocks.content: Building a Quality Check Notebook

# Data quality validation framework
from datetime import datetime

def check_quality(table_name, checks):
    df = spark.table(table_name)
    results = []
    total = df.count()

    for check_name, check_expr, threshold in checks:
        failures = df.filter(check_expr).count()
        pass_rate = 1 - (failures / total) if total > 0 else 0
        passed = pass_rate >= threshold
        results.append({
            'table': table_name,
            'check': check_name,
            'failures': failures,
            'total': total,
            'pass_rate': round(pass_rate, 4),
            'threshold': threshold,
            'status': 'PASS' if passed else 'FAIL',
            'checked_at': datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
        })

    return results

# Define checks for Silver customers table
checks = [
    ('null_customer_id', 'customer_id IS NULL', 1.0),
    ('null_customer_name', 'customer_name IS NULL', 0.99),
    ('duplicate_keys', None, 1.0),  # Handled separately
    ('invalid_email', "email NOT LIKE '%@%'", 0.95),
]

results = check_quality('silver.customers', checks)

# Write results to audit table
audit_df = spark.createDataFrame(results)
audit_df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('audit.quality_log')
Quality Gates A quality gate is a checkpoint that halts the pipeline if quality checks fail:

items: In a Pipeline, the quality check Notebook runs after the Silver MERGE. If it returns “FAIL,” the Pipeline’s error path triggers an alert and prevents the Gold layer from refreshing with bad data.
Severity levels: CRITICAL checks (null PKs, duplicates) halt the pipeline. WARNING checks (minor format issues) log to the audit table but do not halt.
Quality dashboards: build a Power BI report on the audit.quality_log table. Track pass rates over time, catch deteriorating sources early.

sections.title: Module 17 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: The medallion architecture — Bronze (raw, append-only, metadata-enriched), Silver (cleansed, deduplicated, conformed), Gold (business-ready, star schema, aggregates); four benefits of layering; flexibility of the pattern

Bronze layer — schema-on-read, append-only contract, metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system, _source_file, _load_batch_id); four ingestion patterns (full snapshot, incremental, file landing, CDC); late-arriving data handling

Silver layer — six standard transformations (deduplication, type casting, null handling, standardization, renaming, validation filtering); the Silver MERGE pattern in PySpark; conformed dimensions across sources
Gold layer — five table types (fact, dimension, aggregate, wide/OBT, feature); building facts from Silver with surrogate key joins; aggregate tables for Power BI performance

Implementing medallion in Fabric — four layout options (one Lakehouse per layer, one with naming, domain-based, hybrid Lakehouse+Warehouse); recommended structure; naming conventions for all item types
Star schema design — grain definition, surrogate keys, six advanced dimension types (degenerate, junk, role-playing, conformed, bridge, outrigger)
Slowly changing dimensions — five SCD types (0, 1, 2, 3, 6); SCD Type 2 implementation in PySpark with Delta MERGE (identify changes, expire, insert new versions, handle new entities)

Data quality — six quality dimensions (completeness, uniqueness, referential integrity, range, format, freshness); quality check framework in PySpark; quality gates in Pipelines; audit logging and quality dashboards

Lineage and documentation — item-level lineage in Fabric portal; column-level lineage via Purview; OneLake Catalog for discovery; endorsements; documentation best practices (descriptions, data dictionary, grain statements, domain tags)

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab: Build a Medallion Lakehouse Implement the complete medallion architecture:

items: Create three Lakehouses: lh_bronze, lh_silver, lh_gold.
Ingest raw data from at least two sources (e.g., CSV files and an API response) into lh_bronze. Add metadata columns: _load_timestamp, _source_system. Use append-only writes.
Build a Silver Notebook that reads from Bronze, deduplicates by business key, applies type casting and null handling, standardizes values, and MERGEs into lh_silver tables.

Build a Gold Notebook that creates a star schema: at least one fact table (fact_orders) and three dimensions (dim_customers with SCD Type 2, dim_products, dim_date). Use surrogate keys for the dimensions. Create one aggregate table (agg_daily_revenue).
Implement SCD Type 2 for dim_customers: process a batch of updates where one customer’s region has changed. Verify that the old row is expired and a new row is inserted with is_current = 1.

Add data quality checks between Silver and Gold: validate that no primary keys are null, no duplicates exist, and referential integrity holds (all foreign keys in the fact match dimension keys). Log results to an audit.quality_log table.
Create a Pipeline that orchestrates the full flow: Bronze ingest → Silver merge → Quality check → Gold build. Add an error path that sends an alert if quality checks fail.
Verify lineage in the Fabric portal: navigate to the lineage view and confirm the flow from Lakehouse → Notebook → Lakehouse → Semantic Model is visible.

blocks.content: You have designed and built the architecture. The medallion pattern, star schema modeling, SCD handling, and quality gates are the structural decisions that determine whether a data platform stays healthy at scale or collapses into an unmaintainable tangle of ad-hoc queries.

In Module 18, we add the governance and security layer: Security, Governance, and Microsoft Purview — workspace permissions, data protection labels, Purview integration, compliance, and audit logging across the entire Fabric platform.

chapters.title: Security, Governance & Microsoft Purview
chapters.description: Trust Is the Foundation of Every Data Platform Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Secure the Fabric platform end-to-end — workspace permissions, item security, OneLake data access controls, Row-Level Security across workloads, sensitivity labels, Microsoft Purview integration, network security, and audit logging for regulatory compliance.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 18 You have built a complete data platform: Lakehouses, Warehouses, Pipelines, Notebooks, KQL, Power BI reports, ML models, and a medallion architecture that organizes it all. But a platform without security is a liability. A single misconfigured permission can expose customer PII to an unauthorized user. A missing audit trail can turn a routine compliance inquiry into a crisis. Security and governance are not features you add at the end. They are architectural constraints that shape every decision from day one. Who can see which data? Who can modify which pipelines? How do you prove, six months later, that a particular dataset was accessed only by authorized users? Fabric’s security model is layered: identity at the foundation (Microsoft Entra ID), then workspace-level access, then item-level permissions, then data-level controls (OneLake security, RLS, column security). Each layer builds on the one below it. This module walks through every layer, from the outermost (tenant) to the innermost (individual rows and columns). This module covers: the security model overview, workspace roles, item-level security, OneLake data security, Row-Level Security across workloads, sensitivity labels, Microsoft Purview integration, network security, audit logging and compliance, and a hands-on security blueprint checkpoint.

sections.title: Fabric Security Model Overview Fabric uses a layered security model where each layer narrows access further. A user must pass through all layers to reach data. The layers, from outermost to innermost:

blocks.content:
Layer 1:  TENANT ──────────  Entra ID tenant. Who exists in the org?
  Layer 2:  WORKSPACE ───────  Workspace roles. Who can access this workspace?
  Layer 3:  ITEM ────────────  Per-item sharing. Who can access this specific item?
  Layer 4:  DATA ────────────  OneLake policies, RLS, column security, OLS.
                                 Which rows/columns can this user see?

Identity Foundation: Microsoft Entra ID
Every Fabric identity is a Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) object: a user, a service principal, or a security group. There are no Fabric-local passwords or accounts. All authentication goes through Entra ID, which means:
•  Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enforced at the Entra ID level, not in Fabric.
•  Conditional Access policies (require managed device, block risky locations) apply automatically.
•  Security groups in Entra ID are the primary mechanism for managing permissions at scale — assign a group to a workspace role, and all group members inherit the role.
•  Service principals enable automated access (Pipelines, APIs) without human credentials.
The Principle of Least Privilege
Every security decision in this module should follow one rule: grant the minimum permissions needed for the task. If a user only needs to view reports, do not make them a Workspace Member. If a Pipeline only needs to read from Bronze and write to Silver, do not give it Admin access to the entire workspace.
Important: Security misconfigurations are usually caused by convenience, not malice. Someone gets Workspace Admin because it was the fastest way to fix a permission error. A service principal gets broad access because scoping it correctly seemed complicated. Every shortcut becomes a vulnerability. Take the time to configure correctly.

sections.title: Workspace Roles and Permissions Workspace roles are the primary access control mechanism in Fabric. They determine what a user can do within a workspace. The Four Workspace Roles Role What They Can Do Who Gets This Admin Everything: manage permissions, delete workspace, manage all items, add/remove members. Platform administrators. One or two people per workspace. Member Create, edit, delete, and share all items. Cannot manage workspace settings or roles. Data engineers, senior analysts who build and maintain items. Contributor Create, edit, and delete items they own. Cannot share items or manage roles. Developers who build pipelines, notebooks, and reports. Viewer View reports and dashboards. Cannot edit, create, or delete any item. Business users who consume reports. Read-only access.

blocks.content:
Role Inheritance and OneLake
Workspace roles cascade to OneLake data access:
•  Admin, Member, Contributor: Full read and write access to all data in the workspace’s OneLake storage (Lakehouse Tables, Files, Warehouse tables). This is the broadest data access level.
•  Viewer: Read-only access to items (reports, dashboards) but NOT automatic read access to underlying OneLake data. Viewers see reports through the semantic model, which may have RLS applied.
Best Practices
•  Use security groups, not individual users. Assign the “Data Engineering” Entra ID group as workspace Members, not individual engineers.
•  Separate workspaces by environment: ws_dev, ws_staging, ws_prod. Engineers are Members in ws_dev but only Viewers (or have no role) in ws_prod.
•  Separate workspaces by security boundary: sensitive HR data in a dedicated workspace with restricted membership, not co-mingled with general analytics.
•  Minimize Admins. Most workspaces need exactly one Admin (or a small admin security group). If everyone is an Admin, the role is meaningless.
Warning: Workspace Member and Contributor roles grant full read/write access to all OneLake data in the workspace. This means a Contributor in a workspace with a Lakehouse containing PII can read that PII directly. If you need to restrict data access within a workspace, you must use OneLake security policies (Part 4) or separate the data into different workspaces.

sections.title: Item-Level and Object-Level Security Beyond workspace roles, Fabric provides finer-grained controls at the item and object level. Item-Level Sharing Individual items (reports, semantic models, Warehouses, Lakehouses) can be shared with users who do not have a workspace role: • Share a report directly with a user or group. They can view the report without having any workspace role. • Sharing a semantic model lets the recipient build their own reports on top of it (“Build” permission). • Sharing a Lakehouse or Warehouse grants read access to the SQL endpoint (ReadAll permission). • Sharing does not grant edit or delete rights. The recipient can read but not modify. SQL Object Permissions (Warehouse and SQL Database) Warehouses and SQL Databases support T-SQL GRANT / DENY / REVOKE for granular object-level security:

blocks.content: Compute-Level Permissions Item Read Path Write Path Permission Control Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint (read-only T-SQL), Spark (read Delta files) Spark Notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows Workspace role + OneLake security policies Warehouse T-SQL queries T-SQL DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) Workspace role + SQL GRANT/DENY SQL Database T-SQL queries, JDBC/ODBC T-SQL DML Database users/roles + SQL GRANT/DENY Semantic Model Power BI reports, DAX queries Model editor (web or Desktop) Workspace role + RLS in DAX KQL Database KQL queries Eventstream ingestion, management commands Workspace role + database principals

sections.title: OneLake Security — Table, Column, Row OneLake security policies provide data-level access control enforced at the storage layer. Unlike workspace roles (which are all-or-nothing for data), OneLake security lets you control which tables, columns, and rows a user can access within a Lakehouse. OneLake Data Access Roles OneLake data access roles define granular access to Lakehouse tables and folders: • Define a custom role (e.g., “Gold Read Only”) that grants read access only to tables in the Gold layer. • Assign the role to Entra ID users or groups. • Users with this role can query Gold tables through the SQL endpoint or Spark but cannot access Bronze or Silver tables. • If no OneLake security role is defined, access falls back to workspace roles (Admin/Member/Contributor get full access). Table-Level Security Restrict which tables a user can see:

blocks.content: // OneLake security role definition (conceptual — configured in the portal) Role: gold_reader Granted Tables: dim_customers, dim_products, dim_date, fact_orders Denied Tables: bronze_*, silver_*, audit.* Members: [analytics_team@contoso.com]

Column-Level Security
Column masking restricts specific columns within tables that are otherwise accessible:
•  Mask PII columns (email, phone, SSN) so that only authorized users see the real values.
•  In the Warehouse: use T-SQL DENY on specific columns (as shown in Part 3).
•  In the Lakehouse: use OneLake security policies to restrict column access at the storage layer.
Row-Level Security in OneLake
OneLake RLS filters which rows a user can see, enforced at the storage level:
•  Define a filter predicate (e.g., region = ‘North’) tied to the user’s identity.
•  Every query through any engine (Spark, SQL endpoint, Power BI DirectLake) automatically applies the filter.
•  This is more powerful than semantic model RLS because it applies everywhere, not just in Power BI.
Important: OneLake security policies are the most robust data security mechanism in Fabric because they are enforced at the storage layer. A user cannot bypass them by switching from Power BI to Spark or from the SQL endpoint to a direct file read. Always prefer OneLake security over application-level security when available.

sections.title: Row-Level Security Across Workloads RLS was introduced for Warehouses (Module 9), Semantic Models (Module 14), and SQL Databases (Module 11). In a production platform, you need RLS to behave consistently across all access paths. RLS Implementation Comparison Workload RLS Mechanism Syntax Scope Warehouse Security policy with inline predicate function CREATE SECURITY POLICY with function that filters by SESSION_CONTEXT or SUSER_SNAME() T-SQL queries to the Warehouse only. Semantic Model DAX filter expression in RLS role [region] = USERPRINCIPALNAME() or security mapping table Power BI reports and apps using the semantic model. SQL Database Security policy (same as Warehouse) CREATE SECURITY POLICY with SESSION_CONTEXT T-SQL queries to the SQL Database only. OneLake Storage-level row filter predicate Configured in OneLake security policies via the portal All access paths: Spark, SQL endpoint, DirectLake, APIs. Lakehouse SQL endpoint Inherited from OneLake policies OneLake security filters apply to SQL endpoint queries Read-only SQL queries to the Lakehouse.

blocks.content:
Ensuring Consistency
The most dangerous scenario is when RLS is applied in one layer but not another, creating a security gap:
•  A semantic model has RLS so Power BI users see only their region. But the same user has Workspace Contributor access and can open a Notebook that reads the Lakehouse directly — bypassing semantic model RLS entirely.
•  Solution: apply OneLake security policies at the storage layer. This ensures RLS is enforced regardless of the access engine.
•  If OneLake security is not yet available for your scenario, use workspace separation: put sensitive data in a restricted workspace where the user has no Notebook access.
The Security Mapping Pattern
For dynamic RLS that scales across all workloads, use a single security mapping table:

-- security_mapping table in Gold Lakehouse/Warehouse
CREATE TABLE gold.security_mapping (
    user_email     VARCHAR(256),
    region         VARCHAR(50),
    business_unit  VARCHAR(100)
);

-- Insert mappings
INSERT INTO gold.security_mapping VALUES
('alice@contoso.com', 'North', 'Retail'),
('bob@contoso.com', 'South', 'Wholesale'),
('carol@contoso.com', 'North', 'Retail'),
('carol@contoso.com', 'East', 'Retail');  -- Carol sees two regions
Reference this same table in Warehouse RLS policies, semantic model DAX filters, and OneLake security rules. One table, one source of truth, consistent access control everywhere.

sections.title: Data Protection Labels (Sensitivity Labels) Sensitivity labels are Microsoft Information Protection (MIP) labels applied to Fabric items. They classify data by sensitivity level and can enforce protection policies like encryption and access restrictions. How Sensitivity Labels Work • Classification: Labels like “Public,” “General,” “Confidential,” and “Highly Confidential” communicate the sensitivity of the data. Users see the label and understand how to handle the item. • Inheritance: When a label is applied to a Lakehouse, items derived from it (reports, downstream tables) can automatically inherit the label. If the source is “Confidential,” the report built from it inherits “Confidential.” • Enforcement: Labels can trigger protection policies: encrypt exported files, prevent sharing with external users, require specific access levels. These policies are defined in Microsoft 365 Compliance Center and enforced across Fabric. • Downstream protection: When a user exports a labeled report to Excel or PDF, the export carries the label and any associated encryption. The data remains protected even outside Fabric. Applying Labels • Manual: item owners apply labels through the item’s settings in the Fabric portal. • Automatic: configure auto-labeling policies in the Compliance Center. If a table contains columns matching patterns (SSN, credit card numbers), the label is applied automatically. • Mandatory labeling: tenant admins can require that every Fabric item must have a sensitivity label. Items without labels cannot be created or shared. Label Configuration Label Classification Typical Policy Public Data intended for external consumption. No restrictions. Can be shared freely. General Internal business data. Not sensitive. Available to all internal users. No encryption. Confidential Sensitive business data. Limited audience. Encrypted exports. Restricted sharing. No external access. Highly Confidential Most sensitive data: PII, financials, trade secrets. Encrypted at rest and in export. Restricted to named users. No external sharing. Audit all access.

blocks.content: Important: Sensitivity labels are not just decorative metadata. With enforcement policies enabled, they control what happens to data when it leaves Fabric (exports, downloads, sharing). A “Highly Confidential” label with encryption means the exported Excel file requires authentication to open — even if someone forwards it to an unauthorized recipient.

sections.title: Microsoft Purview Integration Microsoft Purview is the unified data governance platform that extends beyond Fabric to govern your entire data estate — Azure, on-premises, multi-cloud. Fabric’s native governance features are a subset of Purview’s capabilities. Purview Capabilities for Fabric Capability What It Does Value Data Catalog Searchable catalog of all data assets across Fabric and beyond. Discovery: users find data assets without asking the engineering team. Data Classification Automatic detection and tagging of sensitive data types (PII, financial, health). Compliance: know where sensitive data lives across all your Lakehouses and Warehouses. Data Lineage Column-level lineage across the entire transformation chain. Impact analysis: know what downstream reports break if a source column changes. Data Quality Define quality rules, monitor scores, track quality over time. Trust: consumers know which tables are reliable and which need attention. Glossary Business glossary linking technical column names to business definitions. Clarity: “net_revenue” in the Warehouse maps to “Net Revenue (after returns and discounts)” in the glossary. Access Policies Centralized access management. Define who can access what from Purview. Scale: manage access across hundreds of workspaces from one place.

blocks.content:
Connecting Fabric to Purview
•  Fabric is automatically registered in your organization’s Purview account (if one exists in the same Entra ID tenant).
•  Purview scans Fabric workspaces to discover and classify data assets (Lakehouses, Warehouses, semantic models).
•  Scan results populate the Purview Data Catalog with schemas, classifications, and lineage.
•  Data stewards use Purview to add business descriptions, glossary terms, and ownership metadata.
•  Data consumers search the Purview Catalog to discover datasets, understand their lineage, and request access.
Endorsements
Fabric items can be endorsed to indicate their reliability:
•  Promoted: The item owner marks it as ready for broader use. A signal that the dataset is stable and documented.
•  Certified: A designated authority (data steward, governance team) has reviewed and approved the item. Certification is the highest trust signal.
Endorsements appear in the OneLake Catalog and in Power BI when users select data sources. They help consumers distinguish trusted, governed datasets from experimental or personal items.

sections.title: Network Security and Private Endpoints By default, Fabric is a SaaS service accessed over the public internet (with Entra ID authentication). For organizations with strict network requirements, Fabric provides additional network security controls. Network Security Options Feature What It Does When to Use Managed Virtual Networks (VNet) Fabric Spark compute runs inside a Microsoft-managed VNet with outbound connectivity controls. Prevent Spark notebooks from accessing unauthorized external endpoints. Managed Private Endpoints Create private endpoints from Fabric’s Managed VNet to Azure services (SQL, Storage, Key Vault). Connect to Azure data sources over private network, not public internet. Trusted Workspace Access Allow specific Azure services to access OneLake through trusted service firewall bypass. Azure Synapse, Data Factory, or other Azure services accessing OneLake data. Conditional Access (Entra ID) Require MFA, compliant device, trusted location, or risk-based evaluation for Fabric access. Enforce device and location policies organization-wide. Tenant Allow/Block Lists Restrict which external tenants can share or receive Fabric content. Prevent data leakage to unauthorized external organizations.

blocks.content: Managed VNet Architecture

With Managed Private Endpoints, a Notebook reading from Azure SQL connects over the Microsoft backbone network, never traversing the public internet. This satisfies compliance requirements for data-in-transit security. Tip: Network security configuration is typically a tenant admin responsibility. As a data engineer, you should understand the capabilities so you can request the right configuration from your admin team. Knowing that Managed VNets exist — and when to ask for them — is as important as knowing how to write PySpark.

sections.title: Audit Logging and Compliance Audit logging answers the question: who did what, when, and to which data? This is essential for regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI-DSS) and for internal security investigations. Fabric Audit Logs Fabric generates audit events for virtually every user action. These events flow to the Microsoft 365 Unified Audit Log: Event Category Example Events Authentication User signed in, failed sign-in attempt, sign-in from unusual location. Item operations Created Lakehouse, deleted Pipeline, edited Notebook, published Report. Data access Executed query on Warehouse, read Lakehouse table, exported report to PDF. Permission changes Added user to workspace role, shared item, changed OneLake security policy. Admin actions Changed tenant setting, modified capacity assignment, enabled/disabled feature. Sensitivity labels Applied label, changed label, removed label from item.

blocks.content:
Accessing Audit Logs
•  Microsoft 365 Compliance Center → Audit → search by date range, user, activity type. The primary audit interface.
•  PowerShell: use the Search-UnifiedAuditLog cmdlet for programmatic access and bulk export.
•  Fabric Admin Portal: the Activity Log provides a subset of audit events specific to Fabric items.
•  Export to a Lakehouse: schedule a Pipeline that pulls audit logs via the Admin API and writes them to a Bronze Lakehouse for long-term storage and analysis.
Compliance Frameworks
Framework
Key Requirements
How Fabric Helps
GDPR
Right to access, right to deletion, data minimization, breach notification.
Sensitivity labels for PII. Purview classification. Audit logs for access tracking. Delta DELETE for right-to-deletion.
HIPAA
Protected Health Information (PHI) safeguards. Access controls. Audit trails.
RLS for PHI tables. Sensitivity labels with encryption. Full audit logging.
SOC 2
Security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy.
Entra ID MFA. Workspace roles. Audit logs. Managed VNets. Sensitivity labels.
PCI-DSS
Protect cardholder data. Encrypt in transit and at rest. Access control.
Column masking for card data. Managed Private Endpoints for transit encryption. RLS.

Building an Audit Dashboard
For production environments, build a dedicated audit analytics pipeline:
•  Pipeline reads audit logs daily from the Admin API and writes to a Bronze audit Lakehouse.
•  Silver Notebook cleanses and normalizes audit events (parse JSON payloads, extract user, action, item).
•  Gold tables aggregate events: access counts per user per day, permission change history, failed sign-in attempts.
•  Power BI report visualizes audit data: who accessed sensitive data, when, how often. Alert on anomalies (unusual access patterns, after-hours activity, bulk exports).
Warning: Audit logs in Microsoft 365 have a default retention of 180 days (E5 license extends to 1 year). For regulatory compliance that requires longer retention, export logs to your own Lakehouse where you control retention. Do not rely solely on the default Microsoft 365 retention period.

sections.title: Module 18 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: Fabric security model overview — four-layer model (Tenant → Workspace → Item → Data); Entra ID as identity foundation; MFA, Conditional Access, security groups, service principals; principle of least privilege
Workspace roles — Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer; role-to-OneLake data access cascade; security groups for scalable assignment; workspace separation by environment and security boundary

Item-level and object-level security — per-item sharing (report, semantic model, Lakehouse); T-SQL GRANT/DENY for schema, table, and column-level security in Warehouses; compute-level permission comparison across all item types
OneLake security — data access roles for table-level restriction; column-level security and masking; row-level security at the storage layer; enforcement across all engines (Spark, SQL, DirectLake)

RLS across workloads — five RLS implementations (Warehouse, Semantic Model, SQL Database, OneLake, Lakehouse SQL endpoint); the gap risk when RLS exists in one layer but not another; the security mapping table pattern for consistency

Sensitivity labels — MIP label classification (Public, General, Confidential, Highly Confidential); inheritance from source to downstream items; enforcement (encryption, sharing restrictions); manual, automatic, and mandatory labeling
Microsoft Purview — six capabilities (Catalog, Classification, Lineage, Quality, Glossary, Access Policies); connecting Fabric to Purview; endorsements (Promoted, Certified)
Network security — Managed VNets, Managed Private Endpoints, Trusted Workspace Access, Conditional Access, tenant allow/block lists; architecture diagram for private connectivity

Audit logging and compliance — six audit event categories; four access methods (Compliance Center, PowerShell, Admin Portal, Lakehouse export); four compliance frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI-DSS); audit dashboard pipeline pattern

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab: Implement a Security Blueprint Configure security for a sample project:

items: Create two workspaces: ws_dataplatform_dev and ws_dataplatform_prod. Assign yourself as Admin in both. Add a “Data Engineers” security group as Member in dev and Contributor in prod. Add a “Business Analysts” group as Viewer in prod.

In the prod workspace, create a Lakehouse with Gold tables. Configure OneLake security: create a “gold_reader” role that grants read access only to Gold tables. Assign the Business Analysts group to this role.
In the Warehouse, configure T-SQL object security: GRANT SELECT on the gold schema to the analyst group. DENY SELECT on the bronze and silver schemas.
Implement RLS in the Warehouse using a security mapping table and SESSION_CONTEXT. Create a security policy that filters fact_orders by the logged-in user’s region. Test with EXECUTE AS.

Apply the same RLS in the semantic model: add the security mapping table, create an RLS role with USERPRINCIPALNAME(), and test with “View as role.” Verify that a user sees the same data whether they query the Warehouse or view the Power BI report.
Apply sensitivity labels: label the Lakehouse as “Confidential.” Verify that the label inherits to the semantic model and report.
Search for your Gold tables in the OneLake Catalog. Add descriptions to key tables and columns. Endorse the semantic model as “Promoted.”
Access the audit log in the Microsoft 365 Compliance Center. Search for your recent activities (Lakehouse creation, permission changes, report views). Export the results and verify the event details.

blocks.content: Security is not a one-time configuration. It is a continuous practice: reviewing permissions as teams change, monitoring audit logs for anomalies, updating RLS mappings as the business evolves, and ensuring that sensitivity labels flow with the data as it moves through the platform.

In Module 19, we cover the operational layer: Administration, Capacity, and DevOps — tenant administration, capacity management, Git integration, CI/CD deployment pipelines, and monitoring production workloads.

chapters.title: Administration, Capacity & DevOps
chapters.description: Operating the Platform at Production Scale Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Administer the Fabric tenant, manage capacity and cost, implement CI/CD with Git integration and deployment pipelines, automate with APIs, monitor production workloads, organize with domains, and plan for disaster recovery.

sections.title: Module Overview

blocks.content: Welcome to Module 19 Building a data platform is one challenge. Operating it is another. A platform that works perfectly in development can fail in production because of capacity throttling, uncontrolled changes, missing monitoring, or a deployment process that relies on manual clicks. This module covers the operational discipline that turns a collection of Fabric items into a reliable, scalable, governed production system. Tenant administration controls what the organization can do. Capacity management controls how much compute is available and how much it costs. Git integration and deployment pipelines control how changes move from development to production. Monitoring and alerting catch failures before users do. These are not glamorous topics. There are no flashy visualizations or clever DAX formulas. But operational excellence is what separates a demo from a production platform. A platform without monitoring is flying blind. A platform without CI/CD is a single bad commit away from a production outage. A platform without capacity management is a surprise invoice waiting to happen. This module covers: tenant administration, capacity management, Spark autoscale billing, Git integration, deployment pipelines, CI/CD automation, monitoring and alerting, domains and data mesh organization, disaster recovery, and a hands-on production governance checkpoint.

sections.title: Tenant Administration The Fabric Admin Portal is the central control plane for the entire organization’s Fabric environment. Tenant admins control which features are enabled, who can use them, and how the platform behaves globally. Key Tenant Settings Setting Category What It Controls Examples Workspace creation Who can create workspaces. Restrict to specific security groups to prevent workspace sprawl. Export and sharing What users can export and with whom they can share. Disable “Publish to web.” Restrict external sharing to approved domains. Copilot and AI Whether Copilot features are enabled. Enable Copilot for specific groups. Control data sent to AI services. Developer settings API access, embed codes, service principal authentication. Allow service principals for CI/CD automation. Integration settings Git integration, Azure DevOps/GitHub connections. Enable Git integration for specific workspaces. OneLake settings OneLake data access policies, external data sharing. Control whether OneLake data can be accessed from outside Fabric. Capacity settings Default capacity assignment, autoscale policies. Set the default capacity for new workspaces.

blocks.content:
The Admin Portal
•  Access via the Fabric portal gear icon → Admin portal (requires Fabric Admin or Global Admin role).
•  Tenant settings: feature flags organized by category. Each setting can be enabled for the entire organization or scoped to specific security groups.
•  Usage metrics: see which workspaces, items, and users consume the most capacity.
•  Capacity management: assign workspaces to capacities, view utilization, configure autoscale.
•  Domains: organize workspaces into business domains (covered in Part 8).
Important: Tenant settings are the highest-priority governance controls. A single tenant setting — like disabling “Publish to web” — prevents the most common data leakage scenario across the entire organization. Review tenant settings before anything else when setting up a new Fabric environment.

sections.title: Capacity Management and Monitoring All Fabric workloads consume Capacity Units (CUs) from a shared capacity pool. Understanding how CU consumption works is essential for cost management and performance reliability. How Capacity Works • Capacity SKUs: Fabric capacities are purchased in SKU sizes (F2, F4, F8, F16, F32, F64, F128, etc.). Each SKU provides a defined number of CUs per second. Higher SKUs provide more parallel compute. • Shared pool: All workloads (Spark, Warehouse, Power BI, Pipelines, KQL) draw from the same CU pool. A large Spark job and a Warehouse query compete for the same CUs. • Throttling: When CU demand exceeds capacity, Fabric throttles by queuing requests. Interactive workloads (report queries) are prioritized over background workloads (Spark jobs, pipeline runs). • Smoothing: CU consumption is smoothed over time windows. Short bursts are tolerated; sustained overconsumption triggers throttling. Fabric smoothes background operations over a 24-hour window and interactive operations over shorter windows. Capacity SKU Reference SKU CUs Spark vCores (approx) Power BI Equivalent Typical Use F2 2 8 N/A Individual developer, small projects. F4 4 16 N/A Small team development. F8 8 32 N/A Small production workloads. F16 16 64 N/A Medium production workloads. F32 32 128 N/A Large production workloads. F64 64 256 P1 equivalent Enterprise. Copilot eligible. F128 128 512 P2 equivalent Large enterprise workloads.

blocks.content:
The Capacity Metrics App
Microsoft provides a dedicated Power BI app for monitoring capacity utilization:
•  Install from the Fabric Admin Portal or from AppSource.
•  Tracks CU utilization over time, broken down by workload type (Spark, Warehouse, Power BI, Pipeline).
•  Shows throttling events: when demand exceeded supply and requests were queued.
•  Identifies top consumers: which workspaces, items, or users are consuming the most CUs.
•  Helps right-size capacity: if utilization is consistently below 50%, consider downgrading. If throttling is frequent, upgrade.
Warning: Fabric capacity billing is continuous. Even when no workloads are running, the capacity incurs cost (unless paused). For development and testing, use the capacity pause/resume feature to stop billing during non-working hours. Automate pause/resume with Azure Logic Apps or PowerShell for cost optimization.

sections.title: Autoscale Billing for Spark In addition to fixed-capacity CUs, Fabric offers serverless Spark billing — a pay-per-use model where Spark compute is billed only when Notebooks and Spark jobs actually run. This can significantly reduce costs for workloads with irregular or unpredictable usage patterns. Fixed Capacity vs. Autoscale Model How It Works Cost Pattern Best For Fixed capacity CUs All Spark compute draws from the pre-purchased CU pool. Fixed monthly cost regardless of usage. Predictable, steady Spark workloads that run throughout the day. Autoscale (serverless) Spark compute scales dynamically. Billed per vCore-second consumed. Variable cost. Pay only for what you use. Bursty workloads: nightly batch jobs, ad-hoc notebooks, dev/test.

blocks.content:
Cost Optimization Strategies
•  Use autoscale for dev/test workspaces where Spark usage is unpredictable and intermittent.
•  Use fixed capacity for production workloads with consistent daily schedules (nightly batch pipelines that run every night at the same time).
•  Right-size Spark environments: reduce the default node count if your notebooks do not need 4 or 8 executors. A smaller cluster finishes slower but costs less.
•  Optimize Spark code: efficient PySpark transformations (partition pruning, predicate pushdown, broadcast joins) reduce the amount of compute needed per job.
•  Schedule heavy workloads during off-peak hours when capacity contention is lowest.
•  Monitor cost trends weekly using the Capacity Metrics app. Catch cost spikes early before they compound.
Tip: For organizations just starting with Fabric, begin with a smaller capacity (F4 or F8) and autoscale for Spark. Monitor utilization for 30 days. Then right-size based on actual usage patterns. Starting too large wastes budget; starting too small causes throttling — but it is easier to upgrade than to downgrade.

sections.title: Git Integration Git integration connects a Fabric workspace to a Git repository in Azure DevOps or GitHub. Every supported Fabric item (Notebook, Pipeline, Semantic Model, Report, Warehouse, Lakehouse metadata, and more) is serialized into text files and tracked in version control. Why Git Integration Matters • Version history: Every change is a commit. You can see who changed what, when, and why. Rollback to any previous version. • Branching: Develop new features in a branch without affecting production. Merge when ready. Review changes before they go live. • Collaboration: Multiple developers work on the same workspace items without overwriting each other’s changes. • Disaster recovery: The Git repository is an independent backup of all workspace definitions. If a workspace is accidentally deleted, re-create it from Git.
