Connecting a Workspace to Git • Navigate to Workspace settings → Git integration. • Select the Git provider: Azure DevOps (Azure Repos) or GitHub. • Authenticate and select the repository, branch, and folder. • The initial sync pulls existing workspace items into the repo (or vice versa). • From this point, changes flow bidirectionally: edit in the Fabric portal and commit to Git, or edit in Git and sync to the workspace. Git Workflow

blocks.content: Developer workflow:

items: Create a feature branch in Git
Connect a dev workspace to the feature branch
Make changes in the Fabric portal (edit notebooks, pipelines)
Commit changes to the feature branch
Create a Pull Request to the main branch
Code review and approval
Merge to main
Sync the production workspace from main

blocks.content: Important: Git integration tracks item definitions (metadata, code, configuration), not data. Your Delta tables, Parquet files, and Warehouse data are not stored in Git. Git manages the “code” — the notebooks, pipelines, semantic model definitions, and reports. Data management is handled separately through your medallion architecture.

sections.title: Deployment Pipelines Deployment pipelines provide a visual, stage-based promotion workflow for moving Fabric items from development through testing to production. They are Fabric’s native CI/CD mechanism. The Stage Model

blocks.content:
Development          Test / Staging         Production
  ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐
  │ ws_dev     │ ──→ │ ws_test    │ ──→ │ ws_prod    │
  │            │     │            │     │            │
  │ Notebooks  │     │ Notebooks  │     │ Notebooks  │
  │ Pipelines  │     │ Pipelines  │     │ Pipelines  │
  │ Reports    │     │ Reports    │     │ Reports    │
  └────────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘

Creating a Deployment Pipeline
•  Navigate to the Fabric portal → Deployment Pipelines → Create pipeline.
•  Name the pipeline and define stages (typically: Development, Test, Production).
•  Assign a workspace to each stage.
•  The pipeline compares items across stages: added, modified, unchanged.
•  Click “Deploy” to promote items from one stage to the next.
Deployment Rules and Parameterization
Different stages often need different configurations (connection strings, file paths, Lakehouse references). Deployment rules handle this:
•  Data source rules: change the Lakehouse or Warehouse connection when deploying from dev to prod.
•  Parameter rules: update Pipeline parameters (e.g., file paths, schema names) per stage.
•  The deployed item’s code is identical across stages; only the parameterized values change.
Deployment Pipelines vs. Git Integration

Git Integration Deployment Pipelines Primary purpose Version control and collaboration. Stage-based promotion (Dev → Test → Prod). Change tracking Full commit history per item. Branching and merging. Comparison between stages (what changed?). Granularity Individual commits. Fine-grained change control. Stage-level promotion. Deploy all changes or selected items. Best used together Store all definitions in Git for history and collaboration. Use deployment pipelines to promote validated changes to production.

Important: Git integration and deployment pipelines are complementary. Git tracks all changes with full history and branching. Deployment pipelines provide the governed promotion workflow from stage to stage. Production environments should use both: Git for version control, deployment pipelines for controlled releases.

sections.title: CI/CD Automation For mature teams, manual deployment through the portal is insufficient. CI/CD automation uses APIs and tools to programmatically create, deploy, and test Fabric items. Automation Tools Tool What It Does Use Case Fabric REST APIs Create, read, update, delete Fabric items programmatically. Automate workspace creation, item deployment, trigger pipeline runs. Azure DevOps Pipelines + Fabric Tasks CI/CD pipeline stages with Fabric-specific tasks. Automated build-test-deploy on every Git merge to main. GitHub Actions CI/CD workflows triggered by Git events. Same as Azure DevOps Pipelines but for GitHub-hosted repos. Fabric CLI Command-line interface for Fabric operations. Scripted deployments, batch operations, developer productivity. Terraform Provider Infrastructure-as-code for Fabric resources. Declarative workspace and capacity provisioning. Repeatable environments. PowerShell modules Cmdlets for Fabric admin and workspace operations. Admin automation: bulk permission changes, capacity management.

blocks.content: Automated CI/CD Pattern

Developer pushes code to Git (feature branch)
       │
       v
  Pull Request created → Code review + automated checks
       │
       v
  Merge to main → CI pipeline triggers:
       │
       ├── Sync workspace from Git (Fabric REST API)
       ├── Run data quality tests (trigger test notebook)
       ├── Validate semantic model (DAX queries return expected results)
       │
       v
  All checks pass → Deployment Pipeline promotes to Production
       │
       v
  Post-deployment smoke test (verify pipeline run, report load)
REST API Example

# Trigger a Pipeline run via REST API
import requests

url = 'https://api.fabric.microsoft.com/v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}'
      '/items/{pipeline_id}/jobs/instances?jobType=Pipeline'

headers = {
    'Authorization': f'Bearer {access_token}',
    'Content-Type': 'application/json'
}

response = requests.post(url, headers=headers)
print(f'Status: {response.status_code}')
print(f'Job ID: {response.json().get("id")}')

sections.title: Monitoring and Alerting Production workloads must be monitored continuously. Silent failures — a pipeline that stops running, a notebook that produces wrong results, a report that times out — are more damaging than loud failures. Fabric Monitoring Hub The Monitoring Hub is the central dashboard for all Fabric activity: • Pipeline runs: status (Succeeded, Failed, In Progress), duration, trigger type. Drill into activity-level details. • Spark jobs: Notebook runs, Spark application status, executor utilization, duration. • Dataflow runs: Dataflow Gen2 refresh status and duration. • SQL queries: Warehouse and Lakehouse SQL endpoint query history. • KQL queries: Eventhouse query logs. Setting Up Alerts Alert Type Trigger Notification Channel Pipeline failure Pipeline run status = Failed. Email, Teams, Power Automate. Pipeline SLA breach Pipeline duration exceeds threshold (e.g., > 2 hours). Email, Teams. Spark job failure Notebook or Spark job fails with exception. Email, Teams, Power Automate. Capacity throttling CU utilization exceeds threshold for N minutes. Capacity Metrics app alert + email. Data freshness No new data in a table for N hours (custom quality check). Custom alert via Pipeline error path. Report failure Semantic model refresh fails or report load times out. Power BI alert + email.

blocks.content: Building a Monitoring Pipeline For comprehensive monitoring, build a dedicated monitoring pipeline that checks all production systems daily:

Monitoring Pipeline (runs every 30 minutes): ├── Check: Are all nightly pipelines complete? (query pipeline run history) ├── Check: Are all Gold tables fresh? (MAX(load_timestamp) within SLA) ├── Check: Are data quality scores above threshold? (query audit.quality_log) ├── Check: Are semantic model refreshes successful? (query refresh history) └── If any check fails: send Teams alert + log to monitoring table

Tip: The most valuable alert is the one that fires before the user notices. If your business stakeholder opens a dashboard and sees yesterday’s data, your monitoring has failed. A freshness alert at 7 AM catches the problem before the 9 AM standup.

sections.title: Domains and Data Mesh Organization Fabric Domains provide an organizational layer above workspaces. They group related workspaces by business domain (Sales, Marketing, Finance, HR), enabling delegated governance and data mesh principles. What Domains Provide • Logical grouping: Domains group workspaces that belong to the same business area. The Sales domain might contain ws_sales_dev, ws_sales_prod, ws_sales_analytics. • Delegated governance: Domain admins can manage settings and endorsements within their domain without needing tenant-level admin rights. • Discovery: The OneLake Catalog can filter by domain, helping users find data assets within their business context. • Ownership: Domains establish clear data ownership. The Sales team owns and governs the Sales domain. The Finance team owns the Finance domain. Data Mesh Principles in Fabric The data mesh paradigm aligns well with Fabric’s domain model: Data Mesh Principle Fabric Implementation Domain-oriented ownership Domains with domain admins. Each team owns their data products. Data as a product Gold Lakehouse tables with documentation, quality SLAs, and endorsements. Self-serve platform Fabric provides the shared infrastructure. Domains build on top of it. Federated governance Tenant admin sets global policies. Domain admins manage within their scope.

blocks.content: Configuring Domains

items: In the Admin Portal → Domains → Create domain.
Name the domain (e.g., Sales, Marketing, Finance).
Assign domain admins (responsible for governance within the domain).
Assign workspaces to the domain.
Optionally create sub-domains for more granular organization (Sales → Retail Sales, Sales → Wholesale Sales).

sections.title: Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Fabric is a SaaS platform — Microsoft manages the infrastructure. But SaaS does not eliminate disaster recovery concerns. It changes them. What Microsoft Manages • Infrastructure availability: compute, storage, and networking uptime. Covered by the Fabric SLA (99.9% for most workloads). • Storage durability: OneLake data is stored in Azure Storage with geo-redundant replication. Data loss due to hardware failure is extremely unlikely. • Platform updates: Microsoft patches and updates the Fabric platform without customer intervention. What You Must Manage Risk Impact Mitigation Accidental deletion User deletes a workspace, Lakehouse, or critical table. Git integration (item definitions recoverable). OneLake soft-delete and recycle bin (data recovery). Workspace recovery in Admin Portal. Bad deployment A code change corrupts Gold tables or breaks reports. Deployment pipelines with test stage. Rollback via Git revert. Delta Lake time travel for data recovery. Region outage The Azure region hosting your Fabric capacity goes down. Fabric BCDR (Business Continuity Disaster Recovery) with geo-redundant capacity. Cross-region shortcuts. Data corruption Pipeline bug writes incorrect data to production tables. Data quality gates (Module 17). Delta Lake time travel (RESTORE TABLE). Bronze immutability for full reprocess. Credential compromise Service principal or user account is compromised. Entra ID Conditional Access. Immediate credential rotation. Audit log review. Least privilege.

blocks.content: Delta Lake Time Travel for Recovery
-- Recover a table to a previous state using Delta Lake time travel
-- View table history DESCRIBE HISTORY gold.fact_orders;
-- Read the table as of a specific version SELECT * FROM gold.fact_orders VERSION AS OF 42;
-- Read the table as of a specific timestamp SELECT * FROM gold.fact_orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-01-15T08:00:00';
-- Restore the table to a previous version RESTORE TABLE gold.fact_orders TO VERSION AS OF 42;

Recovery Playbook
Document a recovery playbook before disaster strikes:
•  Table corruption: use Delta Lake time travel (RESTORE TABLE) to roll back to the last known good version. Rerun the pipeline from that point.
•  Item deletion: check the workspace recycle bin (items are retained for a period). If the workspace itself is deleted, use the Admin Portal workspace recovery. Redeploy item definitions from Git.
•  Region outage: if BCDR capacity is provisioned, Fabric fails over automatically. If not, wait for Microsoft to restore the region (typical recovery: hours to a day).
•  Credential compromise: immediately revoke the compromised credential in Entra ID. Review audit logs for unauthorized activity. Rotate all secrets used by the compromised identity.
Warning: Delta Lake time travel depends on the vacuum retention period. By default, old versions are kept for 7 days. After VACUUM removes them, they are gone. For critical Gold tables, increase the retention period (delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration) to 30 or 90 days.

sections.title: Module 19 Checkpoint You now understand:

items: Tenant administration — the Admin Portal, seven key tenant setting categories, feature flag scoping to security groups, usage metrics
Capacity management — CU consumption model (shared pool, throttling, smoothing), SKU reference from F2 to F128, the Capacity Metrics app for utilization tracking and right-sizing
Spark autoscale billing — fixed capacity vs. serverless pay-per-use, six cost optimization strategies, starting small and right-sizing based on actual usage
Git integration — connecting workspaces to Azure DevOps or GitHub, the feature-branch workflow (branch → develop → PR → review → merge → sync), item definitions in Git (not data)
Deployment pipelines — three-stage promotion (Dev → Test → Prod), deployment rules for parameterization across stages, Git + deployment pipelines as complementary tools
CI/CD automation — six automation tools (REST APIs, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, Fabric CLI, Terraform, PowerShell), the automated CI/CD pattern from push to post-deployment smoke test
Monitoring and alerting — the Monitoring Hub (pipelines, Spark, Dataflows, SQL, KQL), six alert types, building a dedicated monitoring pipeline for proactive issue detection
Domains and data mesh — domains as organizational layer above workspaces, delegated governance, four data mesh principles mapped to Fabric capabilities
Disaster recovery — what Microsoft manages vs. what you manage, five risk scenarios with mitigations, Delta Lake time travel for table recovery, the recovery playbook

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab: Set Up a Production Governance Framework Configure the operational infrastructure:

items: Connect your development workspace to a Git repository (Azure DevOps or GitHub). Commit all existing items. Create a feature branch, make a change to a Notebook, commit, and create a Pull Request.

Create a Deployment Pipeline with three stages: Development, Test, Production. Assign a workspace to each stage. Deploy items from Development to Test. Configure a deployment rule that changes the Lakehouse reference when deploying from Test to Production.

Set up monitoring: create a Notebook that checks whether last night’s Pipeline run succeeded and whether all Gold tables have been updated within the last 24 hours. Schedule this Notebook to run every morning at 7 AM.
Configure an alert: if the monitoring Notebook detects a failure or stale data, send a Teams notification (or email) to the on-call engineer.
Create a domain in the Admin Portal (e.g., “Sales Analytics”). Assign your production workspace to the domain. Assign yourself as domain admin.
Practice disaster recovery: deliberately delete a Gold table. Use Delta Lake time travel (RESTORE TABLE) to recover it. Verify the data is restored correctly.
Review the Capacity Metrics app: identify which workloads consumed the most CUs over the past week. Note any throttling events and determine whether capacity right-sizing is needed.

blocks.content: You have built the operational foundation. Git integration provides version control. Deployment pipelines provide governed promotion. Monitoring catches failures. Domains provide organizational clarity. Recovery playbooks prepare for the unexpected. This is the difference between a data platform and a production data platform.
In Module 20, we bring everything together in the Capstone — End-to-End Data Platform Project — a complete, production-ready implementation that exercises every skill from Modules 1–19.

chapters.title: Capstone — End-to-End Data Platform Project
chapters.description: Everything You Have Learned, Unified Into One Platform Estimated Time: 180 Minutes Module Objective: Apply everything from Modules 1–19 to build a complete, production-ready data platform in Microsoft Fabric. Receive a business brief, design the architecture, implement medallion ingestion, build a star schema, create a Warehouse for SQL analytics, build a Power BI report, implement security and governance, configure CI/CD, and present the finished platform. Welcome to the Capstone Nineteen modules. One hundred and ninety lessons. You have learned the entire Microsoft Fabric platform: OneLake, Lakehouses, Warehouses, SQL Databases, Pipelines, Dataflows, Notebooks, PySpark, Spark SQL, T-SQL, KQL, Eventstreams, Activator, Power BI, DAX, Data Science, MLflow, the medallion architecture, star schema modeling, security, governance, Purview, administration, capacity, and DevOps. Now you build it all. The capstone is not a tutorial. It is a project. You will receive a business brief with requirements. You will design the architecture. You will implement every layer — from raw ingestion to interactive dashboards. You will secure it, govern it, and prepare it for production deployment. This module is structured as ten sequential phases. Each phase builds on the previous. At the end, you will have a complete data platform that demonstrates mastery of every Fabric workload. There is no step-by-step code in this module. Each phase describes what to build and the acceptance criteria, but the implementation decisions are yours. Choose the patterns, write the code, and make the trade-offs. This is how real platforms are built. Phase 1: Project Brief and Architecture Design The Business Brief You are the lead data engineer for Contoso Retail, a multi-region e-commerce company. The business has four data sources and four analytical requirements: Data Sources Source Format Volume Delivery ERP (Orders & Customers) CSV files dropped daily into Azure Storage. ~500K orders/year, ~100K customers. Daily batch. Full customer extract + incremental orders (new only). Product Catalog REST API returning JSON. ~5K products. Full refresh weekly. Weekly batch via Pipeline HTTP connector. Web Clickstream Real-time events from the company website. ~1M events/day. Continuous. Streaming via Event Hub. Support Tickets CSV file from CRM system. ~50K tickets/year. Daily batch. Business Requirements Requirement Description Consumer R1: Sales Dashboard Executive dashboard showing revenue by region, product category, and time. YoY comparison. Top 10 customers. CFO, VP Sales (Power BI report) R2: Customer 360 Single view of each customer: orders, spend history, clickstream behavior, support tickets, churn risk. Account managers (Power BI drill-through) R3: Real-Time Traffic Live monitoring of website traffic: page views per minute, top pages, geographic distribution. Digital marketing team (Real-Time Dashboard) R4: Churn Prediction ML model predicting customer churn. Batch scored weekly. Results visible in Customer 360 report. Account managers, retention team Architecture Design Task Before writing any code, design the architecture. Produce a diagram (ASCII, whiteboard, or tool of your choice) that answers: • Which workspaces? How many? Named how? • Which Lakehouses? Bronze, Silver, Gold — one per layer? • Which Warehouse? For which SQL analysts? • Which Pipelines? One master orchestration or one per source? • Which Notebooks? Named and scoped to which transformation? • Which Eventhouse and Eventstream? For which real-time source? • Which semantic model and reports? DirectLake connection to which Gold tables? • Which ML experiment and model? Scoring Notebook and schedule? • Which security roles and RLS rules? • Which Git repository and deployment pipeline? Reference Architecture ws_contoso_dev / ws_contoso_prod │ ├── lh_bronze │ ├── Tables: raw_orders, raw_customers, raw_products, raw_tickets │ └── Files: /landing/erp/, /landing/catalog/, /landing/crm/ │ ├── lh_silver │ └── Tables: orders, customers, products, tickets (cleansed) │ ├── lh_gold │ └── Tables: fact_orders, dim_customers (SCD2), dim_products, │ dim_date, agg_daily_revenue, customer_churn_predictions │ ├── wh_analytics (Warehouse for T-SQL analysts) ├── eh_clickstream (Eventhouse + KQL Database for web traffic) ├── es_web_traffic (Eventstream: Event Hub → Eventhouse + Lakehouse) ├── sm_contoso (Semantic model via DirectLake on lh_gold) ├── rpt_sales (Power BI: Sales Dashboard) ├── rpt_customer360 (Power BI: Customer 360 with drill-through) ├── rtd_web_traffic (Real-Time Dashboard on eh_clickstream) └── exp_churn (ML experiment + registered model) Important: This reference architecture is a suggestion. You may adjust it based on your reasoning. The key is that every item exists for a purpose and every data flow is traceable from source to consumer. Phase 2: OneLake and Lakehouse Setup Deliverables • Create the workspace(s). Follow your architecture design. • Create lh_bronze, lh_silver, lh_gold Lakehouses. • Create the Eventhouse (eh_clickstream) with a KQL Database. • Create the Warehouse (wh_analytics). • Establish the Files/ landing zone structure in lh_bronze (folders per source). • If using shortcuts, create any cross-Lakehouse shortcuts needed. Acceptance Criteria • Three Lakehouses exist with correct names and are accessible. • The Eventhouse has a KQL Database with a table for clickstream events (schema defined). • The Warehouse exists and is ready for Gold views or cross-database queries. • The Files/ landing zone has folders: /landing/erp/, /landing/catalog/, /landing/crm/. Modules Referenced Module 1 (Platform Foundations), Module 2 (OneLake), Module 3 (Lakehouse Fundamentals), Module 9 (Warehouse), Module 12 (Eventhouse). Phase 3: Data Ingestion — Pipelines and Eventstreams Deliverables • Pipeline pl_ingest_erp: Copy Activity to land ERP CSV files (orders + customers) from Azure Storage into lh_bronze/Files/landing/erp/. Parameterize the date so it is reusable. • Pipeline pl_ingest_catalog: HTTP connector to pull product catalog JSON from a REST API (or simulate with a local JSON file). Land in lh_bronze/Files/landing/catalog/. • Pipeline pl_ingest_tickets: Copy Activity for support tickets CSV. Land in lh_bronze/Files/landing/crm/. • Eventstream es_web_traffic: Connect a sample data source (or simulated Event Hub) as the source. Route to the Eventhouse KQL Database as the primary destination. Add a second destination to lh_bronze (for long-term batch storage). • Master orchestration Pipeline pl_daily_refresh: calls the three batch ingestion pipelines in sequence (or parallel where independent). Acceptance Criteria • ERP, catalog, and ticket files land in the correct Files/ folders in lh_bronze. • The Eventstream is active and events are flowing into the KQL Database. • Events are also landing in lh_bronze for batch processing. • The master Pipeline runs successfully end-to-end. Modules Referenced Module 4 (Pipelines), Module 12 (Eventhouse ingestion), Module 13 (Eventstreams). Phase 4: Bronze Layer — Raw Data Landing Deliverables • Notebook nb_bronze_load: Read landed files from Files/landing/ and write to Bronze Delta tables with metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system, _source_file, _load_batch_id). Append-only writes. • Bronze tables: raw_orders, raw_customers, raw_products, raw_tickets in lh_bronze Tables/. • The Notebook is parameterized to accept a source name and process that source’s files. • Add the Bronze Notebook as an activity in the master Pipeline (runs after file landing). Acceptance Criteria • All four Bronze tables exist and contain data with correct metadata columns. • Running the Notebook a second time appends new data without duplicating existing data (files are processed based on watermark or landing folder structure). • The master Pipeline now executes: file landing → Bronze loading. Modules Referenced Module 7 (PySpark Notebooks), Module 17 (Bronze Layer design). Phase 5: Silver Layer — Cleansing Notebooks Deliverables • Notebook nb_silver_customers: Read raw_customers from Bronze, deduplicate by customer_id (keep latest _load_timestamp), cast types, handle nulls, standardize (uppercase names, lowercase emails, coalesce region to ‘Unknown’). MERGE into silver.customers. • Notebook nb_silver_orders: Read raw_orders, deduplicate by order_id, cast amounts to DECIMAL, validate order_date (reject future dates), MERGE into silver.orders. • Notebook nb_silver_products: Read raw_products from latest catalog load, deduplicate by product_id, standardize category names. Overwrite silver.products (Type 1 — products are small and fully refreshed). • Notebook nb_silver_tickets: Read raw_tickets, deduplicate, standardize status values, MERGE into silver.tickets. • Add all four Silver Notebooks to the master Pipeline after Bronze loading. Acceptance Criteria • Four Silver tables exist in lh_silver: customers, orders, products, tickets. • No duplicate business keys in any Silver table. • All columns have correct data types. No raw strings where dates or decimals belong. • Nulls in critical columns (customer_id, order_id) are rejected (logged or filtered). • The master Pipeline now executes: file landing → Bronze → Silver. Modules Referenced Module 7 (PySpark), Module 8 (Spark SQL), Module 17 (Silver Layer, MERGE patterns). Phase 6: Gold Layer — Star Schema and Aggregates Deliverables • Notebook nb_gold_dim_date: Generate dim_date using a date spine (calendar table from 2020–2030). Columns: date_key, calendar_date, year, quarter, month, month_name, day_of_week, is_weekday. • Notebook nb_gold_dim_customers: Build dim_customers with SCD Type 2 from silver.customers. Surrogate key (customer_sk), business key (customer_id), customer_name, email, region, segment, effective_from, effective_to, is_current. • Notebook nb_gold_dim_products: Build dim_products from silver.products. Surrogate key, product_id, product_name, category, subcategory. • Notebook nb_gold_fact_orders: Join silver.orders to Gold dimensions using business keys. Resolve to surrogate keys. Calculate line_amount and net_amount. Write fact_orders. • Notebook nb_gold_aggregates: Build agg_daily_revenue (date, region, category, revenue, order_count, unique_customers). • Add all Gold Notebooks to the master Pipeline after Silver. Acceptance Criteria • Star schema exists in lh_gold: dim_date, dim_customers (SCD2), dim_products, fact_orders, agg_daily_revenue. • dim_customers has at least one example of SCD Type 2: a customer whose region changed, with an expired row (is_current = 0) and a current row (is_current = 1). • fact_orders uses surrogate keys for all dimension joins. No orphan keys (every fact row joins to a valid dimension row). • agg_daily_revenue matches the SUM of fact_orders when queried for the same dimensions. • The master Pipeline now executes: file landing → Bronze → Silver → Gold. Modules Referenced Module 7 (PySpark), Module 9 (Warehouse star schema concepts), Module 10 (SCD Type 2 in T-SQL), Module 17 (Gold Layer, SCD in Delta Lake, aggregate tables). Phase 7: Warehouse and T-SQL Analytics Deliverables • In wh_analytics, create views or shortcuts that expose the Gold star schema tables for T-SQL analysts. • Create a stored procedure usp_customer_lifetime_value that calculates lifetime revenue, order count, first order date, last order date, and average order value per customer. Write the results to a summary table. • Create a view vw_monthly_revenue that aggregates revenue by year, month, region, and category — providing a T-SQL-accessible summary for ad-hoc analysis. • Add cross-database queries that join Warehouse tables to Lakehouse Gold tables (if using the Lakehouse+Warehouse hybrid pattern from Module 17). Acceptance Criteria • T-SQL analysts can query Gold tables from wh_analytics without needing Spark or Notebook access. • The stored procedure runs successfully and produces correct lifetime value metrics. • The monthly revenue view returns results consistent with the Gold aggregate table. Modules Referenced Module 9 (Warehouse), Module 10 (T-SQL Analytics), Module 11 (SQL Database — if relevant). Phase 8: Semantic Model and Power BI Report Deliverables • Create semantic model sm_contoso in DirectLake mode on lh_gold. Include: fact_orders, dim_customers, dim_products, dim_date, agg_daily_revenue, customer_churn_predictions (once Phase ML is complete). • Define star schema relationships: dim_date → fact_orders, dim_customers → fact_orders, dim_products → fact_orders. All one-to-many, single cross-filter direction. • Write DAX measures: Total Revenue, Total Orders, Unique Customers, Average Order Value, Revenue Per Customer, YTD Revenue, Revenue LY (SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR), YoY Growth %, Rolling 12-Month Revenue, Customer Rank (RANKX). • Implement RLS: create a security mapping table, define a role with USERPRINCIPALNAME(). Test with “View as role.” • Build rpt_sales (Sales Dashboard): three-page report. Executive Summary page (cards, KPI, trend line, YoY comparison). Regional Analysis page (bar chart, map, slicers). Product Analysis page (category breakdown, top products). • Build rpt_customer360 (Customer 360): detail page with drill-through by customer_id. Show customer profile, order history table, spend trend line, support ticket list, and churn risk score (once ML predictions are available). • Build rtd_web_traffic (Real-Time Dashboard): three tiles on the Eventhouse KQL Database. Page views per minute (timechart), top 10 pages (bar chart), latest events (table). Auto-refresh every 30 seconds. Acceptance Criteria • Semantic model is in DirectLake mode. Relationships are correct. Measures return expected values. • RLS restricts data by region based on the logged-in user. • Sales Dashboard follows visual hierarchy: headline metrics → trends → detail. • Customer 360 drill-through works: right-click a customer on any summary page → detail page shows only that customer. • Real-Time Dashboard updates live as events flow through the Eventstream. Modules Referenced Module 14 (Semantic Models & DAX), Module 15 (Reports, Dashboards, Distribution), Module 12 (Real-Time Dashboards). Phase 9: Security, Governance, and Deployment Deliverables • Workspace roles: configure at least two roles with different access levels. Engineers as Members, analysts as Viewers (or use appropriate OneLake security roles). • T-SQL object security: in wh_analytics, GRANT SELECT on gold schema to the analyst group. DENY on bronze and silver schemas. • Sensitivity labels: apply “Confidential” to the Lakehouse containing customer PII. Verify inheritance to the semantic model. • Data quality gate: add a quality check Notebook between Silver and Gold in the master Pipeline. Validate: no null primary keys, no duplicate business keys, referential integrity (all fact foreign keys match dimension keys). If quality fails, halt the Pipeline and send an alert. • Git integration: connect the workspace to a Git repository. Commit all items. Create a feature branch, make a change, create a Pull Request, and merge. • Deployment Pipeline: create a three-stage deployment pipeline (Dev → Test → Prod). Deploy items from Dev to Test. Configure a deployment rule that changes the Lakehouse reference for the Prod stage. • Monitoring Notebook: create nb_monitoring that checks pipeline run status and data freshness. Schedule to run every morning. Acceptance Criteria • A Viewer-role user can see Power BI reports but cannot access Bronze/Silver tables. • RLS in the semantic model and Warehouse produce the same filtered results for the same user. • The quality gate halts the Pipeline when a quality check fails (test by introducing a deliberate data issue). • All items are committed to Git with meaningful commit messages. • The deployment pipeline successfully promotes items from Dev to Test. • The monitoring Notebook runs on schedule and would alert on a failure. Modules Referenced Module 17 (Data Quality), Module 18 (Security, Governance, Purview), Module 19 (Git, Deployment Pipelines, Monitoring). Phase 10: Final Presentation and Architecture Review The capstone concludes with a presentation of the completed platform. This is not a formality — it is a critical professional skill. Every data platform needs to be communicated to stakeholders who will never open a Notebook or read a DAX formula. Presentation Structure • Slide 1 — Architecture Diagram: The complete data flow from sources to consumers. Annotate with item names and workload types. • Slide 2 — Medallion Pipeline: Bronze → Silver → Gold transformation chain. Highlight the key transformations and quality gates. • Slide 3 — Star Schema: Entity-relationship diagram of the Gold layer. State the grain of each fact table. • Slide 4 — Live Demo: Walk through the master Pipeline run. Show data flowing from Bronze to Gold. Open the Sales Dashboard and demonstrate slicers, drill-through, and RLS. • Slide 5 — Real-Time Intelligence: Show the Eventstream canvas, the Eventhouse KQL queries, and the auto-refreshing Real-Time Dashboard. • Slide 6 — Security and Governance: Show workspace roles, RLS in action (same data, different views for different users), sensitivity labels, and Git commit history. • Slide 7 — Trade-offs and Decisions: Discuss at least three design decisions you made and why. For example: one Lakehouse per layer vs. one Lakehouse with schemas, PySpark vs. T-SQL for SCD2, aggregate tables vs. relying on DirectLake performance. Architecture Review Questions Answer these questions about your own platform. These are the questions a senior architect would ask: • What happens if the ERP source sends a column with a different name tomorrow? Which layer absorbs the change? • What happens if the master Pipeline fails at the Silver stage at 3 AM? How do you know, and how do you recover? • What happens if a new business unit is added and needs its own regional security filter? How many items do you need to change? • What happens if order volume grows 10x? Which components need scaling and which are already elastic? • If you could rebuild this platform with one change, what would it be? Completion Checklist Verify that your platform includes all of the following: Component Status Workspaces created with meaningful names ☐ Three Lakehouses (Bronze, Silver, Gold) ☐ One Warehouse for T-SQL analytics ☐ One Eventhouse + KQL Database for real-time data ☐ Batch ingestion Pipelines for three sources ☐ Eventstream for clickstream with fan-out to Eventhouse + Lakehouse ☐ Bronze Notebook with metadata columns and append-only writes ☐ Four Silver Notebooks with deduplication, type casting, MERGE ☐ Gold Notebooks: dim_date, dim_customers (SCD2), dim_products, fact_orders, agg_daily_revenue ☐ Master orchestration Pipeline: landing → Bronze → Silver → Quality → Gold ☐ Data quality gate between Silver and Gold ☐ Warehouse views/procedures for T-SQL analysts ☐ DirectLake semantic model with star schema relationships ☐ 10+ DAX measures including time intelligence and RANKX ☐ Row-Level Security in the semantic model ☐ Sales Dashboard (three pages, slicers, drill-through) ☐ Customer 360 report with drill-through ☐ Real-Time Dashboard on Eventhouse (auto-refresh) ☐ Sensitivity labels applied ☐ Git integration with at least three commits ☐ Deployment Pipeline (Dev → Test → Prod) ☐ Monitoring Notebook scheduled for daily execution ☐ Architecture diagram documenting the complete platform ☐ Course Complete You have built a complete data platform in Microsoft Fabric. Not a demo. Not a proof of concept. A production-ready system with ingestion, transformation, modeling, analytics, visualization, real-time intelligence, machine learning, security, governance, and DevOps. You understand not just how to use each Fabric workload, but why it exists and when to choose it. You understand the architectural principles — medallion layers, star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, quality gates — that keep a platform maintainable as it scales. You understand the operational discipline — Git, deployment pipelines, monitoring, capacity management — that keeps a platform reliable in production. The platform you built today is Version 1. Real platforms evolve. New sources arrive. New business questions emerge. New team members need onboarding. The skills you have learned in this course give you the foundation to adapt, extend, and optimize the platform for whatever comes next. Congratulations. From Platform Foundations to Production Deployment. 20 Modules · 200 Lessons · One Complete Platform.
